kb_links.title: Route Optimization For Dispatch: AI Availability Intake
kb_links.content:
You will receive a call right away.
If you're looking for a custom demo, let's connect.
Smarter Route Optimization Begins at the First Call
Route optimization fails when availability inputs are vague or unverified, not because dispatch cannot plan. Small misses like “morning” instead of a real window, unconfirmed on-site access, missing gate codes, guessed job duration, and tentative bookings make routes collapse mid-day and drive reschedules, no-shows, and repeat calls. AI phone calls help by capturing availability correctly the first time with a short intake flow: confirm the scheduling goal, convert preferences into a specific two-hour window, verify who will be on-site and access constraints, capture job details that affect duration, then close with a clear next step and update channel. “Availability collected correctly” means verified time window, access readiness, exact location details, job constraints for duration and skill match, and communication preference. Standardizing a few routing-critical questions improves route density and reduces dispatcher churn because dispatch routes on structured constraints, customers get aligned updates, and exceptions like “access not confirmed” are flagged early.
Route optimization rarely fails because dispatch teams cannot plan. It fails because the plan is built on shaky inputs. One wrong detail, a vague time window, an unconfirmed gate code, or a “call me tomorrow” that never becomes a real slot, and even the best routing logic collapses by noon.
This is where AI phone calls can help, especially during volume spikes or after-hours surges. The goal is not to call more. It is to capture availability correctly the first time, in a way that is consistent, fast, and easy for the customer. When availability is captured cleanly, route optimization improves automatically because dispatch is working with constraints it can trust.
In this guide, you will see how AI phone calls capture availability and job constraints without sounding robotic, which questions matter most before confirming a slot, and how dispatch turns that data into fewer reschedules, fewer no-shows, and tighter routes.
Why Availability Data Breaks Dispatch Routes
Most availability capture breaks in small, predictable ways. It looks fine on the calendar, but it is wrong in the real world.
Common failure points across home services:
The customer gives a “morning” preference instead of a real time window
No one confirms who will be on-site to grant access
Job duration is guessed, so the route gets overpacked
Technicians arrive without key access details (pets, parking, gate code, entry instructions)
The booking is confirmed internally, but the customer assumes it is a tentative hold
What this looks like in practice
Customer: Can you come tomorrow? Scheduler: Sure, we have a slot in the morning. Customer: I’m at work. Morning is not possible. Scheduler: Okay, how about afternoon? Customer: After 6 only, and you need the building code. Scheduler: Noted. We’ll try. Customer: So is it confirmed or not? Scheduler: We’ll confirm later.
Takeaway: Dispatch captured preferences, not a confirmed window. The fix is to confirm a specific time range and access details before locking the job.
When availability stays vague, route optimization becomes reactive. Dispatch keeps reshuffling the board, technicians run late, and customers call again because they do not know what to expect.
What “Availability Collected Correctly” Really Means
Availability is not just “what day works.” For route optimization, availability is a complete set of constraints dispatch can schedule against.
Collected correctly, availability includes:
Confirmed time window (example: 6 pm to 8 pm, not “evening”)
Access readiness (who will be present, gate code, parking, keys, pets)
Location readiness (exact address, unit number, landmark if needed)
Job readiness (symptom, equipment type, urgency, estimated duration)
Communication preference (SMS or call for updates and arrival notices)
This is what changes operationally: dispatch stops calling back for missing details, routes stop breaking mid-day, and technicians stop walking into surprises.
This blog is just the start.
Unlock the power of Convin’s AI with a live demo.
The AI Phone Call Intake Flow For Availability
AI phone calls work best when they follow a tight intake flow. Not a long script. A short, repeatable sequence that confirms what matters before booking.
Step 1: Confirm the scheduling goal
Start by clarifying what the caller wants right now: same-day help, a booked appointment, or a callback. This prevents forced bookings that later turn into reschedules.
Step 2: Capture a specific time window
Turn vague preferences into a bookable range. If the customer says “tomorrow,” confirm whether it is morning, afternoon, or evening, then lock a two-hour window.
Step 3: Confirm access and on-site availability
A route is only as good as access. Confirm who will be present and any constraints that could cause a no-show.
Step 4: Confirm job constraints that impact duration
Route optimization depends on realistic duration. Capture symptom, equipment type, urgency, and anything that changes prep time.
Step 5: Close with a clear next step
Close in one sentence: what is booked, the arrival window, and how updates will happen.
Dialogue example: capturing constraints without sounding robotic
AI: I can help schedule this. What time window works tomorrow? Customer: Evening. AI: To confirm, does 6 pm to 8 pm work for you? Customer: Yes. AI: Great. Will someone 18+ be home during that window to provide access? Customer: Yes, but you’ll need a gate code. AI: Noted. Please share the gate code now so the technician can enter smoothly. Customer: It’s 1947. AI: Thank you. Your visit is booked for 6 pm to 8 pm tomorrow. I’ll send confirmation by SMS and an arrival update before the technician heads out.
Takeaway: The AI did not “chat.” It confirmed the window, access, and update channel so dispatch can route with confidence.
The Questions AI Should Ask Before Confirming A Slot
The fastest way to improve route optimization is to standardize the questions that prevent bad bookings. If a question does not change routing, duration, or access, cut it.
Here is a compact checklist that works across home services:
“What is the earliest time you can do?”
Prevents false “tomorrow morning” holds
“Can you do a two-hour window?”
Scheduling flexibility
Improves route density
“Who will be on-site?”
Prevents no-entry no-shows
“Any gate code, pets, parking notes?”
Saves technician time on arrival
“What system is affected and what changed?”
Improves duration estimates and skill match
“Do you prefer SMS or calls for updates?”
Reduces repeat calls and confusion
A simple rule for AI intake: confirm the slot only after the time window and access are verified.
Turning Availability Into Better Dispatch Routing
Once availability is captured correctly, route optimization gets easier because the constraints are real. Dispatch can plan routes around verified time windows, realistic durations, and fewer unknowns.
A practical operational model looks like this:
Availability becomes structured constraints: time window, duration, location, access notes
Dispatch routes using those constraints, not free-text notes
Updates go out automatically so customers stay aligned with the plan
Exceptions get flagged early, like “access not confirmed” or “window too tight”
What improves when the loop is working
Fewer reschedules because windows are real and expectations are set
Fewer no-shows because access and on-site presence are confirmed
Fewer repeat visits because job context is captured upfront
Less dispatcher churn because routes break less often
If you are evaluating tools to support this workflow, look for solutions that can capture call intent, standardize availability questions, log outcomes into your scheduling or CRM system, and quality-check intake consistency across calls. You do not need a new system of record. You need better inputs feeding the one you already run.
How does availability accuracy improve route optimization outcomes? Accurate windows, access readiness, and duration estimates reduce mid-day reshuffles, so route optimization stays stable and customers get reliable arrival updates.
What availability details are essential for route optimization scheduling? Capture confirmed two-hour window, onsite adult, gate code, parking notes, exact address, system type, urgency, and preferred update channel for route optimization.
How do AI phone calls support route optimization during spikes? AI phone calls standardize intake after-hours, convert “tomorrow” into bookable windows, log constraints, and improve route optimization without extra dispatcher callbacks.
Which questions prevent route optimization failures from vague time windows? Ask earliest workable time, confirm two-hour range, verify who’s onsite, collect access instructions, and clarify job constraints before route optimization locks the slot.
How can dispatch use constraints to strengthen route optimization? Turn availability into structured fields: window, duration, location, access notes; route optimization uses these constraints, flags exceptions, and reduces reschedules and no-shows.

[30/01/26 11:34:08 AM] Vidhya Convin Product: *Product feature update* Human Hand Off Mechanism Till now, human agents had to manually go and change ownership of the lead from AI to Human and vice versa. We’ve added to the human in the loop section in create agent. There is now  a proper AI–human handoff system where lead ownership switches based on conditions and this is useful for customers where humans are operating on their own CRMs and not using our product for calling. AI handles conversations first and, based on defined conditions in the prompt, we can automatically hand the lead to a human by triggering a tool that updates ownership and sends the details to the customer CRM. After the human step is done, control can go back to AI either manually via them hitting our API or automatically based on logic set in that section. This creates a smooth two-way handoff so the right agent handles the lead at the right time. <This message was edited>

[06/02/26 8:39:48 PM] Vaibhav: @⁨Hassan Convin Sales⁩ / @⁨Kushaal Convin Sales⁩ / @⁨Ashish Convin⁩ : I went through 20-25 leads in detail. Here are my observations on boAt: 1. People are engaging after sharing reviews also. Checking about coupon code and when they will get free headphones. This should get solved with real time human transfer mostly. Agent will answer all those questions. While we are proactively not triggering calls in this case, but they message back to check on the coupons. And then we respond with whatsapp messages - this increases cost. 2. People who are not satisfied end up talking a lot - sharing their issues in detail. This does not help in reviews and increases cost - bad bad scenario for us. Possible solution - we can better handle the cases where people didn't like the product. We can just say let me raise this with my senior and they will get back. Should help save long conversations and cost. The goal definition can be improved for cases where customers are not satisfied. Currently, we are calling them again and again to understand their experience when they have already told that they are not satisfied. 4. We can have a cap on overall call duration. Where people want to post review, 5 mins conversation should be more than enough. We have 41 cases of 5 mins + conversation. Most of this conversation was useless. This feature is not there though yet - could be implemented in future. 5. We have many cases where customers agree to post a review in the very first call. But, because they don't confirm that they have, we keep following up. Maybe we can set the goal as just getting them to agree that thye will post a review, rest human agents can follow up. This should save a lot of cost. This I believe is the easiest ti implement and will have help us save cost significantly. but this will work only if human agents diligently follow up our hot/warm leads.

[07/02/26 1:38:34 AM] Sudesha Convin Onboarding: Bot-only conversion – observations: 1. Review posting flow & AHT impact: The bot currently guides customers to post a review during the call. In several cases, customers find it difficult to complete this action live, which results in extended conversations. While this flow is driving a strong bot-only conversion, it is also significantly increasing the average handling time (AHT). 2. Post-review coupon communication We are receiving a high volume of customer queries related to the post-review coupon. The bot currently confirms that the coupon will be shared, but customers continue to follow up. Solution: Direct system integration to trigger and deliver the coupon code automatically will significantly reduce repeat queries and conversational overhead. 3. Message and duration limits for review completion: Our data indicates that, on average, a review can be completed within: fewer than 10 WhatsApp messages, and approximately 3 minutes of talk time. Possible Solution: Introducing a cap on message count and call duration for the review flow will reduce operational cost without negatively impacting conversion. --- Top 20 longest-duration calls – key findings: 1. High AHT driven by non-interested / dissatisfied customers Out of the top 20 longest calls, 7 calls are from “not interested” customers, where the bot attempts to resolve customer issues. In most of these cases, customers remain dissatisfied even after the bot’s responses. Recommendation: For dissatisfied customers, the bot should redirect them to human support and terminate the bot interaction instead of continuing the pitch. This needs alignment with the boat team as they need this and we have to show with proof that this is just increasing cost. 2. Verbose bot responses increasing call duration: Even short customer inputs are currently answered with long and detailed bot responses. Long term fix: Redesign bot responses to be shorter (don't have to repeat big sentences, very conversational). This will both improve human-likeness and materially reduce call duration and cost. --- Spend distribution by lead interest: Hot leads: ₹2.2k Warm leads: ₹2.5k Cold leads: ₹672 Not-interested / dissatisfied leads: ₹3.4k A disproportionate share of spend is currently going toward customers who are already dissatisfied and unlikely to convert. Recommendation: Early exit and support redirection for not-interested / dissatisfied customers will materially reduce unnecessary spend.

[14/02/26 3:56:04 PM] Hassan Convin Sales: Summary given below of the notification : The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) has notified an amendment to the Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021 on 10 February 2026, which will come into effect from 20 February 2026. This amendment focuses on how AI-generated or synthetic content (such as altered images, videos, or audio) is created, shared and governed. The intent is to reduce misuse, misinformation and unlawful use of such content while improving transparency for users. Summary of the amendment mentioned below: Clear recognition and definition of AI-generated (synthetic) content. Expectations for intermediaries to put in place reasonable technical controls to identify and manage such content. Requirement for clear labelling or disclosure when content is synthetically generated. Tighter timelines for handling grievances and responding to lawful takedown requests. Clarification that compliant actions taken to remove or restrict content continue to be protected under the safe-harbour provisions of the IT Act. Objective of the Amendment: The primary intent is to combat misinformation and harmful synthetic media, enhance user transparency, and ensure faster compliance with lawful instructions to remove harmful content, especially in the context of rapidly evolving AI-generated media technologies. Additional accountability for significant social media platforms to verify user declarations and prevent intentional misuse of AI-generated content.

[20/03/26 1:13:16 PM] Vidhya Convin Product: Product Update: Variable Management We’ve introduced Variable Management to give you more control over how variables are configured and mapped. This builds on the existing variable mapping in Voicebot, with a more flexible and structured approach. 1. Creating Variables Navigate to Settings → Variable Management to create and manage variables. While creating a variable, you can define: •	Variable Name – A user-friendly label (e.g., First Name) •	Variable Key – Auto-generated identifier used internally •	Description – context about the variable •	Default Value – Fallback value if no data is available •	Mandatory Toggle – Ensure the variable must have a value for every lead •	Language Preference – •	Dynamic (follows conversation language) •	English •	Data Type – Specify the type of data the variable will hold 2. Using Variables in Knowledge Base Go to the KB section and insert variables by typing {} and selecting the required variable from the dropdown. 3. Campaign-Level Visibility In the Campaign section, the last tab (Variable Management) displays all variables added for that campaign. 4. Mapping Variables While: •	Uploading files (custom_variablekey) •	Setting up integrations You’ll now have the option to map variables directly to corresponding fields, ensuring structured and accurate data handling.

[25/03/26 3:17:20 PM] Vaibhav: Hi All PMs / CSMs, I’ve noticed that in some Sense campaigns, the opening line is: “Namaste XYZ, this is Neha calling from ABC. Is this a good time to talk?” Let’s avoid this format. Instead, please ensure we add context upfront, for example: “Namaste XYZ, this is Neha calling from ABC. I’m calling because you showed interest / inquired about PQR. Is this a good time to talk?” Adding this small piece of context in the very first line significantly improves engagement. Without it, many users tend to drop off right after the opening line. This becomes especially critical when running large campaigns (500–1000 leads), where even a small improvement like this can have a big impact. Let's not make these basic mistakes please.

[30/03/26 5:30:24 PM] Roopa Sree Convin Product: @⁨Ashish Convin⁩ *Update on Lead Interest Issue – Guvi & Bajaj Auto Credit* We identified that in both cases, many leads were incorrectly marked as Hot/Warm due to the inclusion of CRM metadata and lead summary inputs during evaluation. I have tested this on a sample set of leads, and the outputs now appear to be accurate. *Guvi* @⁨Sayani Convin PM⁩ The criteria for marking a lead as _hot_ is when the user has explicitly booked a counselling session. However, the lead summary contained extracted “proposed time,” which was passed into the evaluation. Due to the naming and structure of the summary, the LLM interpreted this as a confirmed booking, leading to incorrect classification. *Bajaj Auto Credit*@⁨Sudesha Convin Onboarding⁩ @⁨Suyasha Convin PM⁩ A lead should be marked as _hot_ only when the customer explicitly confirms both purchase intent and their pincode. However, since the customer’s pincode was already being passed via CRM metadata, the LLM assumed it was provided by the customer during the interaction, resulting in false positives. *Fix Implemented* - Removed CRM metadata and lead summary from LLM inputs for this metric. - Modified the user prompt with stricter guardrails to avoid assumptions @⁨Sayani Convin PM⁩ @⁨Suyasha Convin PM⁩  Please re-run the metric and confirm if the issue persists @⁨Ashish Convin⁩ Identified a few product improvement opportunities during this process - will connect separately to discuss.

[01/04/26 11:51:33 AM] Vaibhav: Hi PMs, I’ve mentioned this to a few of you earlier - sharing it here so we’re all aligned. In kickoff calls with client, let’s avoid asking the client to re-explain the use case from scratch. In most cases, they’ve already gone over it multiple times with Sales/solutioning, and repeating it can get the client irritated (we have seen this happening 2-3 times now). Instead, start by summarizing your understanding of the use case in a few lines (that you must have learnt in the internal kick-off with Sales), confirm if it’s accurate, and then move into your follow-up questions. This keeps the conversation crisp and shows better ownership (also shows that there was proper KT between sales and PM). Let’s follow this approach going forward.

kb_faqs.question: What were the connectivity and qualification rates for Miles Education's pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: Miles Education had a 35% connectivity rate and 10% qualification rate. The leads given were those who had shown interest more than a month ago through Meta ads. The client expected Convin Sense to qualify at least 10% of leads, matching their existing performance.

kb_faqs.question: What were the key funnel metrics for Cashify's pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: Out of 7,800 leads who complete the sell query, 30-35% complete the sell order (~2,200 successful orders). Margin per sell order is ₹450-550. Rohit internally calculated ₹60 incremental cost per sell query. Convin's campaign showed a 20% increment in meetings scheduled (13.8% to 16.4%), which was entirely attributed to Convin, and an overall 34% incremental improvement over their existing WhatsApp/SMS campaigns.

kb_faqs.question: What was Cashify's client sentiment and next steps after seeing pilot results?
kb_faqs.answer: Rohit (Cashify) was happy with current numbers and said if similar results could be shown across more campaigns, they would onboard Convin fully. After 2K leads run, he agreed to share 1K more leads manually, but stated he couldn't share leads beyond that without encrypted API integration. The team planned to present to Rohit and his bosses around Dec 16-17 on 5K leads results and pitch for an annual contract.

kb_faqs.question: What were the Animall UAT pilot metrics and lead structure?
kb_faqs.answer: Calling was done on 450 external users during UAT. 93 leads were marked as hot/warm and 31% were qualified by the bot. 99 leads had empty lead scores due to a tech issue being investigated. Out of ~3,000 daily leads, agents handle ~1,100 while 1,900 surplus leads are what the bot qualifies. Connectivity was ~74%, in line with the human benchmark of 70-80%.

kb_faqs.question: What was the business context and goal for Animall's pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: Animall's leads are abundant (not from human-touch funnel). Human conversion rate is 8-10% with average revenue per conversion of 6-7%. The client's goal is to increase conversion to 10% through the bot. Two agents were assigned to follow up on bot-qualified leads, with a TAT of 3-4 days for concluding follow-ups.

kb_faqs.question: What was the ABSLI pilot status and agreed structure?
kb_faqs.answer: ABSLI agreed to a fixed cost model and will use WhatsApp for campaigns going forward. The team targeted completing the pilot in 4 weeks (one month). Lead-to-appointment conversion was referenced at 20%, though a detailed breakdown call was still pending. Ubona integration was required and Rushil was requested as SPOC given prior ABCL bot experience.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Snabbit pilot follow-up issue and its scale?
kb_faqs.answer: Snabbit's pilot ran on 3,000 leads, but ~50% of qualified leads were not being called back by human telecallers even after multiple reminders. One person was dedicated to call not-connected leads, but almost 50% remained unconnected. The team escalated to senior stakeholder Vikas Choudhary (vikas.choudhary@snabbit.com) via LinkedIn to unblock the situation.

kb_faqs.question: What were the Miles Education pilot issues around data sharing and follow-up?
kb_faqs.answer: Miles Education's pilot ran for ~2 months with good numbers, but went on hold because the client was not doing follow-up calls or sharing data. Only 65% connectivity was achieved by human telecallers on qualified leads shared by Convin. The client also hadn't shared conversion numbers on qualified leads or benchmark conversion numbers (Superbot, Google, Meta separately). ~7K leads remained, with a plan to wrap up by end of the following week.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Wishlink pilot status and why was it put on hold?
kb_faqs.answer: Wishlink's content-creator leads were already contacted through IVR, manual calling, and in-app notifications before Convin could engage them. No remaining leads were available mid-month since all reward programs are assigned at the start of the month. The pilot was put on hold, and Convin decided not to spend further time until leads were available and payment was made. Wishlink had not yet paid the invoice as of early December.

kb_faqs.question: What was Wishlink's revised timeline for engagement?
kb_faqs.answer: Wishlink planned to finalize reward programs and milestones for January between Dec 27-29, with most team members on leave Dec 22-26. They wanted WABA integration and conversation quality testing on 100 creators completed before Dec 22. Payment was expected by the following day (Dec 10), and the team agreed to proceed with the limited testing scope within that window.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Miles Education bot hallucination/name issue and how was it resolved?
kb_faqs.answer: Some WhatsApp messages were saying 'Hi Meera' where 'Meera' was the agent name rather than picking up the customer_name field correctly. The issue was raised with Lovelesh and was expected to be resolved the same afternoon. This was a variable mapping bug in the WhatsApp message template.

kb_faqs.question: What was the dynamic opening bug that caused AriseAll campaign issues?
kb_faqs.answer: A tech issue caused problems only in campaigns using dynamic opening of the voicebot call; campaigns with static opening were unaffected. The issue appeared on a specific day's campaign for AriseAll (previous AriseAll campaigns were fine). The team paused all campaigns with dynamic opening until the fix was released. RCA pointed to dynamic opening being the trigger, though not every call with dynamic opening was affected.

kb_faqs.question: What was the non-stop/repeat calling bug and how was it resolved?
kb_faqs.answer: The bot was making back-to-back calls even after the conversation goal was achieved or when a user requested a callback the next day. Root cause was the LLM generating an NBA (Next Best Action) for a 'WhatsApp outbound call' channel that was not properly implemented. Lovelesh disabled the WhatsApp outbound call feature in production to fix this. The fix was released and all orgs were updated, though the issue recurred and required a second fix the same day.

kb_faqs.question: What was the '91trucks' pronunciation issue and its root cause?
kb_faqs.answer: The bot was mispronouncing '91trucks' because initially only one variation ('91Truck') was added to the word replacement list. The system needed multiple combinations (91trucks, 91truck, etc.) to be added. Word replacement was also found to be case-sensitive, requiring exact case matches. The fix was deployed within ~30 minutes of escalation.

kb_faqs.question: What was the DND + WhatsApp NBA routing issue observed in PolicyBazaar pilot testing?
kb_faqs.answer: When a lead's phone number was on DND, the LLM generated an NBA for 'WhatsApp AI Call' (outbound calling via WhatsApp), which was not a properly implemented feature. This meant the system neither called the lead nor sent a WhatsApp message. The fix was to disable the WhatsApp outbound call channel, after which the bot correctly scheduled the AI call for post-DND hours (10:45 AM). The system needed at least one call attempt to detect DND status before disabling the AI call channel.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Animall NBA wrong generation issue?
kb_faqs.answer: NBA (Next Best Action) was being wrongly generated for Animall's Sense pilot, which also caused non-stop calling. The issue was flagged to Lovelesh and Vidhya. It was linked to the same non-stop calling bug fixed across all orgs. The fix required a second round after initially breaking again.

kb_faqs.question: What happened when WhatsApp inbound was tested on the PolicyBazaar Sense demo?
kb_faqs.answer: When testing WhatsApp inbound for the PolicyBazaar demo, the bot responded with 'Hi can I help you' and then went silent. This was flagged to Ashish and Lovelesh for investigation immediately before the client demo scheduled the next morning.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Animall lead score empty issue during UAT?
kb_faqs.answer: 99 out of 450 leads called during Animall UAT had empty lead scores. The product team was investigating the issue, and final qualification data (which could go higher than 31%) would be shared post-resolution. This was a data tracking/scoring bug, not a calling or conversation issue.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Cartesia voice volume issue?
kb_faqs.answer: Voices in Cartesia were found to be very low and barely audible at some points during testing. Sudesha flagged this to Lovelesh requesting volume increase before campaigns could be started. This was a configuration/TTS provider issue affecting campaign readiness.

kb_faqs.question: What was the agent name inconsistency issue found before the PolicyBazaar demo?
kb_faqs.answer: Lovelesh noticed during a prod check that the agent config had inconsistent naming — the agent name was 'Riya' in some places and 'Aarav' in others. Hassan clarified that the name was changed to 'Riya' because the default voice was female. The team needed to standardize the config and re-upload fresh leads before the demo.

kb_faqs.question: What is the guidance on STT interruption word configuration?
kb_faqs.answer: Vaibhav issued a team-wide instruction: when setting up an agent, do not change the interruption words setting to 3 inside STT configuration — leave it at its default value. This was communicated as a universal rule for all agent setups.

kb_faqs.question: What system prompt changes were made for Miles and AriseAll and why?
kb_faqs.answer: For Miles and AriseAll, Lovelesh changed the system prompt to use template matching. For Cashify, the system prompt was changed to avoid repeating questions already answered. All other accounts used the default system prompt. Changes beyond the default required explicit justification and Lovelesh's involvement.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Handpickd WABA integration setup decision for the POC vs pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: For Handpickd's POC (100-200 customers), they would use Handpickd's own WABA account. For the full pilot post-POC, they would switch to Karix integration instead of Handpickd's portfolio. The WABA integration call required the person who manages their WhatsApp Business account to be present.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Mosaic integration workaround proposed when a requested feature wasn't available?
kb_faqs.answer: The workflow Mosaic's team was requesting was identified as a product feature not yet available. As a workaround, Convin proposed sending the recommended product ID, session ID, and unique visitor ID to Mosaic, and Mosaic would build their own workaround to add the specific product to the cart. The 'push' feature proposed by Raajith was in testing at Convin's end. Mosaic's SPOC (Himanshu) said he'd check feasibility on their end.

kb_faqs.question: What is the word replacement configuration learning from the 91trucks pronunciation fix?
kb_faqs.answer: Word replacement requires all possible variations of a term to be added (e.g., '91Truck', '91trucks', '91truck'). The system is case-sensitive, so each case variation must be explicitly added. Only adding one variation will result in mispronunciation for all other variations.

kb_faqs.question: What was the decision on using Truecaller whitelisting for pilots?
kb_faqs.answer: Ashish proposed getting caller numbers verified/whitelisted on Truecaller via Elision for pilots where it could add value (improving connectivity). Elision agreed to unlimited impressions on Truecaller for a small charge. The team discussed which pilot to pick first, with Boat being one candidate. Vaibhav was tasked with providing more context on the best use cases.

kb_faqs.question: What was the AriseAll resource assignment and kickoff timeline?
kb_faqs.answer: AriseAll was signed and Sudesha was assigned as the implementation SPOC. The kickoff was scheduled for Monday at 12:30 PM. Rushil was assigned to 91Trucks. Internal kickoff was mandated before any client-facing kickoff call.

kb_faqs.question: What was the ABSLI voice bot pilot resource assignment rationale?
kb_faqs.answer: Hassan requested Rushil as SPOC for the ABSLI voice bot pilot because Rushil had already done the Ubona integration for the ABCL bot, and ABSLI also required Ubona integration. The handover form required additional integration-specific details to be captured, leading to a template update for future pilots requiring integration during the pilot phase.

kb_faqs.question: What SOP was established for internal vs client kickoff sequencing?
kb_faqs.answer: Convin established that internal kickoff must happen before any client-facing kickoff call. MSA signing was also required before proceeding to client kickoff. This was enforced for Wishlink and reiterated as standard process. For the ABSLI pilot, DM (decision maker) presence on the first call was treated as optional for large enterprise accounts after sales sign-off.

kb_faqs.question: How did the team handle Cashify's concern about data set size for incremental improvement metrics?
kb_faqs.answer: Rohit acknowledged the 20% increment in meetings scheduled but attributed the visit-to-phone-sold conversion improvement to small data set variance rather than Convin. The team's response was to run more campaigns (target 5K total leads) and present a more robust dataset to Rohit and his bosses by Dec 16-17 to pitch for an annual contract. The team also noted internally that relative improvement figures should always be explicitly called out in presentations rather than left for client interpretation.

kb_faqs.question: How did the team handle Shaadi.com's feedback that the bot voice lacked enthusiasm and energy?
kb_faqs.answer: Shaadi.com's feedback was that while voice quality was human-like, it lacked enthusiasm and energy. Ashish's guidance was to be transparent with the client that this is the best available voice currently, and offer voice cloning of one of their own agents' voices when going live, allowing the client to choose their preferred agent voice.

kb_faqs.question: How did the team handle 91Trucks' quality concerns and payment hesitation?
kb_faqs.answer: 91Trucks raised quality issues identified during a detailed QC by Vaibhav and Ashutosh. Kushaal's position was that payment should not be blocked by first-iteration quality issues — the setup and first campaign were done, improvements would come in the next iteration. Shivam communicated this to the client, who agreed to speak with Vikas before deciding. Rushil committed to closing the conversation with the client.

kb_faqs.question: What was the ABSLI WhatsApp campaign disagreement and how was it resolved?
kb_faqs.answer: ABSLI's team (Karishma/Suveer) had not confirmed WhatsApp usage for the next campaign, and Dhanraj thought the pilot was on contextual calling (voice bot) only, with WhatsApp to come later. The Convin team's position was that campaigns should not run without WhatsApp integration. After a 4PM sync, ABSLI agreed to WhatsApp with a fixed cost model and committed to completing the pilot in one month.

kb_faqs.question: What feature request was raised for handling DND numbers via WhatsApp with context?
kb_faqs.answer: Kushaal requested a feature where if a number is on DND but the customer provides an alternate number during a WhatsApp chat, the system should call that alternate number with full conversation context carried over. This was flagged as a high-value intelligent orchestration feature and a voice note was sent to Ashish for follow-up.

kb_faqs.question: Was there any discussion about applying the prompt generator tool to Sense pilots?
kb_faqs.answer: Biswa asked whether Amit and Bharat's prompt generator tool was applicable for Sense pilots. Ashish responded that this would need to be discussed further, indicating it was not yet confirmed or implemented for Sense at that time.

kb_faqs.question: What was the pricing/billing guidance for Convin Sense vs Voice Bot proposals?
kb_faqs.answer: Convin's internal guidance was to send pricing slabs based on pulses/units rather than minutes, as the two metrics are not equivalent. This was specifically discussed for the CreditMantri proposal. Adding a minutes column for customer understanding was discussed but rejected to avoid confusion.

kb_faqs.question: What was the escalation strategy used at Snabbit when the SPOC wasn't responsive?
kb_faqs.answer: When Snabbit's SPOC Prithvi was unresponsive and not arranging follow-up callers for qualified leads, Kushaal directly messaged Vikas Choudhary (VP/senior stakeholder) on LinkedIn to apply top-down pressure. The team was advised not to reach out to Vikas directly without going through Prithvi's channel first, but the escalation was deemed necessary given the stalled pilot.

kb_faqs.question: What was the team's decision framework for pausing pilot engagement when leads aren't available?
kb_faqs.answer: When Wishlink had no leads available, Ashish's clear directive was to put the pilot on hold, not spend time on calls or setup, raise the invoice, and only proceed after payment. The principle was not to invest implementation resources when the client cannot provide leads or hasn't paid.

kb_faqs.question: What was the guidance on pilot duration and escalation for stalled pilots like Miles Education?
kb_faqs.answer: Ashish stated that a 2-month pilot should not happen and there must be a defined end date. When Miles Education repeatedly failed to share follow-up calling data, the team considered escalating to Rohini (CBO) and Varun (founders) to get their attention. Kushaal committed to calling Miles' team and defining a hard end date within one business day.

kb_faqs.question: What is the key learning about assigning dedicated agents for qualified leads from pilots?
kb_faqs.answer: Multiple pilots (Snabbit, Animall) revealed that clients need dedicated agents with a defined TAT to follow up on bot-qualified leads, or the pilot results are meaningless. For Animall, 2 agents were assigned with a 3-4 day follow-up TAT. For Snabbit, 50% of qualified leads went untouched even after repeated reminders. Convin now proactively asks whether specific agents are assigned before declaring the pilot setup complete.

kb_faqs.question: What benchmarking data should be collected from clients before starting a pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: Kushaal identified three critical pieces of pre-pilot benchmarking: (1) lead source clarity — abundant vs human-touch funnel, (2) existing channel metrics — connectivity, qualification, and conversion rates across human calling, other bots, WhatsApp, etc., and (3) the specific goal the client wants to achieve with Convin. Without these baselines, it's impossible to evaluate pilot success objectively.

kb_faqs.question: What was the learning about conversion tracking structure for lead qualification pilots?
kb_faqs.answer: For pilots where the bot only qualifies and passes leads to human agents, it's critical to know the human qualification rate from the same lead pool (not just final closures) so that bot performance can be benchmarked fairly. For Animall, agents handled 1,100 of 3,000 daily leads but didn't track their qualification rate, making it hard to set a comparable target for the bot on the remaining 1,900 leads.

kb_faqs.question: What was the learning about WhatsApp integration sequencing for pilots?
kb_faqs.answer: The team established that campaigns should not be run without WhatsApp integration, as the combined channel approach (voice + WhatsApp) is core to Convin Sense's value proposition. For Handpickd, a POC used their existing WABA account while the full pilot would use Karix integration. The person managing the client's WhatsApp Business account must be on the WABA integration call.

kb_faqs.question: What ROI framework data was collected from Cashify and how should it be presented?
kb_faqs.answer: Cashify's ROI framework used: leads entering funnel, sell order completion rate (30-35%), margin per order (₹450-550), and incremental conversion improvement. Ashish emphasized that relative improvement (e.g., 13.8% to 16.4% = 20% increment) must be explicitly written out in presentations and not left for the client to calculate. The team planned a formal deck for the Dec 16-17 pitch.

kb_faqs.question: What was Animall bot's connectivity performance vs human benchmark?
kb_faqs.answer: Animall's bot achieved ~74% connectivity, which was within the human benchmark range of 70-80%. The client's existing human calling and WhatsApp messaging achieved 70-80% connectivity, making the bot's performance comparable. The client's target was to increase conversion from 8-10% to 10% using the bot.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Cashify bot's specific performance contribution as attributed by the client?
kb_faqs.answer: The 20% increment in meetings scheduled (from 13.8% to 16.4%) was entirely attributed to Convin by Rohit. The overall incremental improvement was 34% vs existing WhatsApp/SMS campaigns. Rohit was willing to not complete all 20K leads and onboard Convin earlier if similar results were shown across a few more campaigns (~5K total leads).

kb_faqs.question: What voice quality feedback was received across demos and pilots?
kb_faqs.answer: Shaadi.com feedback was that voice quality was human-like but lacked enthusiasm and energy. General feedback noted that pronunciation of proper nouns was still dicey. The new voice was noted as significantly better in quality by the team (tested before a Shaadi.com demo). Cartesia voices were flagged as too low in volume for actual campaigns. Word replacement (case-sensitive, multiple variations needed) was the fix for proper noun pronunciation issues.

kb_faqs.question: What was the voicebot silence/no-reply bug on Dec 16 and how was it resolved?
kb_faqs.answer: The voicebot went silent and did not reply to users during WhatsApp calls. The root cause was an issue where calls were happening but recordings and transcripts were not being generated, causing the system to incorrectly show calls as 'not answered'. Lovelesh confirmed calls (both WhatsApp and normal) were restored within ~30 minutes, and recording/transcript issues were resolved shortly after.

kb_faqs.question: What was the context retention bug on WhatsApp calls and what was its impact?
kb_faqs.answer: The bot was not retaining context from prior WhatsApp chat conversations when transitioning to a WhatsApp voice call, causing it to re-ask questions already answered. This was flagged as especially critical because many customers who buy have DND-activated numbers, making WhatsApp calls the primary channel. The issue was resolved on Dec 16 after the recording/transcript fix was deployed.

kb_faqs.question: What is the correct way to configure the bot's identity in the knowledge base?
kb_faqs.answer: The bot's name and role must always be explicitly defined in the Knowledge Base (KB), not just in agent creation settings. The recommended format is: 'You are [Name], a customer support executive for [Company]. You are speaking with people who showed interest…'. Additionally, a line like 'Start by introducing yourself - I am [Name] from [Company]' should be added to handle edge cases where a non-template message is sent (e.g., within the 24-hour WhatsApp session window).

kb_faqs.question: What hallucination issue was found during the StarHealth demo?
kb_faqs.answer: During the StarHealth voice bot demo, the bot replied with the wrong name ('Suresh' instead of 'Siddharth'). Additional issues reported included hallucinations triggered by loudspeaker audio, robotic voice quality, poor interruption handling, and sensitivity to background noise. These were flagged as critical demo-impacting issues.

kb_faqs.question: What were the identified issues with Cartesia voices in WhatsApp notes?
kb_faqs.answer: Two main issues were found with Cartesia voices on WhatsApp: (1) Hindi words were being rendered in Roman script in WhatsApp voice notes, which distorted pronunciation, and (2) there was inconsistency in tempo/pacing, making the voice sound robotic. Additionally, a male voice was reported appearing unexpectedly. The recommended workaround was to use the 'Ronika' voice, and later 'Tara' (voice ID: 167b0bc7-3dbf-44c1-969b-e190347ada52) and 'Shrishti' were identified as the best voices.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Ronika voice training issue and how was it being resolved?
kb_faqs.answer: Ronika's voice re-training on Cartesia failed across two rounds because the raw training audio contained background noise, which impacted output quality. The team attempted a workaround of using ElevenLabs TTS output of Ronika (which removes background noise) to re-train a new Ronika voice on Cartesia. In the interim, 'Tara' (voice ID: 167b0bc7-3dbf-44c1-969b-e190347ada52) was recommended as the primary demo voice.

kb_faqs.question: What were the Cashify phase 1 and phase 2 pilot results?
kb_faqs.answer: In the first Cashify campaign, Convin achieved a 20% increment in visits scheduled compared to baseline, with a cost per visit of 186 INR (target break-even was 150 INR). In the second campaign, the incremental visits scheduled dropped to 13.8%, likely because Cashify increased baseline coupon offers independently, raising their organic visits. The team was preparing a full ROI deck with LMF costs included (INR 1.1/15s pulse, INR 0.5/WhatsApp message, INR 1/lead LMF).

kb_faqs.question: What was the AriseAll WhatsApp campaign performance issue?
kb_faqs.answer: A campaign run on 390 AriseAll leads saw only 27 people respond on WhatsApp. Investigation revealed that for over 33% of leads, the WhatsApp NBA (Next Best Action) was generated but never executed, leaving leads stuck. Additionally, 10% of leads had zero WhatsApp messages sent from Dec 19 onwards. The root cause was identified as a credit exhaustion event, and a Jira ticket was raised for priority resolution.

kb_faqs.question: What happened to the ABSLI and AriseAll WhatsApp numbers and how was it handled?
kb_faqs.answer: ABSLI's WhatsApp number quality rating dropped to low (likely due to users marking messages as spam), so the team removed WhatsApp and resumed campaigns with AI calling only. AriseAll's WABA account was restricted by Meta from sending messages for 30 days due to low quality rating triggered after a 390-lead campaign. The team requested a Meta account review and switched AriseAll to calling-only campaigns.

kb_faqs.question: What was the decision on using utility vs. marketing WhatsApp templates across pilots?
kb_faqs.answer: The team decided to migrate most pilots to marketing templates to reduce spam risk, as utility templates were consistently getting marked as spam. Large pilots (10k–20k leads) like ABSLI, AESL, Guvi, and 91Trucks were to be moved to marketing immediately. New small pilots (~1k leads) would stay on utility since the account suspension risk is low at that volume. Campaign-level template selection feature was pending release (expected the following week), so the transition was done selectively.

kb_faqs.question: What was the 91Trucks marketing template delivery rate issue?
kb_faqs.answer: When 91Trucks was switched to marketing templates, the first-message delivery rate was only 26%. The team noted there was no retry mechanism implemented for failed WhatsApp message deliveries, and this was added to the WhatsApp product story for development. The team planned to monitor delivery rates on subsequent messages.

kb_faqs.question: What was the call limit breach bug found in ABSLI campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: A critical bug was identified where the configured maximum calls per lead (set to 6 for ABSLI) was being breached—in 405 cases, the actual call attempts exceeded the limit, with some leads receiving up to 24 calls. Dashboard numbers and report numbers also did not match. A Jira ticket (AC-740) was raised and assigned to the product team for priority resolution.

kb_faqs.question: What was the 'back-to-back calling' and 'click-to-call not working' issue status as of late December?
kb_faqs.answer: Three critical issues were flagged: (1) back-to-back calling without proper intervals, (2) call limit breaches (6 configured, 24 triggered), and (3) Click-to-Call not working due to an Exotel API error. The first two were being handled by the product team (Raajit). The Click-to-Call issue was being investigated by Exotel, with no definitive timeline available as of Dec 29.

kb_faqs.question: What was ABSLI's brochure attachment feature request and its status?
kb_faqs.answer: ABSLI requested the ability to attach brochures (documents) to WhatsApp messages. This requires a separate WABA document message type that Convin Sense did not yet support. A Jira ticket was already in the product backlog, and the product team (Raajit) committed to sharing an ETA. The feature was not yet released as of late December.

kb_faqs.question: What was the LSQ CRM integration request for GUVI and why was it deferred?
kb_faqs.answer: GUVI requested LeadSquared (LSQ) integration during the pilot for better lead tracking. The team decided to push back on this for the pilot phase, with the rationale that it would consume significant configuration time without directly demonstrating the core bot value. Additionally, a systemic issue was flagged that clients (e.g., Medibuddy) were not accepting the standardized post-call integration framework that the VB product team had built. The decision was to revisit LSQ integration after a successful first month.

kb_faqs.question: What was Wishlink's voice cloning request and how was it handled?
kb_faqs.answer: Wishlink's client (Ajay, Leads Revenue & Growth) requested that a specific SPOC's voice be cloned and used as the bot voice. The team confirmed this is technically possible with approximately 1 hour of clean training audio from Cartesia. However, the decision was to not pick this up during the pilot due to timeline risk and the iterative improvement cycles it would require. Ashish explicitly said not to take this even post-pilot for Wishlink.

kb_faqs.question: What was the bot name hallucination issue found in the Bigul demo?
kb_faqs.answer: In the Bigul demo, the bot was introducing itself with the wrong name because the agent's name was not explicitly defined in the Knowledge Base. Vaibhav explained that without KB-level name assignment, the bot sometimes defaults to 'Tara' or the agent creation name inconsistently. The fix was to always add the bot's name and context in the KB, and also add an instruction to introduce itself in the first message.

kb_faqs.question: What is the recommended WhatsApp message template strategy for first outreach in hinglish markets?
kb_faqs.answer: The team approved a hinglish utility template with the line 'kya hum 2 mins baat kr sakte hain' (instead of English 'Can we speak for 2 mins') to ensure consistent hinglish conversation flow. For marketing templates, teams can use custom word choices. Until the campaign-level template selection feature was released, only the approved hinglish utility template was to be used as the default tick-marked template on the app domain.

kb_faqs.question: What was the duplicate CTA line bug in ABSLI WhatsApp messages?
kb_faqs.answer: The line 'Can we speak now to know more about it?' was appearing twice in ABSLI WhatsApp messages—once from the personalized message content and once from the approved utility template footer. The resolution was to move to a marketing template where the CTA line could be customized and the duplication eliminated. A new ABSLI marketing template was approved and a new WhatsApp number was created because the old number's health rating was still low.

kb_faqs.question: What is the bot's language behavior on WhatsApp chat vs. voice calls?
kb_faqs.answer: On voice calls, the bot can dynamically switch language based on the customer's language. On WhatsApp chat, the language defaults to Hinglish because it factors in all previous conversation history and the response language is hardcoded to Hinglish. PolicyBazaar flagged this as feedback (bot responding in Hindi even when customer replied in English on chat). The team's stance was that Indian customers respond better to Hinglish on chat, validated by higher response rates.

kb_faqs.question: What is the voicemail tracking and configuration feature released for Cashify cost optimization?
kb_faqs.answer: Lovelesh released a feature on Dec 24 that allows voicemail calls to be properly tracked on Sense, with the ability to configure the voicemail content sent to customers. This was identified as a cost optimization lever for Cashify since voicemail calls were previously being charged at the same rate as live calls. A plan was made to implement different/lower pricing for voicemail calls going forward.

kb_faqs.question: What were the Cashify cost optimization levers identified by the team?
kb_faqs.answer: The team identified the following cost optimization levers for Cashify: (1) ~30% cost reduction via pricing discounts, (2) real-time Cashify app integration to auto-stop outreach once a booking is confirmed—estimated 5-10% cost reduction (30-35% of bookings happen on app), (3) not charging for voicemail calls, (4) usage caps on calls and WhatsApp. Analysis showed 15-19% of leads respond only after the 3rd WhatsApp message, making WhatsApp message caps risky for conversion.

kb_faqs.question: What campaign slot configuration was used for Cashify's 5K active leads?
kb_faqs.answer: For Cashify's ~5K active leads, the team increased concurrent call slots to 50 with 1K leads processed per day in a single campaign. Vaibhav noted that when starting with WhatsApp (vs. AI call), the default 10 slots may be sufficient since calls are spread over time and not all leads necessarily need AI calls.

kb_faqs.question: What key pre-pilot setup process was identified as missing and how was it addressed?
kb_faqs.answer: The team identified that a formal internal handover form and kick-off process was not consistently being followed before client pilots. A handover form was formalized, and an SOP for Sense Pilots was created and shared (Google Doc). The process mandated: filling the handover form before any client kick-off call, having an internal sync between sales and onboarding before the client call, and ensuring all configurations are validated.

kb_faqs.question: What dialer and pricing transparency gaps were identified during pilot-to-contract transitions?
kb_faqs.answer: The team flagged that dialer migration (client dialer vs. Convin's Exotel), number procurement costs, whitelisting charges, and pricing post-pilot were not being documented in pilot contracts, creating billing ambiguity at go-live. The team's standard position was to integrate with the client's dialer post-pilot. Ashish directed that all default contracts must include these specifics, to be finalized immediately.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Miles Education pilot status and next steps as of late December?
kb_faqs.answer: Miles had ~2,200 leads processed with results compiled in a deck, plus a new leads campaign running. The team planned to present outcomes to founders Varun and Rohini, and aimed to complete the pilot with 5K total leads by the following Friday. A key data challenge was that Miles provided 2.5K prior leads only as call recordings (not text), and the team decided not to bear the transcription cost (~2K INR) during the pilot, deferring this to post-signing if monthly revenue justified it.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Wishlink pilot status and go-live timeline?
kb_faqs.answer: Wishlink received very positive initial feedback from Ajay (Leads Revenue & Growth), who was highly impressed with Convin Sense. The team planned to test bot conversation quality on 100 real creators before the official go-live on January 1st. The pilot was being managed by Sudesha and Varsha on implementation.

kb_faqs.question: What was the PW (Physics Wallah) context retention and goal achievement message bug?
kb_faqs.answer: Two Jira tickets were raised for the PW Sense pilot: AC-748 (context not retained in calls, causing repeated messages to be sent) and AC-747 (business outcome/goal achievement message being incorrectly sent to the student/customer). These were flagged by Utsav (CSM) and escalated to Raajit (Product) for review.

kb_faqs.question: What was the TTS voice configuration issue flagged for the Wiom domain?
kb_faqs.answer: Multiple TTS configuration issues were flagged for the Wiom domain: word replacement settings were disappearing after saving, the 'ElevenLabs' name was still visible in TTS settings (branding issue), and the Voice ID had to be manually entered at multiple points with inconsistency between Settings-TTS and Agent Settings. A use case was also identified requiring different voices for WhatsApp vs. AI phone call channels. The word replacement bug was confirmed fixed and released on Dec 30.

kb_faqs.question: What was the unexpected laughter hallucination bug found in the Handpickd Train campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: In the Handpickd Train campaign (lead: Himanshu), the bot was observed laughing unexpectedly twice during a 5-minute call. This was identified as a voice/TTS-related issue specific to the Ronika voice. The recommended fix was to switch to Tara voice (ID: 167b0bc7-3dbf-44c1-969b-e190347ada52) for demos until Ronika's re-training was complete.

kb_faqs.question: What voice quality issues were found with Geeta v2 voice?
kb_faqs.answer: Geeta v2 was found to have inconsistent pacing—speaking too fast at the beginning of calls and in patches throughout. This was flagged as a tempo inconsistency problem with the Cartesia voice model. Biswa confirmed switching to Tara as the primary voice while Cartesia worked on resolving the pacing issue.

kb_faqs.question: What are the recommended voices for demos and pilots as of late December 2025?
kb_faqs.answer: Based on testing, the team identified four top-performing human-like voices: Shrishti (0247c210-2375-46a3-bdc6-0242e9455803), Rani (dc7ad08e-b934-4493-a0f6-c1ddb509468d), Ronika_v2 (3f835cce-d86a-4341-bcda-c61f6db193ee), and Gauri (817a4f77-283f-4104-8539-8147d6a84a47). Shrishti received the best feedback including strong positive response from the Shaadi.com demo. The team recommended using Shrishti as the default demo voice.

kb_faqs.question: What was the campaign DND hours bug causing NBA delays?
kb_faqs.answer: NBAs (Next Best Actions) were being scheduled for later times than expected because campaign DND (Do Not Disturb) hours were being applied to calls even when the lead's personal number was not on DND. Lovelesh confirmed that DND hours in campaign settings apply only to calling, not WhatsApp messages, but the scheduling logic was incorrectly delaying NBAs for some leads. This was identified in the context of the Animall campaign setup.

kb_faqs.question: What was the rule-based tool call limitation discovered for widget vs. AI calls?
kb_faqs.answer: Mihir (tech) flagged that rule-based tool calls (such as tool calls triggered at the start and end of a call) are only supported for AI calls and not for the widget channel. Enabling this for widgets would require additional implementation effort. This was escalated to Raajit (Product) and Ashish for a decision on how to proceed, specifically impacting the Mosaic Wellness use case.

kb_faqs.question: What was the bulk system message feature and how is it accessed?
kb_faqs.answer: A feature exists to pass bulk system messages on-the-fly while a campaign is running. This is accessible via the internal dashboard under 'Tools'. Biswa flagged this as a useful capability for real-time campaign management.

kb_faqs.question: What was the key learning about callback speed from the 91Trucks pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: For 91Trucks (going live with 400+ leads in late December), Rushil emphasized that interested/qualified leads must be called back on the same day or next day maximum to achieve good results. This was communicated to the client (Aakash), but the team flagged that Aakash had previously delayed callbacks despite being told, impacting outcomes. Both Rushil and Vaibhav pushed for Shivam to reinforce this directly with the client.

kb_faqs.question: What was the TTS voice speed configuration change made for ABSLI?
kb_faqs.answer: Ashish directed the team to change the TTS voice speed setting to 0.9 or 0.95 for the ABSLI campaign to improve voice naturalness. This was communicated to Sudesha (Onboarding) to implement and shared as an FYI to Hassan (Sales).

kb_faqs.question: What was the Adani Realty campaign NBA issue where the bot incorrectly stated the customer denied a call?
kb_faqs.answer: In the Adani Realty campaign, the NBA incorrectly reported 'customer denied a call' and sent a message saying it 'can't call,' when the actual reason was the customer's number being on DND. Vaibhav confirmed the system prompt included logic to explain DND reasons to the customer. Lovelesh was assigned to investigate the mismatch between the NBA message and the actual DND state.

kb_faqs.question: What was the thinking UI and decision context feature released in mid-December?
kb_faqs.answer: Lovelesh announced the release of a 'Thinking UI' and 'Decision Context' feature on the UI dashboard, which shows the rationale behind the bot's decisions in real-time. This was flagged as helpful for debugging and understanding bot behavior during audits. Bharat noted that the decision context output should default to English since the product's standard language is English.

kb_faqs.question: What was the team's approach to ABSLI's request to add a new dormant-user activation use case during the pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: ABSLI requested adding a second use case (activating old dormant users via calling) in parallel with the existing matured policy payout campaign, with an initial ask for a static voice blast. The team declined voice blast (not a supported product feature) but confirmed calling via Sense was possible. The team's stance was to not expand scope during the pilot—Hassan was directed to discuss with the client and any new use case engagement needed to be scoped carefully to avoid delaying the core pilot results.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Kotak Securities (KSEC) pilot contract status and technical discussion need?
kb_faqs.answer: The KSEC pilot contract had already been couriered for physical signature (expected within 2 days) as of Dec 18. The client urgently needed a technical discussion as part of their sprint planning cycle. The PM team (Rakshitha) was engaged to plan the technical kick-off, and the handover form was confirmed as already filled.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Miles Education leads delay issue and how was it impacting the pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: Miles Education had committed to sharing 5K leads for the campaign on Monday (Dec 17) but had not shared them, directly delaying campaign triggers. The team escalated this through the sales team (Shivam/Kushaal) to push the client. The pilot target was to complete the 5K lead run by the following Friday (Dec 27), with a key milestone being a presentation to founders Varun and Rohini on existing results.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Cashify WhatsApp response distribution finding and its implication for cost optimization?
kb_faqs.answer: Analysis of two Cashify campaigns showed that 15-19% of customers respond only after the 3rd WhatsApp message. This means limiting WhatsApp messages as a cost-cutting measure would result in losing approximately 15-19% of conversions. Therefore, the team concluded that reducing WhatsApp message limits was not a viable cost optimization lever for Cashify.

kb_faqs.question: What was the GUVI pilot use case and what was the key goal metric being tracked?
kb_faqs.answer: The GUVI Sense pilot focused on improving ADPS (Average Demos per Salesperson) by nurturing leads, sending timely reminders, and driving higher demo completion rates from appointment booking. The team needed to define 2-3 specific goals around this. Sudesha was assigned to manage the GUVI pilot, and the first version knowledge base was being built from call recordings provided by the client (call script was still pending).

kb_faqs.question: What were the key connectivity and engagement issues observed in the ABSLI pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: In a campaign of 2789 leads over 2 days, 22% (615 leads) had 0 calls attempted, and 30% (840 leads) had only one call attempted with no follow-up, resulting in only 28% connectivity. Together, 52% of leads were not meaningfully engaged. ABSLI was also delayed in sharing region-wise conversion benchmarks (passed to RM %) for over a week, blocking ROI analysis.

kb_faqs.question: What was the ABSLI pilot's current conversion rate and what was the expected benchmark?
kb_faqs.answer: The ABSLI pilot achieved 1.5% conversion, while the internal expectation was at least 2%. Sales acknowledged they were 'still not at 1%' before getting the updated number, and the client was yet to share their region-wise benchmarks for comparison.

kb_faqs.question: What were the results of the Bigul 100-lead UAT campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: From 100 leads (which were very old, from August 2025), 34 conversations were connected, and 6 hot leads were generated where customers answered all profiling questions — roughly 17.6% qualification rate among connected leads. The overall lead quality was flagged as poor due to lead age, and the client (Shivani) was hesitant to move to the next phase despite good bot conversation quality.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Avataar Skincare pilot status after running 270 leads?
kb_faqs.answer: After running ~270 leads, 100+ were marked as warm leads but the actual genuinely warm leads were significantly lower upon audit by the Data Labs team. Very short interactions of 2–5 seconds were being incorrectly classified as Warm, and the human agent had already started outbound calling via the Convin Sense platform on the hot leads. Around 670 leads remained to be run.

kb_faqs.question: What were the drop-off reasons in Avataar's 30–60 second call analysis?
kb_faqs.answer: An audit of 20 interactions showed: 3 calls not connected/unreachable, 3 calls ended after pitch, 5 callback requests (customer busy/out of town/said 'not now'), 4 cases of no requirement or just browsing, 3 call quality/audio issues (background noise, low voice, dead air), 1 customer health issue, and 1 bot silence issue after voicemail. This analysis was shared with Sales and Implementation teams.

kb_faqs.question: What was the connectivity problem observed in AESL campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: In AESL Campaign C3, 53% of leads had only one call attempted with no subsequent contact, also with no WhatsApp outreach due to the marketing template health issue. Across campaigns, 30–40% of leads had no WhatsApp response and only 1 call in 4 days, massively impacting overall connectivity metrics.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Snabbit pilot outcome?
kb_faqs.answer: Snabbit's campaigns were completed over a week before the discussion, but their agents' follow-up calling was still ongoing with connectivity below 50%. The team decided to formally close this pilot as the numbers were poor and the client was not taking it seriously enough.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Ezobooks pilot process and update?
kb_faqs.answer: After the Ezobooks UAT campaign ended, around 22 hot leads were generated and the client's agents called only the hot leads. Warm leads had not been called yet despite the agreed 60% hot / 40% warm effort split. The team flagged that agents were also not updating the shared tracking sheet regularly.

kb_faqs.question: What was the back-to-back calling bug and which clients were affected?
kb_faqs.answer: A recurring bug (Jira AC-760, AC-773, AC-803) caused the bot to make back-to-back calls in violation of daily call attempt limits. This was observed in the 'app domain' and also on the BACL client. The issue was reported multiple times and ETAs were tracked on Jira.

kb_faqs.question: What hallucination issues were observed and reported in early January 2026?
kb_faqs.answer: In a Handpicked client UAT (Jira AC-772), hallucination occurred in DND scenarios — after correctly identifying DND, the bot restarted the conversation on January 1st at 11:09 AM and re-pitched the same offer. Additionally, the system was unable to accurately capture callback date and time. For Wiom's 1st UAT (Jira AC-771), hallucination caused the intro to be repeated at two points within a 5-minute conversation.

kb_faqs.question: What caused the bot to read names letter by letter (e.g., P-R-A-S-H-A-N-T) in Bigul?
kb_faqs.answer: When customer names were passed in ALL CAPITAL LETTERS to the Cartesia TTS engine, it spelled them out character by character. The RCA was confirmed and the immediate workaround was to pass all customer names in sentence case or lowercase. A platform-level fix was tracked (Jira AC-857).

kb_faqs.question: What caused all voice speeds to suddenly increase and accents to shift on January 8, 2026?
kb_faqs.answer: The model language setting was changed from Hindi to English to fix pronunciation issues (e.g., 'Are' being pronounced as 'अरे'). While this resolved pronunciation, it caused the speech rate (tempo) to increase and voice accents to shift on selected voices. The fix was reverted within approximately 30 minutes after discovery.

kb_faqs.question: What was the RAG Knowledge Search tool misuse issue in Bigul's UAT?
kb_faqs.answer: When the Knowledge Search tool was added to the Bigul agent, the bot skipped profiling steps, asked multiple questions simultaneously, and went off-script into app support topics not in the prompt (Jira AC-807). The root cause was misusing the tool as a call flow proxy instead of its intended purpose of answering knowledge questions. Removing the tool resolved all three issues.

kb_faqs.question: What was the WhatsApp WABA health issue and what caused it?
kb_faqs.answer: Using marketing templates for AESL campaigns caused the WhatsApp Business Account (WABA) health to drop to 'Red' (low). This also meant that subsequent WhatsApp outreach to leads in those campaigns could not be triggered, compounding the connectivity problem. New pilots were directed to use Karix instead.

kb_faqs.question: What was the warm lead misclassification issue in Avataar and how was it proposed to be fixed?
kb_faqs.answer: Very short interactions of 2–5 seconds were being incorrectly classified as 'Warm' leads, even after rewriting the prompt and switching the model. The proposed solution was to upgrade to Gemini 2.5 Flash or Gemini 2.5 Pro, and a Talktime feature was recommended to be implemented on Sense to filter out sub-threshold interaction lengths.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Miles Education 'bot triggered call but did not speak' issue?
kb_faqs.answer: In the Miles Education lead qualifier campaign, a bot triggered calls for lead 'Sandeep' but did not speak — both on the AI call and WhatsApp. Investigation revealed that the WhatsApp call wasn't enabled for that agent, and the AI call failed because an ElevenLabs voice ID was configured instead of a Cartesia voice ID (a legacy config from before Cartesia migration).

kb_faqs.question: What was the number spelled as 'gyarah' issue in Bigul?
kb_faqs.answer: The bot was spelling out the number 11 as 'g-y-a-r-a-h' (character by character) instead of pronouncing it correctly. This was tracked as Jira AC-851 and reported to the product/tech team for resolution.

kb_faqs.question: What WhatsApp DND hours violation was found in the Bigul campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: A WhatsApp message was sent to a lead at 10:33 PM, violating DND hours (Jira AC-911). This was flagged as a critical issue since it also affected the Boat client's requirements, where DND compliance was explicitly non-negotiable. The bug was escalated before the next campaign trigger.

kb_faqs.question: What was the repeated/back-to-back calling issue in the Wishlink campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: One lead in the Wishlink campaign received repeated calls due to LLM hallucination combined with a multi-goal agent configuration issue. The lead complained to Wishlink, negatively impacting client sentiment. The team identified that multi-goal agents need separate goal/agent configurations to prevent this, and the issue was tracked (Jira AC-915).

kb_faqs.question: What was the call recording unavailability bug reported for AESL?
kb_faqs.answer: Calls were being triggered and showing status as 'AI call connected,' but no recordings were available post-call. In one case, the call was confirmed picked up but still had no recording. This was raised as Jira AC-819 and escalated as a priority issue affecting pilot quality validation.

kb_faqs.question: What was the NBA (Next Best Action) not being generated/executed issue?
kb_faqs.answer: In Bigul and Ezobooks, some leads had NBAs not generated or not executed, leaving conversations paused mid-way. This was tracked as Jira AC-913 and separately flagged by implementation — teams were asked to use internal tools to manually add interactions for affected leads as a workaround.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Ezobooks campaign scheduling bug on Sunday?
kb_faqs.answer: The campaign did not pause on Sunday as configured, causing leads to be contacted and hot/warm counts to increase incorrectly. This was raised as Jira AC-867 and reported to the tech team for investigation.

kb_faqs.question: What is the recommended TTS configuration for building agents in Convin Sense?
kb_faqs.answer: Teams were instructed to always choose 'Convin TTS (Managed)' when configuring agents, as this uses Cartesia exclusively. The option to directly select 'Cartesia' from the frontend was being removed to enforce this standard.

kb_faqs.question: How should voice IDs be configured to ensure consistency across AI calls, WhatsApp calls, and WhatsApp audio notes?
kb_faqs.answer: When configuring an agent, the same voice ID must be selected for AI Calls, WhatsApp Calls, and WhatsApp (audio notes) to ensure voice consistency. Previously, different voice IDs had been accidentally applied across channels, causing noticeable quality differences in demos and client interactions.

kb_faqs.question: What are the available Cartesia voice options and their IDs as of January 2026?
kb_faqs.answer: Available voices include: Anika_122425 (0f78c625-8159-4f42-abfd-5a81296e1758), Gauri_122425 (8de1db6b-00b1-4656-b3d7-e556cbde9e76), Ronika_122325/v3 (1823bb24-d160-454d-a7ba-43aae1ca0a43), Tara_122225 (8dab585d-6056-4ad2-8400-641f218548b4), Payal_123025 (2eae9fb8-7ea8-4ea2-87ce-e56e3280a401), Rani v3 (dc7ad08e-b934-4493-a0f6-c1ddb509468d), and Shrishti V3 (0247c210-2375-46a3-bdc6-0242e9455803). A full reference sheet was shared internally.

kb_faqs.question: What is the issue with using Ronika voice for fully English conversations?
kb_faqs.answer: Ronika was flagged as not being the best voice for complete English conversations. Ronika v2 received the best feedback overall, while Ronika v3 had limited testing. The older Ronika versions were noted to have potential gibberish issues. For English-heavy use cases, Anika, Gauri, Tara, or Payal were suggested as alternatives.

kb_faqs.question: How should lead data (especially names) be passed to avoid TTS pronunciation errors?
kb_faqs.answer: All customer names and relevant text fields must be passed in sentence case or lowercase, not ALL CAPS, as the Cartesia TTS engine spells out capitalized words character by character. This is a known Cartesia behavior and the platform-level fix was pending, making data formatting the immediate required workaround.

kb_faqs.question: What pilot structure was standardized for all Convin Sense pilots?
kb_faqs.answer: All pilots must run 1000 leads in 3 batches: 100 leads for UAT, then 2 campaigns of 450 leads each. No CRM or lead flow integration is done during the pilot. Every pilot must have at least one agent from the client's team on Convin's Click-to-Call. Pilot goals, current benchmarks, and conversion metrics must be defined by Sales before kickoff and documented in the Sales Handover Form and MSA.

kb_faqs.question: What is the correct way to raise product issues in Jira to ensure they are prioritized?
kb_faqs.answer: All issues must be raised as 'Bugs' (not Tasks) and must be set to 'In Progress' status immediately upon creation. Issues raised as Tasks are not picked up on priority. Bugs without 'In Progress' status risk being missed in sprint reviews.

kb_faqs.question: What are the two ways Convin team members can access a client's Sense dashboard?
kb_faqs.answer: Access can be done via (1) Virtual User — limited access without being onboarded as a user on the customer dashboard, or (2) Registered User — full access where the person is onboarded as a 'Convin Support User' on the client tenant. SSO login was released on Sense, and access setup was coordinated through Gavin.

kb_faqs.question: What was the decision on WABA provider for new pilots after the AESL marketing template issue?
kb_faqs.answer: After the AESL campaign caused WABA health to drop to Red due to marketing templates, it was decided that all new pilots should start with Karix as the WABA provider. Flow testing with Karix was expected to be completed by the following Tuesday.

kb_faqs.question: What standard pilot analysis structure was defined for all campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: A standardized analysis spreadsheet was finalized to track: WhatsApp connect rate, number of call attempts, engagement metrics, and campaign-level outcomes. This structure was to be applied to all campaigns going forward, ensuring consistent reporting for sales and client reviews.

kb_faqs.question: How did the Bhanzu client respond to the request for baseline conversion data?
kb_faqs.answer: Bhanzu raised objections to sharing their current human-led calling setup costs and conversion data needed to define pilot success metrics. Even after the team explained the necessity, the business team was not aligned. The sales team was asked to intervene and unblock this, as these baselines were essential for evaluating pilot impact.

kb_faqs.question: How did the team handle Bhanzu's request for CRM integration (auto demo scheduling) during the pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: Bhanzu requested integration for auto demo scheduling during the pilot. The CSM clearly said no, explaining that the pilot is for concept testing only and integration would require significant effort from both sides. Despite this, Bhanzu continued to push internally. Sales was asked to communicate this boundary firmly and block any further integration requests during the pilot phase.

kb_faqs.question: What was the Kalpataru client's expectation and how did the team respond?
