This human-in-the-loop model requires agents to log conversion outcomes consistently in a shared sheet so ROI can be calculated in parallel during the campaign.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What happens when NBA (next best action) is not generated or executed for some leads?
kb_faqs.answer: In some campaigns, NBA execution logic failed for a subset of leads, causing conversations to pause midway rather than triggering the next step (e.g., callback scheduling, bot escalation). Root causes include stale campaign configurations, incorrect agent setups, or system glitches. When detected, affected leads must be manually re-triggered using internal tools, and campaigns must be re-run. This is a critical blocker for campaign progression.

kb_faqs.question: What are common WhatsApp message delivery failures and how do you diagnose them?
kb_faqs.answer: Common WhatsApp failures include: (1) 'Recipient may have blocked the business or restricted their account'—channel is automatically deactivated, (2) 'Not authorized to send message to this user'—indicates template or account issues, (3) Payment failures blocking delivery—must be cleared before retry. Diagnosis: Go to Activity > WhatsApp Analytics > scroll for error messages and affected message counts. Check card status in WABA account. Work with WABA SPOC to clear pending payments and retest.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What makes a 'good' vs 'bad' lead CSV for campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Good lead CSVs: (1) freshly sourced prospects (within 30–60 days), (2) no duplicates (deduplicated by phone number), (3) names in mixed case (not all CAPS), (4) valid phone numbers with country codes, (5) 500+ leads per batch minimum for statistical confidence. Bad lead CSVs: (1) stale leads (>3 months old), (2) high duplicate rates, (3) names in all caps (causes TTS mispronunciation), (4) unverified phone numbers, (5) batch sizes <100. Lead quality directly impacts conversation connection rates and qualification rates.

kb_faqs.question: What is the expected turnaround time for human agents to call back qualified leads?
kb_faqs.answer: Qualified leads (hot and warm) should receive callback attempts within 24 hours of the bot call. For optimal conversion, callbacks should happen within 12–24 hours. If the callback window extends beyond 48 hours, lead warmth drops significantly. Clients must commit one dedicated person to manual outbound calling from Convin's system during the pilot to maintain this SLA. Delays in agent onboarding or non-commitment to callback timing will directly impact pilot ROI.

kb_faqs.question: How should callback timing be handled when a prospect asks to be called back in X minutes?
kb_faqs.answer: The system was initially calling prospects back immediately for short gaps (5–30 minutes) instead of respecting the requested delay. A system prompt fix was implemented to honor specific timeframes for callbacks. For hour-level gaps, the system works accurately (e.g., 'call me in 2 hours'), but for minute-level callbacks (e.g., 'call me in 5 minutes'), the system may still call immediately. Clients requiring 95% callback timing accuracy should be informed of this limitation upfront.

kb_faqs.question: What is needed to support click-to-call for hot and warm leads on the Convin platform?
kb_faqs.answer: Click-to-call functionality requires: (1) the client to provide one human agent with dial access, (2) the agent to be onboarded on Convin's system with proper calling permissions, (3) a deduplicated list of hot/warm leads to be available in the dashboard, (4) the agent to have real-time access to lead details (name, phone, call notes from bot). This should be implemented before UAT is completed. Without click-to-call, leads must be shared via CSV, slowing down the callback process and reducing conversion rates.

kb_faqs.question: What notifications or reminders are needed for human agents tracking hundreds of qualified leads?
kb_faqs.answer: For pilots with 100–200 qualified leads, manual Excel tracking works, but beyond 1000 leads, manual tracking is infeasible and leads are lost. A system-level feature is needed to send notifications/reminders to agents for: (1) leads waiting for callback, (2) leads where the prospect requested a callback at a later time, (3) leads on which agents have started attempts but not completed. This would prevent qualified leads from falling through the cracks when scaling pilots.

kb_faqs.question: Is there a dropdown or easy way to select bot voices during campaign setup?
kb_faqs.answer: Currently, voice selection requires manually entering voice IDs in the Agent > Voice Call section from a reference sheet (e.g., Rani v3 ID, Shrishti v3 ID). There is no dropdown menu in the campaign builder. A feature request has been raised to add voice selection dropdowns to the UI so users can easily compare and select voices without copy-pasting IDs. Common voice options include Rani v3, Shrishti v3, and Anika.

kb_faqs.question: What causes some leads to be stuck in a conversation state with no next step executing?
kb_faqs.answer: Leads can get stuck when the NBA (next best action) flow is not properly configured or when a conversation completes but no exit/escalation step is triggered. The system displays the conversation but does not progress to the next stage (callback scheduling, CRM sync, etc.). Resolution involves checking the campaign configuration, verifying agent setup, and using internal tools to manually trigger the next step or re-run the conversation flow.

kb_faqs.question: Why are leads incorrectly classified as warm/hot when conversation duration is very short (2–5 seconds)?
kb_faqs.answer: Short conversations (2–5 seconds) are being incorrectly classified as warm leads despite prospects hanging up immediately. Root causes include: (1) overly lenient LLM prompts that consider any response as engagement, (2) model limitations (even Gemini 2.5 Flash struggles with short conversations). Solutions being tested: (1) upgrade to Gemini 2.5 Pro for better classification, (2) implement a 'talk time' feature that gates qualification to calls with minimum duration (e.g., 15+ seconds), (3) rewrite prompts with explicit call duration thresholds. This is a critical issue affecting ROI calculations.

kb_faqs.question: How should system prompts be adjusted for different client personas and call scenarios?
kb_faqs.answer: System prompt tuning is iterative: (1) start with a standard qualification template, (2) conduct UAT with 100 sample leads and listen to calls, (3) gather feedback on conversation flow, pausing points, and question clarity, (4) identify specific phrases or questions that cause hangups or confusion, (5) rewrite those sections with clearer language or different phrasing. Clients may request changes like reduced speed of speech, different opening statements, or altered question order. Each adjustment should be tested on a small batch before rolling to the full campaign.

kb_faqs.question: What does a typical call outcome distribution look like in a pilot campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: In a 100-lead UAT: approximately 34–40% of calls connect (depending on phone list quality and DND hours), 60% of connected calls result in hot or warm leads (where the bot engages the prospect), 6–20% of total calls produce hot leads (all questions answered), 10–30% produce warm leads (partial engagement). On a 450-lead production batch, expect 150–180 connected calls and 30–50 hot leads. These rates vary significantly based on lead freshness, time of day, and bot quality.

kb_faqs.question: What percentage of calls typically connect in a cold outbound campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: Call connection rates typically range from 30–50% depending on lead quality, time of calling, and DND compliance. Fresh leads (30–60 days old) achieve 40–50% connection rates. Stale leads (>3 months old) achieve 20–35% connection rates. Connection rate is directly impacted by: (1) lead freshness, (2) correct DND hour configuration, (3) call retry logic and timing between attempts, (4) phone number accuracy in the CSV.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: How should DND (do-not-disturb) hours be configured and what happens when they're not respected?
kb_faqs.answer: DND hours must be set to respect local time zones and client-specific quiet hours (typically 9 PM to 9 AM for India). When DND is not properly configured or tested, calls are placed outside permitted windows—resulting in client complaints (e.g., receiving calls at 1 AM). If a contact receives a call during DND hours, that number should be flagged internally to avoid future violations. Testing DND configuration before UAT is critical; any deviation should be flagged on day 1 of the campaign.

kb_faqs.question: What ROI metrics should be tracked during and after a pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: Core metrics: (1) Cost Per Acquisition (CPA) = (Total Campaign Cost / Qualified Leads), (2) Conversion Rate = (Leads Closed / Hot Leads), (3) Average Handle Time (AHT) reduction vs. human agents, (4) cost per lead vs. cost per qualified lead. Typical ROI improvements: a bot can handle 10–20x more leads per week than a human agent, reducing CPA by 50–70% while maintaining or improving qualification quality. Track these metrics in parallel during the campaign by having human agents update conversion status in a shared sheet daily.

kb_faqs.question: What happens if integrations or prerequisites are missing at kickoff?
kb_faqs.answer: If WABA integration, LSQ/CRM integration, or lead export API is not live at kickoff, the pilot must still proceed without it. Convin should not be dependent on client system readiness. Instead, proceed with manual lead upload (CSV) and manual lead handoff via the click-to-call platform. Document the missing integration and plan it for post-pilot. If a client tries to block the pilot on integration readiness, push back: the pilot is for concept testing, not full system integration. Integrations add complexity and delay proof-of-concept validation.

kb_faqs.question: What voice options are available and which perform best for different client types?
kb_faqs.answer: Available voice options include Shrishti v3, Rani v3, and Anika (all from Cartesia TTS engine). Voice selection is subjective and client-dependent. Some clients prefer slower speech rates (e.g., Shrishti at reduced speed), while others prefer a friendlier tone (Anika). Best practice: (1) start with one recommended voice for UAT, (2) if client dislikes it, demo 2–3 alternatives, (3) get client approval before production rollout. Voice changes post-UAT require re-recording agent setups but do not impact conversation logic.

kb_faqs.question: What should a Sales Handover Form document before campaign kickoff?
kb_faqs.answer: Sales Handover Form must include: (1) Current calling benchmarks (e.g., human agents achieve 60% qualification rate, 2% conversion), (2) Pilot goal and success metrics (e.g., maintain qualification rate, reduce CPA by 40%), (3) Lead volume and timeline (e.g., 1000 leads over 3 weeks), (4) Client's current process (how they source, qualify, and close leads), (5) Key stakeholder contact and decision-maker, (6) Integration requirements and blockers, (7) DND hours and compliance requirements, (8) Budget and contract terms. This document should be signed off by Sales and Implementation before kickoff.

kb_faqs.question: What causes campaigns to not pause on scheduled days (e.g., Sundays)?
kb_faqs.answer: Campaigns may continue running on Sundays or other scheduled pause days if the campaign pause logic is not properly configured or if the campaign was created before the pause feature was implemented. When this occurs, leads are called and connected despite being outside the intended calling window, increasing client complaints. Solution: verify campaign configuration includes pause rules for specific days, check agent-level overrides, and manually pause if needed. This should be tested during UAT.

kb_faqs.question: What are the prerequisites for launching WhatsApp campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Prerequisites: (1) WABA (WhatsApp Business Account) integration must be live, (2) WhatsApp calling must be enabled on the agent profile, (3) correct TTS voice ID (Cartesia, not ElevenLabs) must be configured, (4) message template must be approved by Meta (applies to utility templates and marketing messages), (5) recipient opt-in must be confirmed (cannot send to non-opted contacts). If any of these are missing, WhatsApp campaign will not send or bot will fail to speak. Test WhatsApp flow separately from AI voice to catch these issues early.

kb_faqs.question: What's the difference between utility and marketing WhatsApp templates and how do they impact delivery?
kb_faqs.answer: Utility templates (e.g., appointment confirmations, shipping updates) have higher delivery rates and less restrictive approval. Marketing templates (e.g., promotional offers) have lower delivery rates and stricter Meta approval requirements. Delivery rates also depend on sender reputation, recipient engagement history, and opt-in status. Payment failures on the WABA account will block all template delivery. Check WABA account payment status and template approval status in Meta Business Manager before campaign launch. Failure to do so will result in zero message delivery and campaign stalls.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: How should phone numbers be formatted in the lead CSV?
kb_faqs.answer: Phone numbers should include the country code (e.g., +91 for India, +1 for US). The system will attempt to match numbers to the configured country and parse them correctly, but including the full international format (E.164 format) ensures no ambiguity. Examples: +919876543210 (India), +12025551234 (US). Numbers without country codes or in incorrect formats may fail to dial or be flagged as invalid.

kb_faqs.question: What should be validated during UAT before moving to production campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: UAT validation checklist: (1) Bot successfully connects to 50%+ of dialed numbers, (2) conversation flow is natural with no abrupt cuts or latency issues, (3) bot speaks clearly without mispronunciation, (4) qualification logic correctly identifies hot/warm leads, (5) DND hours are respected, (6) WhatsApp integration works if required, (7) lead data from CSV imports correctly, (8) callback timing works as expected, (9) hot/warm lead counts and conversation summaries are accurate, (10) client is satisfied with voice and persona. Only after these are confirmed should production campaigns be launched.

kb_faqs.question: What is the impact of latency and interruption handling on call quality?
kb_faqs.answer: High latency (>2 second delay between prospect speaking and bot responding) causes prospects to think the call is dead and hang up. Poor interruption handling (bot continuing to speak while prospect tries to interrupt) creates frustration and leads to hangups. Clients perceive this as low-quality calls even if qualification logic is correct. These issues are often due to TTS engine delays or conversation model response time. Test latency and interruption on 10+ sample calls during UAT and adjust if needed before production rollout.

kb_faqs.question: What does a 'connected' vs 'not connected' vs 'warm' vs 'hot' lead actually mean?
kb_faqs.answer: Definitions: (1) Connected = prospect answered and bot delivered opening statement (call connected), (2) Not connected = no pickup or hangup before bot spoke (call dropped), (3) Warm = prospect answered some profiling questions but not all, or partial engagement detected, (4) Hot = prospect answered all or nearly all profiling questions, indicating genuine interest. These classifications drive the handoff to human agents. Only hot and warm leads should be passed to humans for callback.

kb_faqs.question: What is the 'talk time' feature and why is it needed?
kb_faqs.answer: Talk time feature would measure the total duration of meaningful conversation in a call and use it as a gating factor for lead qualification. Instead of relying solely on LLM analysis, a call with <10 seconds of talk time would be automatically classified as 'cold' even if the LLM detected engagement. This would reduce false-positive warm/hot classifications caused by very short exchanges. Implementation would require adding a talk time calculation to the post-call analysis and setting thresholds per client.

kb_faqs.question: What is the root cause of numbers being spelled out (e.g., '11' as 'G-Y-A-R-A-H')?
kb_faqs.answer: When the Cartesia TTS engine receives a number in numeric format (e.g., '11'), it sometimes converts it to the word form in the target language, and if that word is passed with incorrect casing or formatting, it gets spelled out letter-by-letter. Fix: format all numeric data (names, numbers) in sentence case before passing to TTS, or explicitly tell the TTS to treat numbers as digits and not convert them to words.

kb_faqs.question: How do you handle clients who want concurrent call scaling from a single phone number?
kb_faqs.answer: Clients often ask if 100 concurrent calls can run from a single phone number. Answer: No. Each concurrent call requires a unique phone number due to telecom infrastructure limitations. However, if the client has their own dialer/PBX system, Convin can integrate with it to route outbound calls through their existing number pool. For the pilot, discuss whether they'll procure additional numbers or integrate with their existing dialer. This should be clarified in the MSA before the pilot starts.

kb_faqs.question: What does it mean when a pilot is labeled 'for concept testing only' and why does this matter?
kb_faqs.answer: 'Concept testing' pilots have a narrow scope: validate that the bot can qualify leads better than random outreach, and that hand-off to humans works. These pilots should NOT include full CRM integrations, complex workflows, or new features. Scope creep kills pilots. If a client requests an integration (e.g., auto-demo scheduling), push back clearly: 'This is for concept validation only. Integrations come in phase 2 after proof-of-value.' Get sales buy-in on this messaging to prevent daily requests for 'just one more integration.'

kb_faqs.question: Why should a conversion tracking sheet be updated by humans in parallel during the campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: If human agents don't log conversion status (whether they closed the lead or not) in real-time, ROI cannot be calculated until days or weeks after the campaign ends. Tracking in parallel allows Convin to: (1) validate that hot leads are actually converting at higher rates than warm/cold, (2) identify process bottlenecks (e.g., agent not calling back hot leads on time), (3) calculate accurate CPA and conversion metrics mid-campaign, (4) adjust strategy if conversion is underperforming. This is a hard requirement; make it clear in the kickoff that daily updates are mandatory.

kb_faqs.question: What is needed to export qualified leads and their conversation summaries to CRM systems?
kb_faqs.answer: A feature request exists for exporting each qualified lead with a 500-character conversation summary and post-call CRM update payload to the client's CRM. This requires defining: (1) which fields to extract from the conversation (intent, objections, next steps), (2) CRM field mapping (Convin field → CRM field), (3) trigger logic (export immediately after call or batch-export nightly). For pilots, this is typically a manual export or CSV integration rather than a real-time API call. Discuss implementation approach during kickoff.

kb_faqs.question: Why do some pilots fail to progress past kickoff?
kb_faqs.answer: Pilots stall when prerequisites are not met or clients delay submitting them. Common blockers: (1) WABA integration not approved by client IT (takes 1–2 weeks), (2) Lead CSV not shared or in wrong format (delays UAT start), (3) Client stakeholder unavailable for kickoff call (scope misalignment), (4) Client wants integrations before concept validation (scope creep), (5) Client delays confirming success metrics (no clear go/no-go criteria). Sales must ensure all prerequisites are confirmed in writing before kickoff date is set. If a client has not provided leads or signed MSA 3 days before kickoff, consider postponing.

kb_faqs.question: What should you say when a client claims the bot voice sounds unnatural or has bad pronunciation?
kb_faqs.answer: Acknowledge the concern and provide context: 'The current voice achieves X% qualification rate on your leads, which is comparable to your human team's performance. Pronunciation issues on <2% of calls are being addressed in the next version.' Offer solutions: (1) try a different voice from the available options, (2) adjust speech rate, (3) improve input data formatting (names in mixed case). Make clear that voice tweaks don't block the pilot—the goal is lead qualification, not perfect voice synthesis. If the client insists on a 'perfect' voice before UAT completion, de-scope and focus on core metrics.

kb_faqs.question: What payment issues can cause WhatsApp message delivery to fail?
kb_faqs.answer: WhatsApp message delivery will fail if: (1) the WABA account has a pending payment or failed card charge, (2) the Meta Business Account is not in good standing, (3) the billing payment method has expired. Check in Meta Business Manager under Billing > Invoices and Payment Methods. If payment failed, work with the client's finance team or WABA SPOC to clear the payment immediately. Until cleared, no WhatsApp messages will deliver and the channel is effectively down. Document payment status before campaign launch.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What does NBA (next best action) mean in the context of a qualification campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: NBA is the automated next step after a lead is qualified. Examples: (1) if hot lead, trigger immediate callback by human agent, (2) if warm lead, schedule followup call in 24 hours, (3) if cold lead, add to nurture drip campaign. NBA execution depends on correct agent configuration, prompt logic, and conversation state tracking. If NBA fails, the lead stays in a 'qualified but unactionable' state and is effectively lost. Always verify NBA configuration and test it on 10+ leads during UAT.

kb_faqs.question: How can conversation design be adjusted if clients find the bot too robotic or lacking empathy?
kb_faqs.answer: Adjustments: (1) add more conversational filler words (e.g., 'I understand', 'absolutely'), (2) reduce machine-like phrases, (3) add personality-aligned language based on target audience (e.g., casual for Gen Z, formal for enterprise), (4) adjust response latency to feel more natural, (5) allow bot to acknowledge prospect objections before moving forward. These changes are made in the system prompt and require re-testing on 20–30 calls. A skilled conversation designer should lead this effort; it's not just prompt wording but conversation flow design.

kb_faqs.question: Why do call drop rates spike 30–60 seconds into calls?
kb_faqs.answer: A pattern was observed where calls drop unexpectedly 30–60 seconds after pickup. Root cause analysis needed to determine if this is due to: (1) network timeout (telecom level), (2) bot latency causing long pauses that prospects interpret as call drop, (3) interruption handling failure, (4) incorrect conversation state management. Solution: analyze 15–20 dropped calls with human ear, check system logs for timeout events, and coordinate with telecom provider if needed. This is a blocking issue affecting connection rate and should be diagnosed immediately.

kb_faqs.question: What happens if a campaign goes live with known issues?
kb_faqs.answer: If issues are discovered during UAT but client wants to proceed to production anyway, formally document the known issue, expected impact, and mitigation plan. Examples: (1) name mispronunciation on 2% of calls → mitigate by improving input data formatting, (2) callback timing may be off by 5–10 minutes → mitigate by setting expectations, (3) latency on poor network calls → mitigate by retrying failed calls. Get written sign-off from client acknowledging the issue. Never hide known issues; full transparency prevents post-pilot disputes.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What happens if duplicate leads are not removed from the CSV?
kb_faqs.answer: Duplicates result in: (1) the same prospect receiving multiple calls from the bot, (2) skewed conversion rates and qualification percentages, (3) client complaint and negative perception of the service. Always deduplicate the CSV by phone number before import. If duplicates are discovered mid-campaign, pause the campaign, remove duplicates, and restart. Provide the client a report of how many duplicates were found and removed.

kb_faqs.question: How should CPA (cost per acquisition) be calculated and what benchmarks exist?
kb_faqs.answer: CPA = (Total Pilot Cost + Infrastructure) / (Qualified Leads / Conversion Rate). Example: If pilot costs 50K INR, produces 100 qualified leads with 5% conversion (5 customers), CPA = 50K / 5 = 10K per customer acquired. Industry benchmarks: (1) traditional outbound (human agents) costs 5K–20K per customer, (2) bot-assisted achieves 2K–8K per customer, (3) 50–70% CPA reduction vs. baseline is typical for successful pilots. Track and report this metric to justify expansion beyond the pilot.

kb_faqs.question: What should be communicated in the internal kickoff before client kickoff?
kb_faqs.answer: Internal kickoff should cover: (1) campaign goal and success metrics, (2) lead quality and expected connection rate, (3) conversation flow and bot persona, (4) team assignments (who owns what), (5) daily standup cadence, (6) escalation path for blockers, (7) known risks and mitigation plans, (8) timeline and milestones. This ensures the Convin team is aligned before speaking to the client. Any discrepancies in understanding should be resolved internally first.

kb_faqs.question: What is 'prompt level tuning' and when is it needed?
kb_faqs.answer: Prompt tuning refers to adjusting the system instructions given to the bot's conversation model. Examples: (1) change opening statement, (2) reorder profiling questions, (3) adjust depth of follow-up questions, (4) add specific objection handling, (5) change bot persona. Prompt changes are made post-UAT based on feedback and don't require code deployment. They should be tested on 20–30 sample calls before production rollout. Prompt tuning is lighter-weight than model swaps and can be done mid-campaign if performance is suboptimal.

kb_faqs.question: Why might an old campaign's TTS voice be incorrect after a system migration?
kb_faqs.answer: System migrations (e.g., switching from ElevenLabs to Cartesia TTS as the default) can leave old campaigns pointing to the old TTS engine. Agents created before the migration may have ElevenLabs Voice IDs instead of Cartesia. This causes TTS failures on WhatsApp or AI calls because the voice ID is not recognized by the new engine. Fix: audit old agents, update voice IDs to match the new TTS engine (e.g., switch to Cartesia voice IDs), and re-test. This is a common issue when upgrading infrastructure.

kb_faqs.question: How should you respond to a client who wants to delay lead submission until integrations are ready?
kb_faqs.answer: Push back: 'Integrations are post-pilot. For the concept test, we'll work with CSVs and manual lead handoff. This allows us to validate the bot's capability independently of your system readiness. We'll design the integration phase after we've proven value.' Stress that delaying lead submission delays proof-of-concept validation and increases time-to-decision. Propose a target date for lead submission (e.g., within 3 business days of MSA) and escalate if client misses it.

kb_faqs.question: What should you do if the actual number of warm/hot leads is much lower than the bot's classification indicates?
kb_faqs.answer: This suggests false-positive classification. Steps: (1) manually audit 20–30 calls labeled as warm/hot to confirm they're truly engaged, (2) check conversation duration and quality indicators, (3) identify patterns in false positives (e.g., all false positives are from a specific time of day or geography), (4) implement talk time gating (minimum X seconds to qualify as warm), (5) adjust LLM prompt to be more stringent, or (6) upgrade to a more capable model (e.g., Gemini 2.5 Pro). This is critical because false positives waste human agent time and inflate ROI projections.

kb_faqs.question: Why is it critical to have at least one client team member on Convin's click-to-call system?
kb_faqs.answer: Having a client user on click-to-call ensures: (1) immediate visibility into hot leads and conversation summaries, (2) faster callback turnaround (minutes instead of hours), (3) client ownership of the qualification process, (4) ability to test and validate the system in real-time, (5) buy-in and confidence from the client stakeholder. Without this, leads sit in a system the client doesn't see, conversions are delayed, and the client perceives low value. Mandate at least one click-to-call user before UAT starts.

kb_faqs.question: How can you resolve issues where a campaign setup appears complete but conversations are pausing or not progressing?
kb_faqs.answer: Common causes: (1) NBA configuration missing or disabled, (2) agent not linked to campaign, (3) system state corruption (rare), (4) conversation model timeout. Diagnosis: check the lead record in the dashboard—does it show 'hot', 'warm', 'cold'? If yes, NBA should trigger next. If not, check agent configuration and campaign settings. If still stuck, use internal tools to manually trigger the next step or escalate to tech team. Test the fix on 10 leads before resuming full campaign.

kb_faqs.question: What is the typical timeline from kickoff to live campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: Timeline: Kickoff (Day 0) → Lead submission + onboarding (Days 1–2) → UAT setup + test calls (Days 3–4) → UAT feedback incorporation (Days 5–6) → Client sign-off on UAT (Day 7) → Live campaign start (Day 8). This assumes all prerequisites are met and feedback incorporation is fast. Delays in any phase push live date back by 2–3 days per delay. Communicate this timeline to the client at kickoff and set expectations for rapid feedback cycles.

kb_faqs.question: What happens if WhatsApp calling is not enabled on the agent profile?
kb_faqs.answer: If WhatsApp calling is disabled on the agent, WhatsApp campaign will not place voice calls—messages may go through but no audio connection is established. The prospect will not receive a WhatsApp call even if the system shows it queued. Fix: Enable 'WhatsApp Calling' in the agent settings (Admin > Agent Config > WhatsApp Calling). Verify this is on before production WhatsApp campaigns. This is a common oversight in multi-channel setups.

kb_faqs.question: Why is it important to clarify the definition of 'success' for a pilot before UAT starts?
kb_faqs.answer: Vague success metrics lead to disputes at pilot end. Clear success definition: 'We will consider the pilot successful if the bot qualifies 15%+ of contacted leads as hot/warm AND the human team converts 5%+ of those leads to customers, achieving a CPA of ≤8K.' Specific definition prevents scope creep and allows both sides to agree on pass/fail upfront. If the client has unrealistic expectations (e.g., 'bot must be perfect or we stop'), address it early and consider closing the deal.

kb_faqs.question: What is the 'rate of speech' parameter and how does it affect call quality?
kb_faqs.answer: Rate of speech controls TTS output speed. Fast speech (1.3–1.5x) causes prospects to feel rushed and struggle to understand. Slow speech (0.8–0.9x) sounds unnatural and extends call duration unnecessarily. Optimal range: 1.0–1.1x (normal to slightly fast). Some clients prefer 0.95x for clarity. Adjust based on UAT feedback. Very old/non-technical prospects prefer slower speech; younger, fast-paced audiences prefer faster speech. This is a quick tuning parameter that should be tested early.

kb_faqs.question: How should you handle a situation where a client's legal/compliance team blocks WABA integration or WhatsApp usage?
kb_faqs.answer: If WhatsApp is blocked, proceed with AI voice-only campaign. If WABA integration is blocked for data governance reasons, propose: (1) Convin handles call data, no lead data shared to Convin's servers (local inference), (2) encryption and data residency guarantees, (3) offline conversation mode if available. If none of these work, accept the constraint and build the pilot around voice calls only. Don't force WhatsApp—it's an enhancement, not a requirement for core qualification.

kb_faqs.question: What is the expected range for conversion rate from hot leads?
kb_faqs.answer: Conversion rates from hot leads (prospects who answered all profiling questions) typically range from 3–10%, depending on industry and prospect type. B2B SaaS: 5–10%. Education/ed-tech: 8–15%. B2C consumer: 2–5%. These rates are measured as 'leads contacted by human agent' → 'leads who completed desired action' (purchase, demo booking, appointment). If conversion rates are <2%, either lead quality is poor (false-positive hot classification) or the human follow-up process is broken.

kb_faqs.question: What is the recommended effort split between hot and warm leads in a campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: Best practice is to allocate effort proportionally: 60% on hot leads and 40% on warm leads. However, both segments must be actively worked simultaneously. Delaying warm lead outreach significantly impacts conversion rates and pilot outcomes. Warm leads should not remain untouched for days, as they represent qualified prospects ready for engagement.

kb_faqs.question: What is the DND hour issue and how should it be handled?
kb_faqs.answer: Messages and calls should never be sent during Do Not Disturb (DND) hours (typically 10 PM - 6 AM or per local regulations). Clients observed WhatsApp messages being sent at 10:33 PM. This requires proper DND configuration in campaign settings and must be validated during UAT. Always ensure DND rules are enabled before campaign launch.

kb_faqs.question: How should critical issues be reported for faster resolution?
kb_faqs.answer: All critical issues must be raised in Jira as 'bugs' (not tasks), and tickets must be moved to 'in-progress' status immediately. Otherwise, they may get missed. Provide lead links and exact reproduction steps. Critical issues blocking next-day campaigns require same-day escalation to tech leads with clear context on pilot impact.

kb_faqs.question: Can custom lead metrics be configured per domain?
kb_faqs.answer: Yes, custom lead metrics and scoring can be configured per domain. For example, collection score configuration is available for financial services domains. Consult the implementation team to understand which metrics are relevant for your industry and how to set collection thresholds for proper lead classification.

kb_faqs.question: What are the most common reasons for call failures in pilot campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Analysis of 20 audited interactions (30-60 seconds each) revealed: Customer not reachable/not connected (30%), Call ended quickly after pitch (15%), Customer busy/asked for callback (25%), No requirement/not interested (20%), Call quality/audio issues (15%), and Bot/voicemail silence issues (5%). These patterns help identify where to focus improvements—most critical is improving connectivity for interested leads (target 80%+).

kb_faqs.question: What causes LLM hallucinations in back-to-back calling scenarios?
kb_faqs.answer: LLM hallucinations causing repeated calls on the same lead are often triggered by multi-goal agent configurations. When multiple goals are enabled in a single agent, the system can incorrectly retrigger calls believing goals weren't achieved. The fix is to configure goals separately and create different agents per goal. This issue typically affects only individual leads but can escalate if multi-goal setup isn't reviewed.

kb_faqs.question: What is the expected lead handoff turnaround time for human agents?
kb_faqs.answer: Once leads are marked as 'hot' or 'warm' by the bot, human agents should attempt contact within 30 minutes to 1 hour. Delays beyond this window significantly reduce conversion probability. Best practice is 3 attempts per day with 30-minute gaps between attempts. Agents should begin calling warm leads immediately after bot profiling is complete, not wait for all leads to be profiled first.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What makes a good lead CSV for campaign launch?
kb_faqs.answer: High-quality lead CSVs should have: (1) Fresh data (generated within last 2-3 hours when possible), (2) Accurate phone numbers with correct country code formatting, (3) Clear contact names and minimal spelling errors, (4) Deduplication already applied, (5) Compliance with DND/opt-out lists, (6) Correct lead source attribution. Poor CSV quality directly impacts connectivity rates and should be validated before UAT.

kb_faqs.question: Why are all profiling questions being asked in a single call problematic?
kb_faqs.answer: Asking all profiling questions in one call leads to longer conversations, higher disconnect rates, and poor customer experience. Best practice is to start with WhatsApp message first (for connectivity), then transition to voice call with 1-2 key qualifying questions. This phased approach improves answer quality and reduces call abandonment rates.

kb_faqs.question: Should WhatsApp be the first channel before voice calls?
kb_faqs.answer: Yes, WhatsApp should always be the first engagement channel for better connectivity and experience. Start with WhatsApp text/voice note, then transition to voice call if needed. This is the standard Sense flow. Starting directly with voice calls skips this high-conversion channel and breaks the established cadence. WhatsApp inbound calling should also be enabled to allow customers to call back directly.

kb_faqs.question: What WhatsApp setup is required before campaign launch?
kb_faqs.answer: Before any campaign: (1) WhatsApp Business account must have client logo, proper business name, description, and website URL filled in, (2) Blue verification tick should be obtained if available (improves trust), (3) WhatsApp calling must be enabled from Meta dashboard (not just Convin dashboard), (4) Both of these can be done by client SPOC with Meta access or by implementation team. Campaign won't work without Meta-level enablement.

kb_faqs.question: What causes WhatsApp message failures?
kb_faqs.answer: Common issues: (1) DND hours violations, (2) Invalid or incorrect phone numbers, (3) WhatsApp calling not enabled at Meta level, (4) Template approval not completed, (5) Lead ownership changes not synced properly. When troubleshooting, check the campaign logs for specific error codes. For bulk failures across multiple leads, investigate template status, DND configuration, or sync issues with third-party integrations like Exotel.

kb_faqs.question: What is the issue with TTS (Text-to-Speech) voice generation failures?
kb_faqs.answer: When using ElevenLabs TTS, if you see 'payment_issue' errors, the subscription has incomplete payment. Switch TTS provider to Cartesia as workaround. Ensure Voice ID is properly configured in agent settings. If Cartesia TTS shows latency spikes (3-5 seconds observed), escalate to provider for optimization. For new agents, default to Cartesia if available.

kb_faqs.question: How should call recording failures be handled?
kb_faqs.answer: Call recordings between human agents and customers sometimes fail to download due to third-party API errors (e.g., Exotel). When this occurs: (1) Check error logs on tech dashboard, (2) Request retries to be implemented for failed downloads, (3) For bulk failures, escalate to third-party provider, (4) Track which leads are affected and manually verify. This should be preventive—add automatic retry logic to reduce manual intervention.

kb_faqs.question: What causes RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) tool failures?
kb_faqs.answer: RAG tools fail when: (1) Knowledge base is uploaded in JSON format instead of plain text (LLM confidence embedding model doesn't support JSON), (2) Tool configuration is not saved properly after creation, (3) Widget can't reach the RAG endpoint. Fix: Convert all KB content to plain text format, click 'save' to recreate embeddings, hard-refresh the page. For JSON-based content, extract text and reformat before uploading.

kb_faqs.question: How do conflicting KB instructions affect NBA generation?
kb_faqs.answer: When KBs contain instructions like 'don't output JSON' or 'do not generate JSON', these conflict with the main NBA prompt that requires JSON output. This causes NBA generation to fail or hallucinate. Fix: Remove all anti-JSON instructions from pillar KBs. This is a permanent pipeline issue—the KB generation pipeline should be updated to avoid adding such conflicting instructions automatically.

kb_faqs.question: What causes campaigns to continue calling leads after ownership is transferred to humans?
kb_faqs.answer: When lead ownership changes from Convin to human agent, the system sometimes continues triggering AI calls or WhatsApp messages. This happens when the ownership change isn't properly synced with the calling orchestration system (e.g., Exotel). Work with tech team to verify: (1) Lead status updates propagate correctly, (2) Calling system checks lead ownership before triggering, (3) Sync mechanisms are working.

kb_faqs.question: What are spam numbers in human agent calling and how should they be handled?
kb_faqs.answer: Some phone numbers assigned to human agents are flagged as spam or have poor reputation, causing customers to reject calls and reducing connectivity. These should be identified and removed from the agent's pool. Request client to validate agent numbers or switch to fresh numbers with better reputation. This directly impacts conversion and should be resolved before scaling campaigns.

kb_faqs.question: What is the hot/warm lead classification issue and how should definitions be updated?
kb_faqs.answer: In some cases, leads where the AI bot has completed full profiling are still classified as 'warm' instead of 'hot'. This happens due to loose or inaccurate hot/warm definitions. Update definitions so that: (1) Hot leads = full profiling completed + clear requirement identified, (2) Warm leads = partial profiling + interest shown but needs clarification. Review call transcripts and reclassify misidentified leads to improve agent prioritization.

kb_faqs.question: What causes campaign analytics dashboard to show incorrect data?
kb_faqs.answer: Dashboard filters defaulting to 'Last 7 Days' instead of 'Today' cause confusion about real-time performance. Always check the active filter before analyzing metrics. Additionally, some calls may not appear in summaries if the calls were made outside the filtered date range. Hard-refresh the page after campaign updates to see latest data. For debugging, cross-check raw call logs with dashboard numbers.

kb_faqs.question: Should there be a confirmation message after goal achievement on a call?
kb_faqs.answer: Yes, this is a feature request from pilots. Currently, if the goal is achieved on the first call, no follow-up message is sent, which can feel incomplete to the customer. Ideal flow: Once goal achieved on call, send a WhatsApp summary/confirmation message. This improves customer experience and provides documented proof of interaction. Add to roadmap as low-priority feature for future release.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What is the recommended retry cadence for leads that don't connect?
kb_faqs.answer: For leads with no connectivity, use 3 attempts per day with 30-minute gaps between attempts. This balances customer experience (not spamming) with reaching potential buyers. Warm leads that are prioritized for human calling should have human attempts coordinated with bot retries to avoid duplicate calls. After 3 days of no connectivity, mark as 'did not reach' and move to lower priority bucket.

kb_faqs.question: How should multi-goal agents be configured to avoid hallucinations?
kb_faqs.answer: Multi-goal agents (single agent with multiple objectives like qualification + scheduling) can cause the bot to incorrectly retrigger if it loses context. Better approach: Create separate agents per goal or use sequential goal logic. For example: Agent 1 = Profiling, Agent 2 = Closing. This prevents the bot from looping back unnecessarily and improves clarity on what was already achieved per lead.

kb_faqs.question: What is the pre-campaign checklist for UAT?
kb_faqs.answer: Before UAT: (1) Conversation design reviewed and approved by sales lead, (2) Test call made by leadership (10-15 test calls minimum), (3) WhatsApp handle fully set up with logo, name, description, (4) WhatsApp calling enabled at Meta level, (5) DND rules configured, (6) Small lead batch (50-100) provided, (7) Wallet/credits topped up, (8) Human agent onboarded and trained, (9) All tech dependencies verified (TTS working, RAG responding, NBA generating). UAT approval from leadership required 24 hours before go-live.

kb_faqs.question: How should agents be trained for bot handoff calls?
kb_faqs.answer: Agents receiving bot-qualified leads need training on: (1) Understanding bot profiling output and NBA summary, (2) Verification of customer information before pitch, (3) Handling objections already surfaced in bot conversation, (4) Converting without repeating questions already asked, (5) Closing techniques for warm leads, (6) When to schedule follow-up vs. closing. Handoff notes from the bot should clearly state what was discussed and what customer expressed interest in.

kb_faqs.question: What ROI metrics should be tracked for Sense pilot campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Key ROI metrics: (1) Connectivity rate (% of leads reached, target 80%+), (2) Qualification rate (% moved to hot/warm, target 40-50%), (3) Conversion rate (% of qualified leads closed by humans, target 15-25%), (4) Cost per acquisition (compare bot + human follow-up vs. traditional dialing), (5) Time to conversion (days from first contact to deal), (6) Lead volume processed per agent per day. Compare against baseline if moving from Voicebot or manual dialing.

kb_faqs.question: Why is increasing qualified lead volume important even if conversion rate is lower?
kb_faqs.answer: Moving from Voicebot to Sense sometimes shows slightly lower conversion rates per lead (quality trade-off), but much higher qualified lead volume reaching human agents. For example: Voicebot = 100 leads with 25% conversion vs. Sense = 300 qualified leads with 15% conversion. Net result: 37.5 closes vs. 45 closes. The funnel expansion outweighs conversion rate dip. Focus on increasing volume of qualified leads delivered to humans rather than micro-optimizing conversion per lead.

kb_faqs.question: What are the top customer objections heard in pilot calls?
kb_faqs.answer: Top objections: (1) 'Not now / not interested' (20%), (2) 'Already exploring / will think and come back' (15%), (3) 'Busy in work / out of town' (callback requests, 25%), (4) 'No requirement right now' (20%), (5) 'Just browsing / exploring' (10%), (6) Health/personal reasons (5%), (7) Call quality issues prevent engagement (5%). Train agents to address these with appropriate rebuttals and timing for follow-up.

kb_faqs.question: How should 'already exploring with competitors' objection be handled?
kb_faqs.answer: This objection indicates genuine interest but active evaluation elsewhere. Strategy: (1) Acknowledge the competition, (2) Differentiate quickly with 2-3 unique value props, (3) Offer comparison or feature walkthrough, (4) Schedule a tighter follow-up (within 3-5 days) to prevent loss to competitor, (5) Provide collateral for quick reference. This lead should be marked 'warm' and prioritized for immediate human follow-up.

kb_faqs.question: What is the optimal call length for AI-bot calls during profiling?
kb_faqs.answer: Optimal call length for profiling is 30-90 seconds. Calls under 30 seconds suggest customer hung up (low intent), 30-60 seconds is typical for initial qualifying question, 60-90 seconds allows for 2-3 follow-up questions. Calls over 2 minutes indicate deep engagement and should be marked hot. If bot is rambling beyond 90 seconds without clear progress, script needs tightening.

kb_faqs.question: What is click-to-call feature and when should it be enabled?
kb_faqs.answer: Click-to-call allows customers to directly call back the business from ads or bot messages via WhatsApp. Enable for ad campaigns to capture inbound intent. Useful when running campaigns with strong creative—interested customers can self-initiate calls. Requires WhatsApp calling enabled and a dedicated agent phone line. Can significantly improve conversion when customer takes initiative to reach out.

kb_faqs.question: What is the expectation for call latency in voice bot conversations?
kb_faqs.answer: Clients typically expect <1.5 seconds latency for bot responses. However, observed latencies are sometimes 3-5 seconds due to TTS provider (Cartesia) spikes or LLM processing delays. Communicate latency expectations upfront. Latency >2 seconds is noticeable to customers and may cause disconnect. Work with infra team to optimize—can include switching TTS providers, caching responses, or upgrading endpoints.

kb_faqs.question: How should customer health-related interruptions be handled?
kb_faqs.answer: In ~5% of calls, customers mention health issues or personal emergencies. Script should include: (1) Pause the call with empathy, (2) Offer to reschedule, (3) Note in CRM for gentle future follow-up, (4) Don't pressure for sale during this window. Mark as 'callback requested' and set follow-up for 7-14 days later. These are often legitimate leads once the situation improves.

kb_faqs.question: What are the common issues when onboarding new agents to the Convin platform?
kb_faqs.answer: Onboarding issues: (1) Agents not clear on which leads to call (hot vs. warm prioritization), (2) Agents also juggling other dialing targets, reducing Convin focus, (3) Unavailability on announced campaign days, (4) Not trained on reading bot profiling notes before calling, (5) Using personal numbers instead of platform, breaking analytics, (6) No SOP on when to call back or mark lead status. Solution: Use fully dedicated agents, provide written SOP, do role-play training.

kb_faqs.question: Why is agent dedication critical for pilot success?
kb_faqs.answer: Non-dedicated agents juggling Convin leads with other dialing targets show poor performance because they prioritize their personal targets. For a 100-lead pilot: dedicated agent can handle 22 attempts per day on hot leads, non-dedicated agent does ~5 attempts. This delays follow-up windows, misses the conversion window, and makes pilot appear to underperform. Insist on 1-2 fully dedicated agents for UAT and early campaigns.

kb_faqs.question: What communication gaps typically occur with client teams during campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Common gaps: (1) Client doesn't inform Convin when onboarded agents become unavailable, (2) Client assigns last-minute replacement agents without notification, (3) No SOP document shared, so agents work ad-hoc, (4) Client SPOC not available for UAT issues, (5) Lead data quality issues not escalated early. Solution: Establish a single point of contact (SPOC), create written campaign SOP, require 48-hour advance notice of changes, schedule daily sync calls during UAT.

kb_faqs.question: What should be in the SOP (Standard Operating Procedure) for agents?
kb_faqs.answer: Campaign SOP should include: (1) Lead prioritization (call hot first, then warm), (2) Attempt cadence (3 attempts per day with 30-min gaps), (3) DND hours (when NOT to call), (4) Script/talking points, (5) Profiling questions already asked by bot (don't repeat), (6) Objection handling, (7) When to close vs. when to follow-up, (8) How to mark lead status in system, (9) Callback and escalation process, (10) Daily reporting requirements. Update as learnings emerge.

kb_faqs.question: How many test calls should be completed before UAT approval?
kb_faqs.answer: Minimum 10-15 test calls should be made by sales/leadership before UAT is approved. These should cover: (1) Various customer response scenarios (yes, no, maybe), (2) Different profiling question paths, (3) Objection scenarios, (4) DND hour checks, (5) WhatsApp message delivery, (6) Bot fallback scenarios. Each test call should be scored on quality (clarity, tone, relevance, naturalness). Issues found must be fixed and retested before UAT.

kb_faqs.question: What is the requirement for leadership sign-off before go-live?
kb_faqs.answer: No campaign should go live without explicit sign-off from leadership (sales lead or account owner) at least 24 hours before. Sign-off must include: (1) Personal testing of the bot (minimum 1 call), (2) Review of conversation design and script, (3) Confirmation of all prerequisites (WhatsApp setup, DND rules, agent onboarding, tech checks), (4) Client readiness confirmation. This prevents broken campaigns and ensures quality.

kb_faqs.question: Why is Day 1 connectivity around 64% and how can it be improved?
kb_faqs.answer: Day 1 connectivity of ~64% (successfully connecting with customer) is typical for cold outreach. Factors: (1) Some numbers are disconnected or wrong, (2) Customers don't answer unknown numbers, (3) DND or time zone issues, (4) Poor number quality from data source. Improvement strategies: (1) Refresh lead data frequently, (2) Validate numbers before import, (3) Use WhatsApp first before voice, (4) Retry during different time windows, (5) Remove flagged spam numbers. Hot leads (already interested) should have 80%+ connectivity.

kb_faqs.question: What percentage of leads should be fully profiled after bot outreach?
kb_faqs.answer: Target is 40-50% of outreached leads becoming 'hot' (fully profiled with clear requirement). Remaining 30-40% become 'warm' (partial profiling) or 'cold' (no engagement). If profiling rate is below 30%, review: (1) Script quality—questions may be too long or complex, (2) Call length—if calls disconnect under 30 seconds, rework opening, (3) Customer intent—data quality may be poor, (4) Bot tone—may sound too robotic or aggressive. Aim to improve over campaign days.

kb_faqs.question: What conversion rate should be expected from bot-qualified warm leads with human follow-up?
kb_faqs.answer: Warm leads handed to human agents typically show 15-25% conversion rate (booking a meeting or expressing buying intent). This is 3-4x higher than cold outreach (5-8%) due to bot pre-qualification. For hot leads already with clear requirements, conversion can reach 30-40% with good human selling. If human conversion is <10%, review: (1) Lead quality from bot, (2) Agent skill/script, (3) Follow-up timing, (4) Price alignment.

kb_faqs.question: How many leads can a single AI agent typically process per day?
kb_faqs.answer: A single AI voice bot can make 100-150 outbound calls per day, depending on script length and call duration. For example: campaign of 118 leads with 30-90 second calls per attempt can be completed in 1-2 days. With retries (3 attempts per lead with 30-min gaps), ~80-100 leads can be attempted daily. WhatsApp can reach 500+ leads per day due to lower latency. Scale outbound volume by multiplying leads × expected attempts.

kb_faqs.question: What is the typical breakdown of lead classification after bot outreach?
kb_faqs.answer: For a 100-lead cohort: Hot leads (full profiling, clear requirement) = 12-20%, Warm leads (partial profiling, some interest) = 20-30%, Connected but not interested = 20-30%, Not reached/no answer = 20-40%. This breakdown varies by industry and data quality. During a 4-day campaign with retries, the 'not reached' bucket should shrink to <20%. Classify leads based on profiling completion (# of questions answered) and intent signals.

kb_faqs.question: How should performance be tracked and reported during campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Daily reporting should include: (1) # leads called / attempted, (2) # leads connected (by %), (3) # leads profiled (hot/warm/cold breakdown), (4) # leads handed to human agent, (5) Avg call duration, (6) Top objections heard, (7) Any technical issues or blockers. Set up automated dashboards or daily Slack/email summaries. Compare daily metrics against targets to catch issues early. Share with both Convin and client teams daily during UAT and first week of campaigns.

kb_faqs.question: What does 'bot went silent' issue mean and how should it be prevented?
kb_faqs.answer: 'Bot went silent' refers to calls that connect but the bot doesn't speak or hangs up immediately. Causes: (1) TTS not generating audio, (2) API timeout, (3) LLM not responding, (4) Audio codec mismatch. Prevention: (1) Test each conversation path multiple times before UAT, (2) Set reasonable timeouts (max 15 seconds for LLM response), (3) Add fallback logic (if bot silent >3 sec, disconnect and retry), (4) Monitor in real-time during campaign launch. If >1% of calls fail silently, escalate immediately.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot determine which channel (voice vs. WhatsApp) to use?
kb_faqs.answer: Recommended flow: (1) Start with WhatsApp message (simple introduction), (2) If no response within 30 min, initiate voice call, (3) If call fails/disconnects, send WhatsApp follow-up, (4) If warm lead, have human agent call via Click-to-Call. This multi-channel approach improves overall connectivity. Don't force voice-first as it has lower pickup rates. WhatsApp inbound calling should be enabled to let customers call back.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: How should lead data be validated before campaign import?
kb_faqs.answer: CSV validation checklist: (1) Phone number format—verify country code, remove special chars, (2) Deduplicate—no phone number appears twice, (3) Compliance—remove DND registry numbers, opt-out requests, (4) Freshness—leads generated <48 hours old, (5) Name/company field populated but not mandatory, (6) No obviously fake numbers (all 0s or sequential), (7) Data source clarity (where did lead come from), (8) Expected volume reasonable for target market. Use automated validation script if possible to catch errors at scale.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What is the impact of poor lead data quality on campaign performance?
kb_faqs.answer: Poor lead data quality cascades through the entire campaign: (1) Disconnected/invalid numbers = low connectivity (50% vs. 80%), (2) Wrong customer segment = poor qualification rate, (3) Duplicate numbers = wasted calls and customer frustration, (4) Old data = reached customer who already bought elsewhere, (5) DND numbers = legal compliance risk and wasted effort. For every 10% data quality improvement, expect ~5% improvement in overall conversion. Always invest time in data validation.

kb_faqs.question: What is the NBA (Next Best Action) issue where NBA is not generated?
kb_faqs.answer: NBA not being generated occurs when: (1) RAG tool fails silently, (2) LLM prompt doesn't align with KB format (JSON vs. text mismatch), (3) Confidence threshold not met (bot uncertain about next action), (4) Profiling incomplete (insufficient data to make recommendation). Troubleshoot by checking: (1) Profiling completion on that lead, (2) KB is in plain text format, (3) LLM response in logs, (4) No conflicting instructions in prompts. If affecting >5% of leads, escalate to tech team.

kb_faqs.question: What causes the widget/tool to fail calling the RAG endpoint?
kb_faqs.answer: Widget can't reach RAG when: (1) RAG embeddings not created (save button not clicked after KB upload), (2) KB content format invalid (JSON instead of text), (3) API endpoint down or misconfigured, (4) Rate limiting on RAG service, (5) Network/firewall issue. Fix: (1) Hard-refresh browser, (2) Re-save the RAG tool to recreate embeddings, (3) Convert KB to plain text if JSON, (4) Test RAG API health from backend, (5) Check logs for specific error. This should be tested before campaign launch.

kb_faqs.question: What is the issue with WhatsApp messages not being generated for some leads?
kb_faqs.answer: WhatsApp messages not generated can mean: (1) Lead status change prevented message trigger, (2) Template not approved by Meta, (3) DND hours prevented send, (4) Customer marked 'do not contact', (5) Profiling incomplete so system doesn't know what message to send, (6) NBA not generated. Check logs for: specific error code per lead, template approval status, lead ownership status. If bulk issue, investigate template or DND configuration. Should be <1% failure rate.

kb_faqs.question: How should WhatsApp template delivery rates be monitored?
kb_faqs.answer: Track WhatsApp metrics: (1) # messages sent (target 95%+), (2) # messages delivered (target 85%+), (3) # messages read (target 30-50%), (4) # messages replied (target 5-20%), (5) Template approval status (must be 'APPROVED'), (6) Rejection rate by Meta (watch for sudden spike). Use Meta Business Manager for detailed template performance. If delivery <80%, check: template quality, customer numbers validity, DND compliance, or provider issues.

kb_faqs.question: What is the process for managing bot persona and voice selection?
kb_faqs.answer: Bot persona selection impacts conversion: (1) Tone should match brand (e.g., casual for fintech, formal for BFSI), (2) Voice should be clear, not robotic (natural speech preferred), (3) Pace should be moderate (not too fast, not dragging), (4) Accent should match customer region (local accent usually performs better), (5) Personalization helps (use customer name once profiling starts). Test at least 3 voice options with 10+ calls each before finalizing. Get client sign-off on persona before UAT.

kb_faqs.question: How should the opening line / hook be crafted for best pickup rate?
kb_faqs.answer: Opening line is critical (determines if customer continues call): (1) Keep <15 seconds, (2) Lead with value or curiosity (not 'this is a sales call'), (3) Use customer name if available, (4) Mention how you got their number (referral, signup, etc.) to build credibility, (5) Ask permission to take 30 seconds ('do you have a quick minute?'), (6) Avoid robotic language. Example: 'Hi [Name], I'm calling from [Company]—we help [segment] achieve [outcome]. Do you have 30 seconds?' Test different openings and measure completion rate.

kb_faqs.question: What system prompt patterns work best for Sense AI agents?
kb_faqs.answer: Effective system prompts: (1) Define clear role (e.g., 'You are a product specialist for EdTech'), (2) State goal concisely (e.g., 'Qualify if customer needs training solutions'), (3) Provide 2-3 key talking points, (4) Include guardrails (e.g., 'Don't discuss pricing unless customer asks'), (5) Specify fallback behavior ('If customer not interested, offer callback'), (6) Be concise (<500 words). Avoid: vague personas, conflicting instructions, overly complex logic, and tone mismatches. Review prompt with sales team before UAT.

kb_faqs.question: What profiling questions work best for qualification?
kb_faqs.answer: Effective profiling questions: (1) Start broad ('Are you currently looking for solutions in X area?'), (2) Progress to specific ('What's your main challenge?'), (3) Gauge timeline ('When are you planning to decide?'), (4) Confirm fit ('Does our approach align with what you're looking for?'). Avoid: Yes/no only questions (too limiting), too many questions (>4 will cause hangup), complex jargon, repeated questions. Order matters—build rapport before hard qualifying. Test script with 15+ calls and refine based on completion and clarity metrics.

kb_faqs.question: Should we collect customer feedback or sentiment during bot calls?
kb_faqs.answer: Yes, collecting sentiment is valuable. Add at call end: 'How was this interaction? [1-5 stars or quick NPS]'. This helps: (1) Monitor bot quality in real-time, (2) Identify common pain points, (3) Train humans on customer mindset. However, don't make it mandatory (adds call time). Keep feedback questions <30 seconds. Use sentiment data to refine script and identify high-value vs. poor-fit leads. This is a nice-to-have, not critical for MVP.

kb_faqs.question: What is a workaround if TTS latency is too high?
kb_faqs.answer: If TTS latency exceeds 2 seconds consistently: (1) Switch from ElevenLabs to Cartesia (faster, though may have spikes), (2) Pre-generate some common responses (intro, FAQ answers) to reduce real-time latency, (3) Cache LLM responses for repeated questions, (4) Escalate to infra team to optimize API endpoints. Latency >3 seconds is noticeable and hurts experience. Communicate expected latency to clients upfront and monitor throughout campaign.

kb_faqs.question: How should backup agents be identified in case of planned unavailability?
kb_faqs.answer: For agents with planned leaves (voting, emergencies): (1) Require 48-72 hour notice to Convin, (2) Identify and onboard backup agent before leave starts, (3) Ensure backup knows the lead status and follow-up plan, (4) Brief backup on customer context (what was already discussed), (5) Update client SPOC on coverage change. Don't delay campaigns due to agent unavailability—have a 2-agent bench ready at all times. Lack of coverage is a common blocker and kills pilot momentum.

kb_faqs.question: How should follow-up timing be optimized for warm leads?
kb_faqs.answer: Follow-up timing impact on warm leads: (1) Same day follow-up (within 4 hours) = highest conversion, (2) Next day = 30% drop in conversion, (3) 3+ days = 60% drop. Cadence: Attempt 1 within 1 hour, Attempt 2 within 4 hours, Attempt 3 by EOD. If customer said 'call me tomorrow', respect that but flag for priority next day. Use CRM to automate reminders. Agents who follow up within 1 hour show 2-3x higher close rates.

kb_faqs.question: What training do human agents need after handoff from the bot?
kb_faqs.answer: Agent training post-bot-handoff should cover: (1) Reading bot notes and understanding profiling already done, (2) Not repeating questions (builds frustration), (3) Using bot context to personalize pitch, (4) Handling objections specific to warm leads ('I'm still evaluating'), (5) Closing techniques (trial, discount, timeline), (6) When to escalate to manager, (7) CRM logging and next-step marking, (8) Callback and follow-up scheduling. Do 2-3 role-play practice calls with trainer before going live.

kb_faqs.question: Should bot call attempts be logged and visible to human agents?
kb_faqs.answer: Yes, absolutely. Agents must see: (1) Previous bot call attempts and outcomes, (2) Customer responses to profiling questions, (3) Objections raised during bot call, (4) Sentiment from bot interaction. This context helps agents: (1) Avoid repeating questions, (2) Tailor follow-up strategy, (3) Provide consistent experience, (4) Close faster. Lack of visibility forces agents to re-qualify and frustrates customers. Ensure all bot interactions are logged in the agent-facing dashboard.

kb_faqs.question: What is the request for improving lead definitions (hot vs. warm vs. cold)?
kb_faqs.answer: Current definitions need clarification: Hot = full profiling complete (customer answered 3+ qualifying questions) with clear requirement stated. Warm = partial profiling (answered 1-2 questions) or showed interest but needs clarification. Cold = customer hung up quickly or said not interested. Issue: some leads with complete profiling still marked warm. Solution: Automate classification based on # questions answered + intent signals (yes/no to requirement). Update definitions document and retrain teams.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: How should the bot handle callback requests from customers?
kb_faqs.answer: When customer says 'call me back later/tomorrow': (1) Confirm time slot ('3 PM tomorrow works?'), (2) Mark lead as 'callback requested' with timestamp, (3) Remove from immediate retry queue, (4) Schedule automated reminder to bot or agent, (5) When bot/agent calls at requested time, reference the commitment ('You asked me to call at 3 PM'). This shows respect and increases pickup rate. Include in handoff notes if lead goes to human agent.

kb_faqs.question: What does it mean when high background noise is detected in calls?
kb_faqs.answer: High background noise (cars, traffic, machinery) indicates customer is calling from noisy environment (commute, factory). Strategy: (1) Ask if it's a good time to talk ('I notice you're in a noisy place—can you move somewhere quieter?'), (2) Offer callback at specific time, (3) If customer insists, shorten call (max 1 question only), (4) Mark for follow-up during business hours. These leads are still valid (customer picked up), just timing is off. Don't give up on them immediately.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What are the correct CSV column formats for lead upload?
kb_faqs.answer: Use separate 'first_name' and 'last_name' fields instead of a single 'name' field. If only 'name' is provided, the system captures it as an external ID instead of properly parsing the lead name. A minimal valid CSV requires: phone number, external ID, and first_name. Last_name is optional but recommended for complete lead profiling.

kb_faqs.question: What happens when bulk lead upload uses a 'name' column instead of separate name fields?
kb_faqs.answer: The system repeats the name field twice and captures it as an external ID rather than parsing it correctly. This was identified across 2-3 domains. The fix is to change the 'name' column to 'first_name' in the CSV before re-upload.

kb_faqs.question: What should be included in a pre-campaign checklist before UAT?
kb_faqs.answer: Ensure RCA and fixes are completed for any known issues before starting UAT (e.g., multi-message delivery issues). Coordinate internal testing first, then add additional stakeholders for shadowing rounds. Lock timeline and success metrics with the client before UAT begins to avoid delays.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What is the proper DND (Do Not Disturb) configuration for campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Always set DND hours for live campaigns. Test campaigns without DND hours can result in calls at inappropriate times (e.g., 11 PM or 4 AM). DND hours prevent bot from attempting outreach during client-specified quiet periods and should be configured before campaign launch.

kb_faqs.question: How should entity capture prompts be designed to improve accuracy?
kb_faqs.answer: Entity prompts require careful design to avoid inaccuracies. Issues arise when LLM output storage has gaps. If entities show as NA despite correct LLM generation, there may be a disconnect between LLM output and storage. Coordinate with product team to review prompt design and run test cases to validate entity extraction before deploying to live campaigns.

kb_faqs.question: What causes entity outputs to show as NA even when the LLM generates correct values?
kb_faqs.answer: There is a gap between LLM entity generation and storage/display in the system. Running the same entity extraction without changing the prompt may yield correct outputs on retry. The root cause is in the entity output storage mechanism. Requires coordination between conversation design and technical teams to identify and fix the storage logic.

kb_faqs.question: What causes WhatsApp messages to not be attempted despite proper campaign setup?
kb_faqs.answer: Parameter mismatch errors can prevent WhatsApp attempts. If the lead CSV uses 'name' instead of 'first_name' and 'last_name', the parameter matching fails because the system expects separate name fields. Update the CSV schema to match expected parameters (first_name, last_name, phone, external ID) and re-upload leads.

kb_faqs.question: How does click-to-call impact conversion speed and outcomes?
kb_faqs.answer: Click-to-call enables dedicated agents to reach hot/warm leads immediately after bot qualification. Clients typically see faster conversion when agents can reach qualified leads within an hour. One client generated a 16k deal audit from 1 conversion within an hour of running a campaign due to immediate agent callback on bot-qualified leads.

kb_faqs.question: What is the typical conversion rate for low-intent leads in a B2B SaaS context?
kb_faqs.answer: Clients typically see 0.8–1% conversion rates on low-intent lead pools. For example, one client with AoV of 7k and LTV of 15–20k needed 5–6 deals (of 7k each) from a campaign to break even. Running 560 low-intent leads through human agents would generate ~35k INR in expected revenue at 1% conversion.

A client ran a 530-lead campaign and achieved 13 hot leads qualified by the bot. From the same batch, they closed 4 conversions of an 8k MRP product and 1 audit of 16k. Expected revenue from 560 low-intent leads at 1% conversion and 7k AoV is ~35k INR; actual results were higher, demonstrating the ROI of immediate click-to-call handoff.
kb_faqs.question: What ROI metrics should be tracked for AI voice bot campaigns?
What is the difference between 'warm' and 'cold' lead classifications?
kb_faqs.answer: Warm leads show interest signals like agreeing to a callback later, even if they didn't have an extended conversation. Cold leads show no interest or explicit refusal. The system should classify leads as warm if they express willingness for callback/follow-up. Human agents then validate warmth through intent-qualifying calls and move genuine buyers to their sales pipeline.

kb_faqs.question: What causes lead interest status to change when clicking 'refresh status' without new bot/agent activity?
kb_faqs.answer: Single evaluation of the interest metric can update the lead status to a different level than batch evaluation. Refreshing triggers a new single evaluation which may be more accurate. Some leads marked as cold should be warm, and vice versa. The product team is working to improve batch evaluation accuracy to prevent false classifications during initial scoring.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: How should lead sheets be formatted for proper campaign tracking and analysis?
kb_faqs.answer: Create separate Google Sheets with proper column structure (phone, external ID, first_name, last_name, etc.) instead of ad-hoc documentation. Update sheets in real-time as campaigns progress. Use consistent sheet naming conventions and duplicate templates for new campaigns to ensure data quality and make analysis/RCA easier.

kb_faqs.question: What objections arise from connectivity or call quality issues?
kb_faqs.answer: Clients may raise concerns about calls dropping, bot silence, latency, or STT not capturing user responses. When these occur, attribute them to external factors (e.g., 'telephone provider issue') while coordinating internal fixes. Document all incidents with call links and RCA, then prioritize fixes before resuming large campaigns.

kb_faqs.question: What causes bot silence or latency in the middle of calls?
kb_faqs.answer: Root causes vary: STT performing poorly due to background noise, unexpected STT behavior handling user utterance chunks, or LLM latency. Three recent cases (on different clients) had different RCAs. The common theme is background noise impacting speech-to-text quality. Other fundamental issues include edge cases in how STT processes utterance chunks—requires coordination with platform team for fixes.

kb_faqs.question: What is the issue with transcript not capturing user's last response in a call?
kb_faqs.answer: This is typically an STT edge case where the system fails to capture the final user utterance. The issue occurs inconsistently. Product team should test with more substantive user input (e.g., more than just 'hello') to reproduce and diagnose the root cause.

kb_faqs.question: Why is WABA (WhatsApp Business Account) integration not visible on the dashboard after client-side setup?
kb_faqs.answer: Certificate ID may be added to client's Meta account, but the integration does not appear in the Convin platform dashboard. This is a sync/visibility issue between Meta and Convin systems. Ticket should be raised with the technical team to make the integration live and visible, even while RCA is being determined.

kb_faqs.question: What WhatsApp delivery issues are clients requesting to be fixed?
kb_faqs.answer: Clients request the ability to send multiple WhatsApp messages in sequence (vs. single message limit). Do not start UAT for affected campaigns until this RCA and fix are completed by the tech team. Feature ticket should be raised in Jira and prioritized by the product team.

kb_faqs.question: What is the expected lead handoff workflow between bot and human agents?
kb_faqs.answer: At end of day, human agents call hot and warm leads for intent validation. If warm lead shows buying intent, agent moves lead to their sales pipeline. If warm lead is not interested, agent gives lead back to bot for further follow-ups. This ensures qualified leads get immediate attention while bot can re-engage lower-intent prospects.

kb_faqs.question: How should campaigns be structured for appointment-based use cases (e.g., health/wellness)?
kb_faqs.answer: Split campaigns into two tracks: one with 500 leads (bot-only, no human follow-up/reminders) and another 500 leads (with human follow-up and reminders until appointment). This tests the impact of human engagement on appointment confirmation rates and allows the client to evaluate cost-benefit of human involvement.

kb_faqs.question: What does 'not connected' mean vs. 'cold lead' in campaign reporting?
kb_faqs.answer: Not Connected: 0 call attempts made, 0 WhatsApp messages received. Cold Lead: At least 1 call attempt or WhatsApp received, but lead showed no interest. Clients should monitor both categories separately. If leads are not connected, investigate why bot isn't attempting outreach (cooldown issues, template mismatches, engagement period expiry, etc.).

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What causes the bot to stop retrying leads after 1–2 attempts?
kb_faqs.answer: Multiple root causes: (1) Cooldown after manual agent call not handled properly, (2) LLM generating NBA with 0 cooldown causing no retry to be scheduled, (3) Lead engagement period expired (e.g., 4-day campaign with no retries after day 2). These should be tracked in Jira and fixed to ensure continuous engagement during campaign window.

kb_faqs.question: What does it mean when a lead is marked 'Completed' without achieving the business goal?
kb_faqs.answer: This is incorrect behavior. Leads should only be marked Completed when the business goal is actually achieved (e.g., appointment confirmed, purchase made). Marking as Completed prematurely stops all bot engagement. The system should mark leads as Completed programmatically only when goal achievement is confirmed, not based on agent remarks about barriers.
