kb_faqs.answer: Lead Outcome Report download fails during execution, and the campaign-level download button is non-functional. Workaround: open the campaign (not analytics view) and click download button there. This affects clients' ability to get consolidated reports and should be prioritized for fixing as it blocks daily reporting workflows.

kb_faqs.question: What reporting consolidation features are needed by clients running multiple campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Clients running multiple campaigns daily need: (1) consolidated reporting across campaigns, (2) date filters + multi-campaign selection in Lead Interest and Goal Achievement dashboard views, (3) single-click multi-campaign report download instead of manual individual downloads per campaign. Currently, clients must download reports separately for each campaign and manually compile data, adding significant operational overhead.

kb_faqs.question: What are the latency issues observed and how were they resolved?
kb_faqs.answer: High latency was observed on calls, typically causing poor customer experience. Root cause: OpenAI LLM latency was higher than Azure alternatives. Resolution involved migrating tenants from OpenAI to Azure LLM, which showed lower average latency in the last 2 days. Implement ability to switch LLM models from frontend to allow quick provider changes if latency issues recur.

kb_faqs.question: How should monthly plan resets and campaign pauses be handled?
kb_faqs.answer: At month-end, plans expire and must be manually recharged. When plans run out, campaigns auto-pause without warning. Check and manually recharge all domains at month start. Resume paused campaigns after recharging. Implement automatic alerts 3-5 days before plan expiry and auto-pause notifications so teams can proactively manage renewals.

kb_faqs.question: What human-in-the-loop handoff mechanism has been implemented?
kb_faqs.answer: New feature: Automated AI-to-human handoff with lead ownership switching based on defined conditions. Previously, agents manually changed ownership. Now: (1) AI handles conversations first, (2) Based on prompt conditions, bot triggers tool to hand off lead to human, (3) Lead ownership and details automatically update in customer CRM, (4) Control can return to AI after human action via API or automatic logic. This enables smooth two-way handoff without manual intervention.

kb_faqs.question: What is the recommended approach for selecting agent voices for different audiences?
kb_faqs.answer: Match voice selection to target audience demographics and use case: Young audiences prefer younger voices (Geeta 1, Durga v1); older audiences prefer mature voices (Gauri 2); collections/assertive use cases benefit from louder/confident tones (Shrishti v3). Test 2-3 voice options before final selection and consider audience language preference (Hindi vs English). Voice tone impacts customer receptivity and conversion rates significantly.

kb_faqs.question: What causes bot to announce call limits and how should it be prevented?
kb_faqs.answer: Bot was verbally announcing to customers 'Call limit khatam ho chuki hai' (call limits exhausted) when daily/monthly call quotas were reached. This is confusing and negative for customer experience. The bot should NOT communicate internal limits to customers. Instead, silently degrade to WhatsApp-only outreach or queue calls for next period. Fix by removing limit-related messaging from bot persona.

kb_faqs.question: What image analysis issues were identified in campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Image analysis was continuing to run on paused or completed leads, causing unnecessary processing and costs. This was fixed and released; verify on your campaigns. To avoid similar issues, implement checks to halt all processing (calls, messages, analysis) when campaign is paused or lead reaches completion status.

kb_faqs.question: What UAT discount code change issue occurred and how was it handled?
kb_faqs.answer: During UAT, client requested mid-testing discount code change (UNLOCK10 → UPGRADE10) with only 1.5 hours notice. Approximately 5-10 hot leads were potentially affected with outdated codes before update. This highlights the need for: (1) finalized codes before UAT start, (2) advance notice (48h minimum) for any changes, (3) versioning/date tracking of codes used. Implement lead tagging by code version to track impact.

kb_faqs.question: What are the data audit requirements for campaign launches?
kb_faqs.answer: SOP requires: (1) 100 leads audited from first UAT campaign by Data Labs, (2) 50 leads audited from final campaign before go-live, (3) PMs personally audit minimum 20 conversations from all campaigns. This ensures script adherence, conversation quality, and bot accuracy are verified before client sees results. Data Labs typical TAT is 2 days but should aim for 1 day to avoid launch delays.

kb_faqs.question: What are clean phone number procurement challenges and how can they be solved?
kb_faqs.answer: Clean numbers (never used for outreach) are critical for high connection rates. Exotel provides a list where only 30-40% are actually clean; these must be verified and added to the account. Vobiz provides fully clean numbers but has slower turnaround. Current challenge: manual back-and-forth with providers. Solution: Build automated number validation API, maintain internal clean number pools, or establish SLA with providers for guaranteed clean numbers within 24 hours.

kb_faqs.question: What issues occurred with parallel NBA generation?
kb_faqs.answer: System generated multiple parallel NBAs for the same lead, causing duplicate actions and confusion. This indicates a race condition or improper locking mechanism in the NBA execution system. Root cause analysis shows issues with server restarts interfering with NBA state management. Implement better concurrency handling, state validation checks, and campaign health monitoring to detect and fix these issues proactively.

kb_faqs.question: What additional channel improvements are driving better conversion rates?
kb_faqs.answer: Data shows WhatsApp as an additional channel to voice calls drives better overall conversions. WhatsApp provides: (1) asynchronous communication allowing customer to respond at convenience, (2) lower cost per message vs call, (3) better for long-form information sharing, (4) higher engagement rates for certain demographics. Multi-channel approach (call + WhatsApp) shows 20-30% improvement in final conversions compared to voice-only campaigns.

kb_faqs.question: What WebApp URL configuration errors cause failures?
kb_faqs.answer: Incorrect or missing voicebot URL in AI config causes complete call initiation failure. Always verify: (1) URL matches the correct domain environment, (2) Full path is included (not just domain), (3) URL is whitelisted in firewall/CORS settings. This is a frequent configuration issue - add URL validation to agent setup form to catch errors before campaign launch.

kb_faqs.question: What causes Exotel integration failures?
kb_faqs.answer: Exotel integration failures occur due to: (1) account configuration changes not being synced, (2) missing required API keys/settings on their side, (3) firewall/IP whitelist issues. When starting new Exotel accounts, verify all configuration is live on their dashboard. Escalate with Exotel support and request confirmation of account readiness before starting campaigns. Allow 2-3 days for new account activation.

kb_faqs.question: What features are needed for better campaign management visibility?
kb_faqs.answer: PMs and managers need: (1) Way to check all human agent calls on a specific date, (2) Bulk transcript download option for quality audits, (3) Journey-level transcription (not just individual call transcripts), (4) Ability to filter and view data across multiple dates/campaigns simultaneously. These features enable proper oversight, quality audits, and performance analysis without manual data compilation.

kb_faqs.question: What is the impact of server restarts on campaign stability?
kb_faqs.answer: Server restarts can cause: (1) NBA execution to pause mid-batch, leaving leads in pending state, (2) Lead status caches to become stale, (3) Filter operations to malfunction (e.g., hot/cold filters). Implement: (1) graceful shutdown with in-flight operation completion, (2) campaign health checks post-restart, (3) automatic resume of pending operations. Add alerts when NBA generation fails post-restart.

kb_faqs.question: What product feature announcement process should be followed?
kb_faqs.answer: Product feature updates should be communicated through: (1) Dedicated product updates channel (separate from client-specific chats), (2) Structured format with feature name, use case, and go-live date, (3) Training session for all PMs, (4) Documentation in knowledge base, (5) Timeline for release to all accounts. Current bottleneck: updates get missed in crowded chat channels, causing inconsistent rollout and customer confusion.

kb_faqs.question: What data points are needed to accurately measure bot conversion success?
kb_faqs.answer: Measure bot success with multiple conversion indicators: (1) Direct coupon code redemptions, (2) Mobile number captures (for CRM follow-up), (3) Baseline conversion of untouched leads (to compare against), (4) Union of all indicators (not just coupon codes). This prevents false positive inflation and shows true incremental value from bot outreach versus organic conversions. Request this data breakdown from clients before campaign start.

kb_faqs.question: What ROI example was calculated for a typical review generation campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: Example: 500 leads × 20% human connection rate = 100 connected × 60% purchase conversion = 60 purchases × ₹5L value × 0.6 final conversion = ₹1.8L revenue. Against cost of ₹12k (calls + WhatsApp + image analysis), ROI = 15X. Always show: (1) factual current metrics, (2) projected multipliers with assumptions stated, (3) conservative conversion rates (20% connection, 60% purchase), (4) final adjusted numbers (0.6x multiplier for various factors).

kb_faqs.question: What is the recommended timeline for campaign UAT to go-live?
kb_faqs.answer: Typical timeline: (1) Day 1: UAT initiation with 50-100 leads, (2) Day 1-2: Real-time monitoring, issue identification, (3) Day 2-3: Data Labs audit of conversations, (4) Day 3: Internal fixes/prompt adjustments if needed, (5) Day 4: Final review and client approval, (6) Day 5: Go-live with full campaign. Allow 1 day buffer if major issues are found during UAT. Rushing this timeline increases go-live issues and client dissatisfaction.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What does 'next best action already executed' error mean and how is it triggered?
kb_faqs.answer: This error occurs when the system detects that an NBA has already been executed for a lead and tries to prevent duplicate execution. However, if triggered incorrectly, it prevents new NBAs from being generated even when the lead has new pending actions (e.g., customer replied but no response sent). RCA: improper state management in NBA execution tracking. Fix: implement better idempotency keys and separate execution tracking from generation logic.

kb_faqs.question: What is the issue with leads not receiving calls and what should be checked?
kb_faqs.answer: If leads don't receive calls, systematically check: (1) Exotel account active and properly configured, (2) Clean phone numbers allocated to campaign, (3) Voicebot URL correct in agent config, (4) DND filtering properly enabled to prevent blocked numbers, (5) Call quota not exhausted, (6) Campaign status is 'active' not paused. Most failures are configuration-related; verify each step before escalating to tech team.

kb_faqs.question: What qualification rates are typical across pilot campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Typical qualification rates (hot + warm leads) range from 30-35% of total leads attempted. In one campaign with 1,000 total leads, 350 were qualified as hot or warm (35%), with human connectivity achieved on 135 of those attempted (47% of qualified leads). These metrics vary by lead quality and bot configuration.

kb_faqs.question: What conversion rates should we expect in early pilot campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Early pilot conversions typically range from 3-6% on total leads depending on bot configuration and lead quality. In one campaign, 32 conversions were achieved on 1,000 leads (3.2%), though this did not include conversions from image analysis which were pending reporting. Performance improves significantly when both bot and human follow-up are optimized.

kb_faqs.question: How does Convin's cost per conversion compare to human-only models?
kb_faqs.answer: With optimized pricing (0.8 rupees per VB pulse, 0.3 rupees per WhatsApp, 0.5 rupees per image analysis), combined bot+human CPC typically ranges from INR 53-62 per conversion, which is competitive with human-only CPC benchmarks of INR 49. On expanded lead sets (60 reviews), combined setups achieve INR 53 CPC against 6% conversion rates on attempted leads, very close to human-only INR 49 benchmark at 11% conversion rates.

kb_faqs.question: What's the payback period when moving from POC to annual contracts?
kb_faqs.answer: Clients converting from POC to annual contracts typically see rapid value realization. In one case, a client converted to annual contract and showed strong interest in independent bot building within 2-3 weeks of go-live, indicating quick ROI and adoption. In-person enablement sessions (2-3 hours) accelerated client confidence and reduced dependency on Convin team support.

kb_faqs.question: How should we classify leads into hot and warm categories during qualification?
kb_faqs.answer: Hot leads are those who provide session date and time commitment during bot conversation. Warm leads show interest in products/services but don't commit to immediate action. In UAT with 200 leads, typical splits are ~7% hot (13 leads) and ~7% warm (14 leads). Disqualified leads should be analyzed separately to improve bot flow and objection handling.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What are the most common lead data quality issues that block campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Common issues include: (1) Invalid/junk names (e.g., 'ABC', '123', 'KBC') causing bot to mispronounce; (2) Blank name fields requiring fallback to 'Sir/Ma'am'; (3) Names from multiple sources with inconsistent formatting (with/without spaces); (4) Leads already in active state with NBA generated showing as 'Lead ID already exists' or 'Phone number already exists and not archived' in upload reports. Clean CSVs should have standardized name fields and avoid duplicate phone numbers.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What's the proper format for bulk lead uploads and how should we validate them?
kb_faqs.answer: Leads should be uploaded in CSV format with standardized columns including phone number, name, and any custom fields. Validation checks should flag duplicate phone numbers, missing critical fields, and leads already in active state. When uploading large batches (1,500+ leads), expect latency; platform should queue uploads separately. After upload confirmation, always refresh and verify leads appear in campaign before launching. Test with smaller batches (50-100 leads) before production uploads.

kb_faqs.question: What's the recommended UAT process before going live?
kb_faqs.answer: Run UAT on 100-200 leads split between old and new lead sources separately, as they cannot be compared against each other. Old/previously-disqualified leads lack conversion benchmarks, so fresh UAT on new leads is critical for validating bot performance. UAT should run for 2-3 days minimum to collect sufficient data. Go-live decision should be based only on fresh-lead UAT performance, not old-lead recycling campaigns.

kb_faqs.question: What pre-campaign checklist items must be completed before launch?
kb_faqs.answer: Critical items: (1) Lead CSV validation and deduplication; (2) Bot prompt review and testing with client on video call; (3) WhatsApp template compliance (utility vs marketing); (4) Wallet top-up verification; (5) Agent dialling strategy alignment (number of attempts, retry timing); (6) DND hour configuration; (7) Connectivity and VoIP setup (IP whitelisting if needed); (8) UAT data collection and conversion analysis; (9) Human handoff process documentation; (10) Client training on dashboard and prompt changes.

kb_faqs.question: How important is real-time prompt configuration review with clients during pilots?
kb_faqs.answer: Extremely critical for trust and adoption. Clients should see dashboard capability during video calls where team shares screen, configures prompts, and explains changes live. This establishes confidence that bot building is easy and reduces vendor comparison friction. In-person sessions (2-3 hours) are more effective than remote calls for complex configuration. Clients who experience good dashboard UX become confident to build bots independently, accelerating contract expansion.

kb_faqs.question: What system prompt patterns work best for sales qualification calls?
kb_faqs.answer: Effective prompts include: (1) Clear persona definition (sales rep, advisor); (2) Explicit instruction to avoid repeating customer names if confidence is low (addresses junk name problem); (3) Multi-turn logic for objection handling; (4) Image analysis trigger when relevant; (5) Soft handoff rules to human agents. Prompts should be tested on both old and new leads separately. Language switching bugs require explicit language lock to prevent mid-call language changes.

kb_faqs.question: What are the most common objections raised by prospects during bot calls?
kb_faqs.answer: Typical objections include: (1) Price/affordability concerns ('too expensive'); (2) Timing objections ('not the right time'); (3) Lack of interest ('not interested'); (4) Need for comparison ('let me think'); (5) Technical issues (poor connectivity, call drops). Effective rebuttals should be personalized to prospect's qualification level. Hot/warm leads warrant more detailed responses; cold leads benefit from bridge-to-human handoff rather than extended objection handling.

kb_faqs.question: What's the expected handoff TAT from bot qualification to human agent follow-up?
kb_faqs.answer: Ideal TAT is same-day or next-morning follow-up on hot/warm leads. When clients delay follow-up (e.g., 2-3 days), conversion rates drop significantly. One pilot showed that delays to follow-up by client's sales team accounted for ~150 of 350 qualified leads not being reached. Clients should commit to following up within 24 hours of bot qualification. Monitor pending follow-ups daily and escalate delays to ensure CX doesn't degrade.

kb_faqs.question: What training do human agents need after receiving bot-qualified leads?
kb_faqs.answer: Agents should be trained on: (1) Lead classification (hot vs warm vs disqualified) and what each status means; (2) Why leads were classified that way (objections raised, interest signals); (3) Recommended follow-up approach for each category; (4) How to handle leads that were already contacted by bot; (5) Data entry standards (updating disposition, notes); (6) How to escalate technical issues (disconnects, call quality). Provide agents with sample call recordings showing bot conversation for context before agent follow-up.

kb_faqs.question: How should dialling strategy be aligned between bot and human agents?
kb_faqs.answer: Establish clear rules upfront: (1) Recommended strategy is 3 dialling attempts on day 1, then 2 per day ongoing; (2) If no answer after configured attempts, drop WhatsApp message; (3) If lead responds to WhatsApp, resume calling; (4) Do not retire leads until all dialling attempts exhausted and no WhatsApp response received; (5) Track attempts separately for bot vs human to avoid duplicate calling. Misalignment here (agent calling once then moving on) causes 20-30% of qualified leads to go un-followed, severely impacting conversion.

kb_faqs.question: What connectivity rates should we expect for different lead types?
kb_faqs.answer: Typical connectivity rates on fresh, attempted leads range from 40-50%. Old leads that have been dialled 2-3+ times in past quarter show much lower connectivity. Connectivity varies by lead source—new direct leads often show 50%+ while re-recycled leads may show 20-30%. Always stratify reporting by lead age and source. High latency (>2 seconds) causes customer frustration and call disconnects; IP whitelisting and regional server selection can improve this.

kb_faqs.question: What technical issues cause call quality problems and how are they fixed?
kb_faqs.answer: Common issues: (1) Calls disconnecting immediately after answer—root cause identified and fix released; (2) High latency (2+ second delay)—often caused by regional VoIP routing or IP whitelisting, resolved via Ubona integration; (3) Customer and agent names confused in system—LLM issue, requires variable confidence filtering; (4) One-way audio—typically IP whitelisting issue like ABSLI case. Implement monitoring dashboards for latency, disconnect rate, and one-way audio incidents. Have escalation path to tech team with lead links for reproduction.

kb_faqs.question: What are the most common bot hallucinations or misclassifications observed in pilots?
kb_faqs.answer: Top issues: (1) Bot reading junk names (ABC, 123, random text) as customer names; (2) Language switching mid-call despite fixed language; (3) Customer name confused with agent name when customer name field is missing; (4) Misclassifying warm leads as cold when objection is soft; (5) Image analysis not triggering when expected due to confidence threshold issues. Fixes require LLM prompt adjustments, variable validation, and explicit instruction to skip unreliable data.

kb_faqs.question: What's the bug fix priority and timeline process during active pilots?
kb_faqs.answer: Critical bugs (blocking campaign launch, causing customer frustration) should have same-day review and 1-2 day fixes. High-priority bugs (degrading conversion but not blocking) get 2-3 day fixes. Medium bugs (minor UX issues, edge cases) get weekly sprints. All bug tickets must include: (1) Lead ID/links for reproduction; (2) Timestamp of issue; (3) Customer impact assessment; (4) Suggested root cause. Tech team should provide timeline estimate within 2 hours of ticket creation.

kb_faqs.question: What feature requests have come from real pilot pain points?
kb_faqs.answer: Top requests: (1) Image analysis per lead reporting—needed to track how many leads shared screenshots; (2) Agent cloning—copy previous agent flows to save config time; (3) Integrated coupon delivery—send promo codes directly without external system; (4) Lead data hygiene tools—detect and clean junk names automatically; (5) Typing indicator immediately on WhatsApp—keep customers engaged while bot processes; (6) Parallel NBA handling—multiple leads in queue simultaneously; (7) Bulk upload queue prioritization—separate queue for large batches to prevent latency.

kb_faqs.question: What workarounds exist for common feature gaps?
kb_faqs.answer: Workarounds: (1) For image analysis tracking—manual report review until column is released; (2) For junk names—instruct bot to address everyone as 'Sir/Ma'am' when name confidence is low (though this reduces personalization); (3) For coupon delivery without integration—bot acknowledges screenshot and customer manually retrieves coupon; (4) For WhatsApp latency—send messages in batches rather than individually to reduce API calls; (5) For duplicate lead messages—manually review and archive accidental uploads in bulk. None are ideal; prioritize feature releases accordingly.

kb_faqs.question: What are the differences between WhatsApp utility and marketing templates?
kb_faqs.answer: Utility templates are transactional (OTP, order confirmation, status updates) and have less restrictive approval/timing rules. Marketing templates are promotional and subject to stricter TRAI guidelines, delivery hour restrictions, and higher scrutiny. Utility templates can be sent 24/7 while marketing templates may have DND hour limits. In pilots, prefer utility templates for lead follow-up (call missed, coupon code) to ensure reliable delivery. Avoid sending 2+ utility templates within 1-minute spans as it wastes message quota and degrades UX.

kb_faqs.question: What WhatsApp delivery rates can we expect and how do we optimize them?
kb_faqs.answer: Typical delivery rates are 85-95% for utility templates sent within 2 minutes of call attempt. Delivery degrades if: (1) Multiple templates sent in quick succession (within 1 minute); (2) Message limit exceeded (typically 3 per campaign); (3) Phone number is blocked/invalid; (4) Lead has opted out of WhatsApp. Optimization: space messages by at least 1-2 minutes, combine related context into single message where possible, respect lead's channel preference, monitor bounce rates daily.

kb_faqs.question: What happens when WhatsApp message limits are exhausted?
kb_faqs.answer: When message quota is reached (typically 2-3 per campaign), system is blocked from sending further WhatsApp messages even if customer qualifies for follow-up. If limit was set too low before campaign launch, warm leads may not receive coupon codes or follow-up messages, degrading conversion and CX. Fix requires updating message limit in campaign settings, but this does NOT retroactively apply to leads uploaded before the change—those leads remain capped at old limit. Mitigation: Always allocate sufficient message quota upfront and test on small batch before full campaign.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What's the NBA (Next Best Action) loop cadence and when does it trigger?
kb_faqs.answer: NBA is triggered when a lead is created or progresses to a new state. The loop initiates call attempts based on configured cadence (typically 3 attempts on day 1, then 2 per day ongoing). Parallel NBA (multiple leads in queue simultaneously) is standard and not a problem. NBA does NOT trigger if: (1) lead is in DND hours; (2) lead has exhausted retry attempts; (3) lead is archived. Monitor 'NBA Generated' field in reports to track qualification rate.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What DND (Do Not Disturb) hours should be configured and how are they enforced?
kb_faqs.answer: Typical DND hours are 9 PM to 7 AM based on local timezone, though this should be customized by region/customer preference. DND rules should prevent: (1) Outbound calls during configured hours; (2) Inbound response delays during DND (message queued but not sent until DND ends). However, if lead is marked as DND but NBA is still scheduled with active AI call channel, system should not attempt calls until DND ends. Monitor for DND override incidents and escalate to tech team.

kb_faqs.question: What's the impact of old/recycled leads vs fresh leads on bot performance?
kb_faqs.answer: Old leads (contacted 2-3+ times in prior quarters with DNP2 disposition) show 20-40% lower connectivity and conversion vs fresh leads. They lack baseline conversion benchmarks because historical agents didn't track conversions on recycled leads. Fresh leads show 50%+ connectivity and higher conversion rates. Always stratify pilots: run separate UAT on old vs fresh; report results separately; only compare conversion benchmarks on fresh leads. Clients should agree not to expect matching performance on old leads.

kb_faqs.question: What causes message sequencing issues and how can they be prevented?
kb_faqs.answer: Issues occur when bot sends multiple messages (especially utility templates) in quick succession (within 1 minute) in response to call events. Example: call disconnects, bot sends 'call missed' message, call reconnects 30 seconds later, bot sends 'status update' message—both in 1 minute. This wastes message quota and confuses customers. Root cause: system doesn't deduplicate message context. Mitigation: (1) Combine related messages; (2) Space messages by 2+ minutes; (3) Add logic to check if message was recently sent before queueing next one. Ticket assigned to tech team for resolution.

kb_faqs.question: What bulk upload issues can occur and how should they be debugged?
kb_faqs.answer: Common issues: (1) Upload shows 'processed' but leads don't appear in campaign after refresh; (2) Leads uploaded to wrong campaign (duplicate campaigns created); (3) Upload stuck in queue for large batches (1,500+ leads). Root causes: latency in queue processing, concurrent uploads to multiple campaigns, database sync delays. Fixes: (1) Add dedicated queue for bulk uploads; (2) Implement upload-to-single-campaign rule; (3) Add user-visible queue status. Debug by checking uploaded sheet against campaign lead count; if mismatch, request sheet from user and verify manually.

kb_faqs.question: How are WhatsApp and voice latency issues typically resolved?
kb_faqs.answer: Latency issues (20-30 second delay between customer message and bot response, 20s+ before typing indicator appears) cause customers to lose interest and abandon conversation. Root causes: API gateway latency, message processing queue delays, regional server routing. Fixes include: (1) Dedicated WhatsApp message queue; (2) Typing indicator shown immediately upon message receipt (not after processing); (3) Regional server selection for low-latency routes; (4) Caching of common responses. Prioritize as P0 since it directly impacts conversion. Target SLA: <2 second end-to-end latency.

kb_faqs.question: What SSO authentication errors occur and how are they resolved?
kb_faqs.answer: Users occasionally see 'Failed to do SSO authentication' errors when logging into Sense dashboard. This is a transient issue that typically resolves within minutes. Workaround: clear browser cache and retry. Root cause may be temporary SSO provider outage or token expiration. Tech team should: (1) Monitor SSO error rate; (2) Implement automatic retry logic; (3) Provide clear error message with resolution steps; (4) Have fallback authentication method. Most issues resolve on second attempt.

kb_faqs.question: How should bot be configured to handle leads with missing or invalid name data?
kb_faqs.answer: Strategy: (1) Add LLM instruction to assess name validity/confidence; (2) If confidence is low, skip personalization and use 'Sir/Ma'am' instead; (3) Never read junk names (ABC, 123, random text) aloud—detect and replace with generic greeting; (4) For blank names, use 'Sir/Ma'am' initially and proactively ask for name during conversation; (5) Log confidence score in system for manual review. This reduces bot hallucinations but requires separate LLM call for validation, adding latency. Consider data hygiene tools (WinPure, Insycle) as longer-term solution.

kb_faqs.question: What role does in-person training play in customer onboarding vs remote sessions?
kb_faqs.answer: In-person training is significantly more effective than remote. Remote 2-hour sessions on dashboard configuration often fail to keep customer focus. In-person sessions (2-3 hours) with hands-on bot building result in: (1) 100% attendance and engagement; (2) Client learns to configure bots independently; (3) Builds trust in product UX; (4) Accelerates contract expansion; (5) Reduces dependency on support team. When client office is geographically accessible, always offer in-person enablement. Expected ROI: customer becomes self-sufficient and advocates for expansion to leadership.

kb_faqs.question: What metrics indicate a successful POC and readiness for contract conversion?
kb_faqs.answer: Readiness indicators: (1) Conversion rate approaching or matching human-only benchmark (typically 3-6% on attempted leads); (2) CPC within 10-15% of human-only cost (INR 49-62 per conversion); (3) Client SPOC actively engaged and requesting feature enhancements; (4) Bot-qualified leads achieving 30%+ hot+warm rate; (5) Human team providing timely follow-up (same-day). If any metric is lagging, do not push for contract—instead, propose extended POC with specific improvements (prompt refinement, dialling strategy alignment, lead quality assessment).

kb_faqs.question: How should conversion numbers be calculated to avoid inflating results?
kb_faqs.answer: Challenges: (1) Distinguishing conversions driven by bot vs human follow-up vs external factors (email, web visit); (2) Image analysis column not yet available in reports, leading to missing conversion data; (3) Old leads lacking historical conversion benchmarks. Best practice: (1) Only count conversions driven by leads first qualified as hot/warm by bot; (2) Wait for image analysis reporting to complete conversion picture; (3) Separately track bot-only conversions vs bot+human conversions; (4) Do NOT include old-lead conversions in KPI calculations; (5) Use conservative 3-6% estimates until data is conclusive. Get agreement on methodology with customer before presenting numbers.

kb_faqs.question: What should be included in a go-live readiness checklist before running full campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: Checklist: (1) Lead CSV deduped and validated (no duplicate phone numbers); (2) Lead quality assessment (% fresh vs old, name fill rate); (3) Bot prompt tested on 50+ sample calls with client observing; (4) WhatsApp templates pre-approved by TRAI guidelines; (5) Agent dialling strategy agreed in writing (number of attempts, timing, retry logic); (6) Connectivity test (calls routing correctly, latency <2s); (7) Human agent team trained on lead classification and handoff process; (8) Monitoring dashboards live (connectivity rate, conversion rate, latency); (9) Escalation contacts defined for each issue type; (10) Day 1 execution dry-run with 50 leads. Do not skip any step.

kb_faqs.question: What's the expected fallback behavior when VoIP provider fails or latency exceeds threshold?
kb_faqs.answer: Current system does not have explicit fallback configured. When latency exceeds ~2 seconds (observed in some regions), calls may disconnect or experience one-way audio. Recommended fallback strategy: (1) Monitor latency SLA (target <1.5s); (2) If latency >2s for >5 consecutive calls, switch to backup VoIP provider; (3) Implement regional routing (route calls to nearest server); (4) Use Ubona integration to call from dedicated TRAI-compliant number (mitigates provider blocking). Work with tech team to implement fallback before go-live.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot persona be defined for different verticals (ed-tech, fintech, e-commerce)?
kb_faqs.answer: Persona should match customer's brand and audience expectations: (1) Ed-tech: friendly, encouraging advisor tone; (2) Fintech: professional, trustworthy, knowledgeable tone; (3) E-commerce: helpful, conversational, deal-focused tone. Core elements: voice selection (matching persona), greeting script, objection response style, handoff script. Test persona on 20+ sample calls with customer feedback before full campaign. Include persona definition in bot system prompt and train agents on persona so handoff conversation is consistent.

kb_faqs.question: What disqualification reasons should be tracked to improve bot performance?
kb_faqs.answer: Track: (1) Price objection (too expensive); (2) Timing objection (not the right time); (3) No interest (not interested in product); (4) Already using competitor; (5) Need comparison (let me think); (6) Technical issue (couldn't hear); (7) No answer/callback failed; (8) Invalid phone number. Analyze disqualifications by reason—if >40% are price objections, refine bot's value prop message. If >30% are 'no answer', review dialling time and lead quality. Share disqualification analysis with client to improve bot flow and identify leads for human follow-up.

kb_faqs.question: What customer feedback or signs indicate bot quality needs improvement mid-campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: Red flags: (1) Agent reports customer complaints about bot (mispronunciation, repetitive, robotic); (2) Disqualification rate >40% suggesting poor objection handling; (3) Connectivity rate <30% suggesting VoIP issue; (4) No conversions from bot-qualified warm leads after 48 hours suggesting issue with lead quality or handoff; (5) Customer requests frequent bot flow changes (sign of poor initial design); (6) WhatsApp delivery rate <80% suggesting template or compliance issue. When red flags appear, pause campaign, convene design review call with customer, and test improvements on 50-100 leads before resuming.

kb_faqs.question: How should voice selection be approached and what voices get the best customer feedback?
kb_faqs.answer: Current voice library is being consolidated to remove duplicates. Customer feedback indicates preference for voices that sound natural, friendly, and professional (avoid overly robotic tones). Request from customers: more female voice options and region-specific accents. Before launching campaign, test 3-4 voice options on 10-20 sample calls and ask customer which sounds most authentic to their brand. Document which voices get positive feedback and avoid others in future. Plan to expand library based on customer demand after consolidation complete.

kb_faqs.question: What causes bot to switch languages mid-call and how is it fixed?
kb_faqs.answer: Bug observed in Frido, Miles, and Artium campaigns: bot switches from intended language (e.g., English) to another language (e.g., Hindi) during middle of call. Root cause: language detection in LLM prompt is not locked to configured language. Fix: (1) Add explicit system instruction 'Respond ONLY in [LANGUAGE] regardless of customer input'; (2) Lock language parameter at bot initialization; (3) Monitor for language switches in call recordings. This is P0 for multi-language campaigns. Current workaround: restart call. Permanent fix requires LLM prompt hardening.

kb_faqs.question: What's the process for re-running LLM prompt changes and what errors occur?
kb_faqs.answer: When updating bot prompt mid-campaign, system should re-run LLM on all existing leads to apply new logic. Observed issue: 'Checking every status every 2 seconds' hangs indefinitely. Root cause: queue processing stuck, likely due to long-running LLM inference or database lock. Fix: (1) Implement timeout for prompt re-run (max 5 minutes); (2) Add job queue status visibility to dashboard; (3) Allow user to cancel stuck jobs; (4) Add logs showing which leads were re-processed. For now, workaround is restart browser. Never do prompt re-run on campaigns with >1000 leads without tech team approval.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What validation rules should be applied to lead uploads to prevent bad data?
kb_faqs.answer: Validation rules: (1) Phone number must be 10 digits and unique (not already in system); (2) Phone number must not be in archive/marked as DND; (3) Name field (if required) must have minimum 2 characters; (4) Email must match valid format; (5) Custom fields must match schema (no extra/missing columns). Error messages should be specific (e.g., 'Lead ID 123 has duplicate phone number already in campaign ABC'). Allow users to download error report and reupload corrected file. Warn user if >10% of leads fail validation before allowing upload.

kb_faqs.question: How should customer name and agent name confusion be handled in system?
kb_faqs.answer: Bug observed in Handpickd: when customer name field is missing, system substitutes agent name (e.g., 'Priya') into customer reference, causing bot to say 'Hi Priya, I'm calling on behalf of Priya' (confusing to actual customer). Root cause: no validation before variable substitution. Fix: (1) LLM should check if substituted name matches known agent names and skip if match found; (2) If customer name is unavailable, use 'Sir/Ma'am' instead of agent name; (3) Require customer name field to be present at upload. Workaround: manually review before campaign and add missing names. Add this to data validation rules.

kb_faqs.question: What's the expected booking/session conversion rate from qualified warm leads?
kb_faqs.answer: Warm leads (showing interest in session/product) typically convert to bookings at 5-15% rate depending on follow-up timing and quality. In one campaign, 28 sessions were booked from bot-qualified leads, with 2 conducted and 1 expected closure. Of 3 bookings completed, 2 failed due to external factors (loan sanction issue). Expectations: 5-10% of warm leads will result in confirmed bookings; 50-70% of bookings will convert to final sale (depending on deal structure). Factor in 2-3 week post-booking cycle for closures.

kb_faqs.question: How should campaign expansion be sequenced after successful POC?
kb_faqs.answer: Typical sequence: (1) Initial POC on 200-500 leads with full metrics collection; (2) Review results and refine bot/process; (3) Second campaign on 500-1000 fresh leads; (4) Third campaign on 1000+ leads with minimal changes. Between campaigns, do not launch new campaigns without getting client feedback and internal alignment. Expansion approval should require: (1) Conversion rate confirmed with latest data; (2) CPC calculation including all costs; (3) Client's agent readiness (trained on handoff); (4) No critical bugs blocking launch. Typical timeline: 3-5 days between campaigns to collect data and plan next iteration.

kb_faqs.question: When should image/document analysis be triggered vs skipped in bot flow?
kb_faqs.answer: Image analysis should be triggered when: (1) Customer proactively shares screenshot or document; (2) Bot explicitly asks for document (e.g., 'Can you share your ID?'); (3) Document is relevant to qualification (e.g., coupon screenshot, bill image). Skip if: (1) Image is not related to conversation (e.g., meme, random photo); (2) Image quality is too poor to analyze; (3) Customer has not consented. Configure confidence threshold so only clearly relevant images are analyzed. In some campaigns, 81 images analyzed per 1000 leads, so ~8% of leads share relevant documents. Track 'images analysed per lead' column in reports.

kb_faqs.question: What should happen during internal kickoff meeting for new pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: Internal kickoff (before customer kickoff) should cover: (1) Customer profile (vertical, size, KPIs); (2) Campaign goals (qualification rate target, conversion target, CPC target); (3) Lead data review (quality assessment, sizing, dedup check); (4) Bot flow design (persona, objections, handoff script); (5) Dialling strategy (attempts per day, retry windows); (6) WhatsApp strategy (templates, timing); (7) Metrics definition and reporting cadence; (8) Escalation contacts and SLA; (9) Timeline (UAT, go-live, reviews); (10) Risk mitigation (fallbacks, contingency plans). Assign SPOC for each domain (sales, support, implementation, product).

kb_faqs.question: What causes incorrect TTS pronunciation of English words like 'do' across voices?
kb_faqs.answer: The issue was a configuration problem affecting all TTS voices, where English words were being pronounced using Hindi phonetics. The fix involved ensuring the language preference was explicitly set to English in the system configuration. Once the language setting was corrected, pronunciation worked as expected across all tested voices.

kb_faqs.question: How do I fix TTS pronunciation issues when replacing problematic words creates new issues?
kb_faqs.answer: Simply replacing individual words (e.g., 'do' with 'डू') doesn't work because the system still applies Hindi phonetics to adjacent words like 'does' and 'don't'. The root cause is a configuration-level language setting. Verify the preferred language is set to English at the campaign level, not at individual word replacements.

kb_faqs.question: Why is the Lead Interest Prompt changing automatically to the default prompt?
kb_faqs.answer: Custom prompts were being overwritten back to default prompts in active campaigns. This was reported affecting multiple concurrent campaigns. Users should verify their custom Lead Interest Prompts after configuration and flag immediately if they revert to default, as this impacts bot behavior and qualification accuracy.

kb_faqs.question: How should we approach testing bot scripts with substantial content changes?
kb_faqs.answer: When scripts have significant changes (context, rapport-building, final goals), run parallel tests: keep the original lead set for baseline metrics and test script variations on fresh leads separately. For example, run 3,000 fresh leads on original script and 2,000 on new script to isolate the impact of script changes on performance metrics.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What lead quality issues should we document during qualification analysis?
kb_faqs.answer: Track disqualification reasons granularly. Example: Out of 20 leads, document how many were rejected due to laptop unavailability, English language proficiency, no-answer, or other factors. This data helps negotiate lead criteria changes with clients—e.g., if 7 of 10 were rejected only for laptop unavailability, client may update policy to accept phone-based attendance.

kb_faqs.question: How should human agent dialling strategy be documented before lead handoff?
kb_faqs.answer: Create a documented SOP specifying: minimum number of calling attempts (typically 3 per day), use of WhatsApp as follow-up channel, tracking mechanism for connectivity, and device/phone requirements. This should be finalized and shared with all stakeholders (delivery manager, client champion, floor execution teams) before agents start calling qualified leads.

kb_faqs.question: What connectivity tracking issues arise when agents use personal devices for dialling?
kb_faqs.answer: When human agents dial from personal phones instead of tracked systems, it becomes difficult to monitor connectivity rates, call attempts, and outcomes. While agents may still complete 3 daily attempts with WhatsApp follow-up, the lack of centralized tracking makes it impossible to verify metrics or optimize the process. Implement a mandatory dialling system to ensure visibility.

kb_faqs.question: Why are clients reporting voices sound too robotic?
kb_faqs.answer: Earlier TTS voices were perceived as robotic by clients. The solution involved testing newer voice options and cloning voices that performed well. If a client preferred a specific voice (e.g., 'Anika' on ElevenLabs), create an instant clone on newer platforms (e.g., Cartesia) and test the clone alongside newer voice options to improve natural sound quality.

kb_faqs.question: Can we assign different phone numbers to different campaigns within the same WABA?
kb_faqs.answer: Yes. Once a new number is added to the WABA integration, select the specific number in campaign settings and save. The number will then appear in the campaign dropdown menu, allowing different campaigns to use different sending numbers from the same WABA account.

kb_faqs.question: What image/attachment capability is needed in the WhatsApp human agent flow?
kb_faqs.answer: Human agents need the ability to send images and attachments (e.g., confirmation screenshots, receipts) within the WhatsApp flow directly from the platform. This drives adoption of in-platform messaging versus agents defaulting to external tools. Implement an attachment upload feature in the human WhatsApp interface to enable this use case.

kb_faqs.question: What causes WhatsApp health to drop to RED and campaign suspension?
kb_faqs.answer: WhatsApp health degrades due to payment issues, template violations, or high complaint rates. When health status drops to RED, Meta suspends message delivery (typically for 24-72 hours). Prevention requires ensuring wallet top-up is complete before campaign launch and using compliant templates. Monitor health status daily during active campaigns to catch issues early.

kb_faqs.question: What is the typical WhatsApp response rate when messages are delivered successfully?
kb_faqs.answer: Response rates vary significantly based on health status and delivery windows. In campaigns where WhatsApp was paused early due to health issues, only 1% of qualified leads responded on WhatsApp (e.g., 3 responses out of 300 qualified leads). When WhatsApp is fully operational, response rates typically range from 5-10% of delivered messages.

kb_faqs.question: How much of campaign cost is typically attributable to WhatsApp versus voice calls?
kb_faqs.answer: In mixed-channel campaigns, voice calls typically represent 85-95% of total campaign cost, while WhatsApp represents only 1-5%. Example: Total cost of ₹12,240 with WhatsApp costing ₹204 (1.6%). If WhatsApp has health issues and is disabled early, cost allocation shifts almost entirely to voice calls.

kb_faqs.question: What is the connection rate impact when WhatsApp is unavailable during a campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: When WhatsApp is paused due to health issues early in the campaign, overall connectivity metrics remain driven by voice channel only. The combined effect (calls + WhatsApp attempted contacts) cannot be measured. In one case, 1.5% of leads connected via phone when 3-4 WhatsApp messages were also sent, but WhatsApp health issues prevented measuring the true multi-channel impact.

kb_faqs.question: How do I calculate accurate campaign ROI when cost data sources show different values?
kb_faqs.answer: Multiple data sources (campaign analytics dashboard, billing section, API exports) may show conflicting campaign costs, sometimes varying by 4.5x. Until consolidated, use only the Campaign Spend tab in the analytics dashboard as the single source of truth. Avoid manually pulling data from APIs or billing sections, as inconsistencies corrupt ROI calculations. Request IT to disable competing cost APIs.

kb_faqs.question: Why does cost calculation differ when using AI call pulses versus AI call minutes?
kb_faqs.answer: The API was updated to report pulses instead of minutes, but cost multipliers weren't adjusted. Previously, minutes were multiplied by 1x, but pulses should be multiplied by approximately 4x (average voice bot cost per pulse). Using the old 1x multiplier on pulse data underestimates costs by 4x. Update all cost calculation logic to use the correct pulse-to-cost multiplier.

kb_faqs.question: What cost items are incorrectly included in campaign spend calculations?
kb_faqs.answer: Current calculations include costs for voicemail messages, failed/undelivered WhatsApp messages, and failed image sends. These should be excluded from billable campaign cost since no service was delivered. Removing failed message costs can reduce total campaign cost by ₹1,800+ per campaign. Work with product to filter out non-billable cost items in the spend dashboard.

kb_faqs.question: What pre-campaign checklist items ensure smooth WhatsApp deployment?
kb_faqs.answer: Before launching, verify: (1) wallet has sufficient top-up balance, (2) WhatsApp templates are approved and compliant, (3) WABA account is healthy and in good standing with Meta, (4) payment method is current, (5) assign the correct phone number to the campaign. Test with a small batch (50 leads) and monitor WhatsApp health status for 24 hours before scaling to full campaign.

kb_faqs.question: How do I compare cost-per-connection and conversion rates between voice-only and multi-channel campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Isolate metrics by channel: voice-only campaigns show baseline CPC and conversion rates. For multi-channel, track separately—calls only, WhatsApp only, and both channels. Example: VB-only CPC ₹159 on 1,000 leads; multi-channel CPC ₹156 on 1,000 leads (slightly better despite higher total spend) because connectivity improved 1.49% → 5.5%, offsetting cost increases.

kb_faqs.question: What is a realistic conversion rate benchmark for human agents in sales qualification?
kb_faqs.answer: Human agent conversion rates vary by use case. Typical ranges: 2.5-5.5% on fresh leads, 25%+ on warm/rejoiner leads. Example: A client reported 60% conversion on rejoiner leads but revealed this wasn't the average—only top agents achieved this. Request average agent performance data, not best-performer data, to set realistic benchmarks.

kb_faqs.question: How should qualified leads be categorized before handoff to human agents?
kb_faqs.answer: Categorize into: (1) Hot leads—ready for immediate agent follow-up, (2) Warm leads—qualified but may need re-engagement, (3) Rejoiner/existing customer leads—previously engaged but not converted. Each category has different expected conversion rates and agent approach. Mixing lead categories in analysis prevents accurate performance comparison.

kb_faqs.question: Why might conversion rates improve on weekends vs weekdays?
kb_faqs.answer: Conversion trends may vary by day-of-week due to customer availability and mindset. Example: A client observed conversions tend to improve on Saturdays/Sundays. Recommend scheduling campaigns to prioritize high-performing days and adjust agent staffing accordingly. Segment campaign results by day-of-week to identify and capitalize on peak conversion windows.

kb_faqs.question: When should internal kick-off happen relative to MSA signing?
kb_faqs.answer: Start the internal kick-off process immediately when the deal appears to be closing, not after MSA is signed. This allows 5 working days for Knowledge Transfer (KT) before the formal client kick-off meeting. After KT completion, the PM should email the CSM team for approval before scheduling the client kick-off, ensuring proper project setup and faster campaign launch.

kb_faqs.question: What information should be included in the KT handoff document to CSM?
kb_faqs.answer: KT handoff should include: (1) pilot deck with campaign objectives and results, (2) complete KT documentation covering bot configuration, lead data, qualification criteria, (3) PM summary of all setup decisions, (4) client context (business goals, success metrics). CSM must approve KT completion before client kick-off to ensure continuity and prevent rework.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot respond when a prospect says 'not interested' multiple times?
kb_faqs.answer: The bot should respect repeated disinterest signals and immediately disengage. Common failure: bots continue persuading after 2-3 'not interested' responses, appearing aggressive. Fix: Add a hard, non-negotiable directive in the bot's system prompt (Pillar 2) instructing immediate closure after multiple 'not interested' signals. Test this behavior before deployment to ensure consistent execution.

kb_faqs.question: What causes the bot to restart conversation after silence?
kb_faqs.answer: When the bot goes silent (network delay, processing pause), it sometimes restarts the conversation with 'Hello' instead of closing gracefully. Fix: Add explicit prompt instruction to prevent conversation restart and enforce direct closure if silence exceeds threshold. Use AACC (Automated Agent Call Conversion) methodology to identify and fix these edge cases before production.

kb_faqs.question: What system prompt structure improves bot tone for sales qualification?
kb_faqs.answer: The 8-Pillar framework structures bot behavior: Pillar 1 = persona/tone, Pillar 2 = call mindset (respect disinterest, close decisively), Pillar 3 = conversation flow (context, rapport), Pillar 4+ = handling objections, edge cases. Using this structure with explicit, hard directives (vs soft suggestions) improves execution reliability. Test framework updates on QA samples before full deployment.

kb_faqs.question: Why do Indian language words get mispronounced (e.g., 'मैं' as 'मैन')?
kb_faqs.answer: This is a consistent TTS issue across Cartesia voices where certain Hindi phonemes are mispronounced. Example: 'मैं' (main) is pronounced as 'मैन' (man). This affects script quality and prospect perception. Currently flagged as known issue. Workaround: Use phonetic spelling alternatives or test alternative voices. Escalate to Cartesia for phoneme-specific fixes.

kb_faqs.question: What causes STT errors that impact bot conversation context?
kb_faqs.answer: STT (speech-to-text) misrecognitions include: 'slimming' heard as 'swimming', 'cancel' as 'cancer'. These errors cascade into wrong context being passed to the LLM, causing hallucinations and incorrect bot responses. Mitigate by: (1) adding confirmation logic for high-risk words, (2) tuning STT model for industry vocabulary, (3) adding fallback questions when confidence is low.

kb_faqs.question: Why does the bot use aggressive tone that's unsuitable for sales qualification?
kb_faqs.answer: Using collections/aggressive voices or fast-paced delivery makes the bot unsuitable for sales qualification. Prospects perceive it as pushy, reducing conversion. Solution: (1) Switch to newer, softer voices (avoid 'Shristi v3' for sales—it's collections-focused), (2) Slow down delivery pace, (3) Add pauses for natural rhythm, (4) Include rapport-building phrases. Test voice swaps on small batches before full rollout.

kb_faqs.question: How should bot script be updated when client offer terms change?
kb_faqs.answer: When client changes offer (e.g., ₹100 → ₹200 price point, new cashback terms), redesign the bot prompt to reflect new offer clearly and accurately. Test the updated prompt on 50 leads before full deployment. Include specific offer details (amount, applicability, validity) to avoid miscommunication. Alert client that new offer requires script update and validation.

kb_faqs.question: What testing approach should be used before campaign launch?
kb_faqs.answer: Run 2 UAT phases: UAT-1 and UAT-2 with 50 leads each to validate bot behavior, script accuracy, integration, and lead handoff. After UAT approval, launch Campaign-1 with fresh leads (not UAT leads). This separates validation (UAT) from measurement (Campaign). Do not reuse UAT leads in measurement campaigns as it skews conversion and connectivity metrics.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What happens when a campaign uses mixed lead age (fresh vs 3-month vs 6-month old)?
kb_faqs.answer: Mixed lead age makes it impossible to measure true bot performance because conversion rates differ dramatically by lead freshness. Fresh leads typically have 2-3x lower conversion than 6-month-old leads. Requires explicit categorization: only use leads of consistent age for a single campaign. If client provides mixed ages, segment analysis by lead age or restart with homogeneous lead sets.

kb_faqs.question: How should we handle clients sharing existing customer leads alongside fresh leads?
kb_faqs.answer: Existing customers (already enrolled in programs) should not be mixed with fresh prospects in the same campaign. When identified, segregate them and run separately. The bot is optimized for new customer acquisition, not retention/upsell. If client includes existing customers, expect higher conversion artificially, making bot performance evaluation invalid. Request data source clarity before campaign launch.

kb_faqs.question: How many leads are typically consumed during UAT before a main campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: Budget 50 leads for UAT-1 and 50 leads for UAT-2, totaling 100 leads. These leads are used for validation only and should not be mixed into main campaign measurement. Example: If a client provides 1,000 leads, allocate 100 for UAT and 900 for measurement campaigns. After UAT-1 and UAT-2 validation, fresh leads should be used for the actual campaign.

kb_faqs.question: Can different leads in a bulk upload have different system prompts or bot instructions?
kb_faqs.answer: Current system applies one system prompt to all leads in a bulk upload. A feature request exists to enable different system messages per lead based on lead attributes (e.g., different script for premium vs standard customers). Until implemented, workaround: upload leads in separate batches and assign different campaigns with different prompts.

kb_faqs.question: What filtering capability is needed in the campaign dashboard?
kb_faqs.answer: Sales teams need entity-based filtering directly in the analytics dashboard (e.g., filter by customer segment, product type, geography). Currently, filtering requires manual data export. Implementing dashboard-level entity filters would allow faster analysis without exporting reports, improving decision speed.

kb_faqs.question: How should cost optimization be approached when multi-channel cost exceeds voice-only?
kb_faqs.answer: If multi-channel CPC increases vs voice-only, analyze the value gained: (1) Did connectivity improve? (2) Did conversion rate increase? (3) What is the net CPA impact? If connectivity improved 3.7x but cost increased 3x, CPA may still improve if conversion also improved proportionally. Optimize by: testing WhatsApp-first (before calls), reducing call frequency, or adjusting message templates for better engagement.

kb_faqs.question: Should we start with WhatsApp-only or multi-channel in campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: A proposed optimization: test WhatsApp-first engagement (attempt full conversion via messages), then escalate to calls only for non-responders. This may reduce cost if WhatsApp achieves meaningful conversions. However, current data shows WhatsApp alone converts only ~1% of leads in most verticals. Recommend: Use multi-channel with cost optimization focus (better targeting, template quality) rather than channel sequencing.

kb_faqs.question: Why is NBA (Next Best Action) not generating for campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: NBA generation stopped for ongoing campaigns (reported outage of 2+ days). This prevents the system from identifying which leads should be contacted next and through which channel. Root cause: backend service failure or queue backlog. Escalate to tech team immediately if NBA is not generating, as it blocks campaign progression and lead distribution.

kb_faqs.question: Why are bulk lead uploads stuck in queue?
kb_faqs.answer: Lead uploads can get stuck in processing queue for 30+ minutes, preventing campaigns from launching on schedule. Causes: backend job queue backlog, integration timeout, or file validation issue. Workaround: Break large uploads into smaller batches (500-1000 leads). If stuck, contact tech support to manually trigger queue processing or restart the upload.

kb_faqs.question: Why does the Lead Outcome Report show '0 Replies on WhatsApp' when replies exist?
kb_faqs.answer: Discrepancies between actual WhatsApp replies and reported metrics indicate a data sync issue between the WhatsApp integration and reporting dashboard. This corrupts conversion and engagement metrics. Escalate to tech team to audit the WhatsApp-to-reporting pipeline. Do not rely on reported WhatsApp metrics until validated.

kb_faqs.question: Why does call duration in reports not match actual call length?
kb_faqs.answer: Call duration mismatches (e.g., reported 32 seconds vs actual 19 seconds) indicate a timestamp or rounding error in the reporting system. This inflates CPC calculations if duration-based pricing is used. Validate actual call recordings against reported durations. Escalate to tech to audit duration calculation logic.

kb_faqs.question: What causes goal completion misclassification in the lead outcome?
kb_faqs.answer: Leads are marked as 'Completed' even when campaign goals are not achieved. This indicates the goal-tracking logic is not properly connected to lead status updates. Example: Lead shows as 'Completed' but did not book a demo as required. Fix: Audit goal-definition configuration and ensure lead status only advances when actual goal criteria are met, not on default progression.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What is the expected NBA cadence for multi-channel campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: NBA (Next Best Action) should generate daily to identify: which leads to contact, through which channel (call vs WhatsApp), and at what time. Standard cadence: daily NBA generation run overnight, with generated actions executed the following day. If NBA generation fails, campaigns stall and leads are not progressed. Critical for multi-channel campaigns to function.

kb_faqs.question: What metrics indicate poor bot performance that requires prompt redesign?
kb_faqs.answer: Red flags: (1) Connectivity <50%, (2) Qualification rate <20%, (3) High hang-up rate (>30%), (4) Prospect complaints about tone/pace, (5) Conversion <5% of connected leads. When multiple flags appear, conduct conversation audit—sample 20 call recordings and analyze script delivery, tone, objection handling. Redesign prompt focusing on pain points identified.

kb_faqs.question: What is the cost calculation formula for multi-channel campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Total cost = (AI call pulses × ₹1-4 per pulse) + (WhatsApp messages × ₹0.5) + (Image sends × ₹0.75) + (WABA account cost). Note: Cost multiplier varies by tier (₹1.1 to ₹4+ per pulse). Failed messages should not be billed. Marketing templates have lower delivery rates (~50%) but incur LLM generation costs on all attempted sends. Use Campaign Spend dashboard for definitive cost.

kb_faqs.question: What causes WABA accounts to get blocked by Meta?
kb_faqs.answer: WABA accounts are blocked (typically for 72 hours) due to: sending unapproved templates, high complaint/block rates from users, policy violations, or account compromise. Health status is shown in platform. When blocked, no messages can be sent. Prevention: use only approved templates, monitor quality metrics, maintain compliant messaging practices. During outage, campaigns cannot leverage WhatsApp—shift to voice-only temporarily.

kb_faqs.question: How should we approach cost optimization when WhatsApp delivers only 1% conversion?
kb_faqs.answer: If WhatsApp engagement is <1% of qualified leads, revisit strategy: (1) Are templates optimized? (2) Are messages being sent at right time? (3) Is the offer compelling? (4) What is the cost-per-responder on WhatsApp? If even after optimization conversion remains <1%, consider reducing WhatsApp to utility-only (confirmations, follow-ups) and rely on calls for main engagement. Cost per conversion may be lower with calls alone.

kb_faqs.question: What is the expected attempt-wise connectivity pattern for campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Typical pattern: 1st attempt 60-70% connectivity, 2nd attempt 20-30%, 3rd attempt 5-15%. DND hours should be respected (e.g., 9 PM-8 AM). If WhatsApp is paused early in campaign, average attempts per lead drops to 1-2, significantly impacting overall connectivity. Monitor connection rate trend; if it drops below 40% on repeat attempts, consider longer retry window or timing optimization.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What is the optimal retry window for lead re-contact?
kb_faqs.answer: Standard retry logic: attempt contact 3 times within a day, respecting DND hours (9 PM-8 AM for India). Space attempts 3-4 hours apart. After no-answer on day 1, retry on day 2-3 before marking unconnected. If using multi-channel, WhatsApp attempt should precede call (lower cost, higher conversion per attempt) or follow same-day for reinforcement. Adjust based on vertical and lead behavior.

kb_faqs.question: What is the most common objection raised by prospects in sales qualification calls?
kb_faqs.answer: From chat context, top objections include: 'Not interested', 'Busy right now', 'Call me later'. Less common but important: 'No laptop/device to attend demo', 'English proficiency concerns'. Bot should handle 'not interested' with graceful closure, not persistence. For time-based objections, offer callback scheduling. For capability objections (device, language), clarify options with prospect upfront.

kb_faqs.question: When should a prospect be marked as 'converted' vs 'qualified'?
kb_faqs.answer: Qualified = met lead criteria (interest, budget, timeline), agreed to next step (e.g., demo). Converted = completed the agreed next step (attended demo, scheduled meeting, made purchase). These are different metrics; qualification rate should be tracked separately from conversion rate. Example: 300 qualified leads, 10 converted to meetings (3.3% conversion-to-meeting rate).

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What makes a high-quality lead CSV for campaign launch?
kb_faqs.answer: Good CSV: (1) Consistent lead age (all fresh or all 6-month old), (2) Valid phone numbers, (3) No duplicates, (4) Complete name/company fields, (5) No existing customers mixed in, (6) Clear source tracking (how/when acquired), (7) <5% invalid contacts. Bad CSV: mixed ages, duplicate entries, existing customers, invalid numbers, poor formatting. Validate before upload.

kb_faqs.question: How should we track and report conversion rates to clients?
kb_faqs.answer: Clearly define conversion: booked appointment, demo attended, or purchase made? Different clients may use different definitions. Example: One client defined conversion as 'appointment booked', another as 'meeting attended'. Align definition upfront in kick-off and report against that single definition consistently. Include in reporting: conversion rate (%), cost-per-conversion, and conversion-to-pipeline-value for ROI.

kb_faqs.question: Why is the analytics spend dashboard showing different costs than the API?
kb_faqs.answer: Multiple sources of cost data exist: Campaign Analytics Spend tab, Billing section (campaign cost view), and legacy API. They show different values (up to 4.5x difference) due to: different cost calculation formulas, inclusion/exclusion of failed messages, different timestamp handling, and tier-based pricing differences. Use Campaign Analytics Spend tab as single source of truth. Disable API cost access to prevent confusion.

kb_faqs.question: How can we reduce LLM credit consumption for marketing templates?
kb_faqs.answer: Currently, LLM generates personalized marketing messages for all 100% of recipients, but only ~50% are successfully delivered (due to Meta restrictions). This wastes 50% of LLM credits. Optimization: pre-build marketing templates without LLM generation (use simple variable substitution like {{name}}, {{offer}}) and reserve LLM for utility-critical messages. This reduces cost by 50% while maintaining message quality.

kb_faqs.question: Should clients be charged for WhatsApp messages that failed to deliver?
kb_faqs.answer: No. Clients should not pay for undelivered messages or messages that failed due to Meta restrictions (health issues, policy blocks). Currently, failed messages are sometimes included in cost calculations. Fix: explicitly exclude non-delivery failures from billable cost. If client received no value (message unsent), no charge. This improves trust and aligns pricing with delivery.

kb_faqs.question: What should be confirmed with the client during campaign kickoff?
kb_faqs.answer: Kickoff confirmation checklist: (1) Lead source and quality, (2) Conversion definition (what counts as success?), (3) Offer terms and any recent changes, (4) Human agent dialling SOP, (5) Success metrics and targets, (6) Timeline and phases, (7) Reporting cadence, (8) Escalation path for issues. Document all confirmations and send to client for explicit sign-off before campaign launch.

kb_faqs.question: How long should a client test the bot before UAT approval?
kb_faqs.answer: Avoid excessive testing loops. Standard approach: 1-2 internal calls to verify functionality, then move to UAT-1 (50 real leads). If client wants extended testing beyond 15-minute calls each, propose formal UAT with 50 leads and clear test cases instead. Extended ad-hoc testing delays campaign launch without additional learning. Formalize testing to 2 x 50-lead UAT phases then launch.

kb_faqs.question: What should be included in a campaign performance summary email to clients?
kb_faqs.answer: Include: (1) Total leads contacted, (2) Connectivity rate (%), (3) Qualification rate (%), (4) Conversions (#), (5) Conversion rate (%), (6) Cost summary (total, per lead, per conversion), (7) Comparison to client's stated baseline/goal, (8) Key insights/recommendations, (9) Next phase recommendation. Provide segment-wise breakdown if applicable (by geography, offer, attempt #).

kb_faqs.question: What percentage of leads typically engage with the voice bot across a campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: Connectivity rates vary significantly by campaign quality. In one pilot, approximately 1,700 out of 2,400 leads (71%) did not respond in any way, while 467 leads connected on call at least once. Of those connected leads, roughly 57% (264 out of 467) were classified as hot or warm prospects. This suggests bot engagement depends heavily on lead quality and outreach strategy rather than platform limitations.

kb_faqs.question: What connectivity issues have been observed in recent campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Low connectivity rates have been noted, with approximately 71% of leads showing no response. Additionally, a critical technical issue was identified where the bot remained silent for ~30 seconds upon call pickup from the customer's perspective, though bot audio was present in recordings. This creates a perception of dropped calls despite technical functionality. The root cause was traced to telephony provider (Exotel) issues and requires ongoing coordination to resolve.

kb_faqs.question: What is the average conversation duration with the bot for review-based campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Analysis of real campaigns shows that 41 leads had conversations exceeding 5 minutes with the bot. For review completion flows, optimal duration is approximately 3 minutes of talk time and fewer than 10 WhatsApp messages. Conversations longer than this typically indicate either customer dissatisfaction being discussed extensively or post-review support queries (coupon codes, product issues) that could be handled by human agents instead.

kb_faqs.question: How should hot, warm, and cold leads be classified based on interaction length?
kb_faqs.answer: Current classification logic has been flagged as inaccurate. Leads with only 15–20 seconds of interaction are often classified as warm, but clients report this does not reflect genuine intent. The system may be over-classifying leads based solely on connection vs. disconnection. Recommendations include: (1) moving intent classification to entity-based extraction rather than lead scoring, (2) improving the system prompt for interest detection, and (3) conducting accuracy audits before using lead scoring in future pilots.

kb_faqs.question: What are the main objection categories and how should the bot handle them?
kb_faqs.answer: Key objections include: (1) Customers asking about coupon codes/post-purchase benefits – should be handled by human agents rather than extended bot conversations; (2) Product dissatisfaction – bot should redirect to human support immediately instead of continuing pitch; (3) Already decided to post review – bot should close the conversation and flag for human follow-up rather than seeking confirmation repeatedly. For dissatisfied customers, the recommended response is: 'Let me raise this with my senior and they will get back to you,' followed by immediate human handoff.

kb_faqs.question: What is the cost per acquisition (CPA) difference between bot-only and human-assisted models?
kb_faqs.answer: In one pilot, human agent CPC was approximately ₹49 (₹32,000 monthly salary / leads generated), but when including external costs, the true human agent cost rose to ~₹50k monthly, effectively making CPC ₹90. The bot's CPC varied by campaign: ₹29 in one case with ₹88 rounded, and ₹158 in another pilot. However, bot CPC can be optimized significantly by reducing spend on dissatisfied/not-interested leads (currently consuming ₹3.4k per lead vs. ₹2.2k for hot leads).

kb_faqs.question: How should ROI calculations account for all costs in a bot campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: A critical finding was that Lead Management Fees (LMF) were not being added to total campaign cost calculations, artificially inflating ROI figures. The correct ROI calculation must include: (1) bot platform costs, (2) telephony costs, (3) lead procurement costs, (4) human agent follow-up costs (including external staffing), and (5) any additional third-party fees. Failure to include all costs creates misalignment between expected and actual performance metrics.

kb_faqs.question: What bot response characteristics reduce call duration and cost?
kb_faqs.answer: Analysis of long-duration calls revealed that bot responses are often overly verbose, with detailed explanations provided even for simple customer queries. Recommended changes: (1) use shorter, more conversational responses without repeating lengthy sentences, (2) avoid asking for confirmation when customer has already agreed to an action (reduces follow-up loops), and (3) implement early exit for not-interested customers instead of continuing pitch. These changes improved human-likeness while materially reducing call duration and operational cost.

kb_faqs.question: What is the agent cloning bug and how is it resolved?
kb_faqs.answer: When cloning an existing AI call agent, the AI call integration fails to attach to the new agent. Workaround: Create a new agent from scratch using the same settings as the existing agent instead of using the clone function. This issue has been logged as AC-1225 and requires a product fix to automatically transfer integrations during the clone process.

kb_faqs.question: What is the 24-hour rolling window limit issue in WhatsApp campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: Meta (WhatsApp Business API) enforces a limit on the number of ongoing conversations per WABA (WhatsApp Business Account) based on a 24-hour opt-in rolling window. Ongoing conversation = number of leads who have interacted within the last 24 hours. When this limit is reached, no new conversations can be initiated until older ones expire. This is configured in the WABA settings page under '24 hour rolling window limit' and requires coordination with the Meta admin to increase the limit based on campaign scale.

kb_faqs.question: What is the issue with numbers being marked as DND incorrectly?
kb_faqs.answer: Some numbers are incorrectly marked as DND (Do Not Disturb) by the telephony provider (Exotel) even though they are not actually registered on telecom DND lists. This blocks outbound calling to those numbers. Resolution: Contact the telephony provider's support (hello@exotel.in) and request removal from the DND list, mentioning the account SID. Allow 24–48 hours for the change to take effect in their system.

kb_faqs.question: What is the audio sync issue where bot audio is present in recording but not heard by customer?
kb_faqs.answer: In rare cases, customers report no audio from the bot for ~30 seconds upon call pickup despite maximum volume, yet bot audio is clearly present in call recordings. This indicates a telephony provider (Exotel) issue with audio stream initialization rather than a platform bug. The issue may resolve on its own after a few calls. Root cause investigation is ongoing with the provider, and a workaround is to retry the call if this occurs.

kb_faqs.question: What is the NBA (Next Best Action) generation lag issue?
kb_faqs.answer: In one campaign, call trigger delays of 1–2 minutes were observed before the bot initiated the call. Additionally, NBA was not generated on subsequent call attempts for the same lead.
