This has been logged as a bug (AC-1262) and requires investigation into the backend workflow that manages call queueing and lead status updates. Create a Jira ticket with the lead link for immediate escalation.

kb_faqs.question: What is the issue with leads being marked as completed even when goals are not achieved?
kb_faqs.answer: In one pilot campaign, leads were being automatically marked as 'completed' in the system even though the defined goal (e.g., review posting) was not achieved. This prevents proper follow-up and skews conversion metrics. The issue (AC-1243) suggests either a bug in goal completion logic or misconfiguration of goal definitions. Resolution requires reviewing the campaign goal configuration and the backend workflow that marks leads as complete.

kb_faqs.question: What is the not-connected leads misclassification issue?
kb_faqs.answer: Not-connected leads (calls that did not connect) are being incorrectly classified as 'not interested' or 'cold' instead of a separate 'not connected' category. This misclassification skews qualification metrics and prevents proper lead recycling. The issue (AC-1123) has already been released and requires product fix to ensure not-connected leads are tracked separately and can be retried without conflating with genuine disinterest.

kb_faqs.question: What telephony provider issues have been encountered with virtual number backups?
kb_faqs.answer: Exotel has been observed to automatically link backup virtual numbers to primary procured numbers. When calling, customers see the backup number on their phone instead of the procured number, causing whitelisting issues and spam classification. This can persist for 2–8 days after number procurement. Workaround: Request Exotel to remove the backup number linking and procure additional numbers if the backup gets marked as spam. Parallelly, consider switching to alternative providers like Vobiz if this becomes a recurring blocker.

kb_faqs.question: What is the Vobiz integration status and what are the concurrent call limits?
kb_faqs.answer: Vobiz telephony integration is set up and operational with an initial concurrency limit of 15 concurrent calls. During testing, a bug was identified where the bot continuously repeats 'end call' statements in a loop. This is being fixed in development. Concurrent channels can be increased in real-time through Vobiz (no procurement delays). Confirm current integration status and any pending work before scaling campaigns using this provider.

kb_faqs.question: What causes WhatsApp to be disabled on a WABA account?
kb_faqs.answer: WhatsApp functionality can be disabled if the Meta business account has outstanding payment dues. In one case, a ₹121 pending payment caused WhatsApp to be disabled. Resolution: The Meta admin must complete the payment using the associated card. No platform action is required; this is a Meta account management issue. Check outstanding payments immediately if WhatsApp messaging fails unexpectedly.

kb_faqs.question: What is the request for a maximum call duration cap across multiple calls?
kb_faqs.answer: Currently, the platform supports a maximum duration limit for individual calls, but not across multiple calls to the same lead. Clients requested a configurable total call duration limit per lead (e.g., 7–8 minutes across all calls) to control voicebot consumption costs. This would allow capping cost per lead instead of per call, enabling better budget management for campaigns where leads may require multiple touchpoints. Feature status: proposed but not yet implemented.

kb_faqs.question: What is the request for automatic coupon code delivery?
kb_faqs.answer: Multiple clients reported high volumes of post-interaction customer queries asking about coupon codes or promotional benefits. Currently, the bot confirms that a coupon will be shared, but there is no automatic delivery mechanism, leading to repeated customer follow-ups and increased bot conversation cost. Requested feature: direct system integration to trigger and automatically deliver coupon codes via SMS/WhatsApp upon review posting or goal completion. This would reduce operational overhead significantly.

kb_faqs.question: What is the request for international calling capability?
kb_faqs.answer: One client requested the platform to support calling international prospects in addition to domestic leads. This request was deprioritized as out-of-scope for the current pilot, as it would require separate telephony setup, compliance handling, and cost implications. Recommendation: International calling should be treated as a separate feature request and implementation, not combined with ongoing domestic campaigns.

kb_faqs.question: What is the pre-campaign checklist for launching a new campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: Critical pre-campaign setup steps include: (1) Procure telephony numbers and configure them in the provider's caller ID section, (2) Add all numbers to the campaign's channel config to enable number shuffling, (3) Conduct UAT on a sample of 200–500 leads and validate quality before full-scale launch, (4) Ensure bot prompts and conversation flow are tested and approved, (5) Align with client on conversion metrics and baseline expectations, (6) Verify wallet/payment status is current, (7) Set up lead CSV with proper formatting and data validation, (8) Confirm human agent readiness for lead handoff.

kb_faqs.question: What is the process for configuring caller ID number shuffling in a campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: To enable number shuffling (rotating caller IDs): (1) Contact your telephony provider and procure the desired number of phone numbers, (2) In the provider's dashboard, go to the Caller ID section and enter all procured numbers, then save, (3) In the Convin platform, navigate to the campaign settings and select Channel Config, (4) Locate the Caller ID dropdown which now shows all available numbers, (5) Select all numbers or your desired subset – the platform will automatically allocate them to calls, rotating through the list. This ensures calls appear to come from different numbers, improving answer rates.

kb_faqs.question: What is the recommended UAT (User Acceptance Testing) approach before campaign go-live?
kb_faqs.answer: Standard UAT approach: (1) Run initial UAT on 200 leads to test bot quality, conversation flow, and technical integration, (2) Review call recordings and lead classifications, (3) Incorporate feedback and fix any identified issues, (4) Run second UAT on 1,000 new leads to validate improvements and ensure consistency, (5) Conduct internal quality audit with product and conversation design teams, (6) Align with client on key metrics (conversion rate, cost per lead, call duration, qualification accuracy) before full-scale launch. UAT duration is typically 2–3 days depending on lead availability and turnaround time.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What makes a good lead CSV vs. a bad lead CSV?
kb_faqs.answer: Good lead CSV characteristics: (1) Clean phone numbers with country codes (+91 for India), (2) Accurate customer segmentation (new vs. existing, product type, source), (3) Deduplicated records with no repeating phone numbers, (4) Complete required fields (name, phone, external ID for CRM linkage), (5) Properly formatted dates and enums matching campaign requirements. Bad CSV signs: (1) Missing or malformed phone numbers, (2) Duplicate entries causing repeated calls to the same prospect, (3) Misclassified leads (e.g., existing customers marked as new prospects), (4) Incomplete data forcing bot to ask clarifying questions, (5) Inconsistent formatting across columns. Always validate CSV structure during pre-campaign checklist.

kb_faqs.question: What are the differences between utility and marketing WhatsApp message templates?
kb_faqs.answer: Utility templates: Used for transactional messages (confirmations, receipts, account updates), have higher delivery rates (90–95% observed), no user opt-in required at time of send, and lower spam complaint risk. Marketing templates: Used for promotional content, require explicit user opt-in within the last 24 hours, have lower delivery rates (53% observed in one pilot), higher spam classification risk, and stricter content guidelines. Recommendation: Use utility templates for follow-ups and transactional communications when possible; reserve marketing templates for promotional campaigns and accept the lower delivery rate.

kb_faqs.question: What is the impact of switching from marketing to utility WhatsApp templates?
kb_faqs.answer: In one pilot, marketing template delivery was 53%. Switching to utility templates is expected to improve delivery to 90–95%, but this may come with trade-offs in engagement metrics since utility templates are more transactional in tone. Recommendation: Test utility templates on a subset of leads (e.g., 1,000 leads) over 2 days to measure impact on conversion before committing to full-scale utility-only campaigns. Clients should be informed that delivery rates will improve but engagement patterns may differ.

kb_faqs.question: What is the expected turnaround time (TAT) for human agent follow-up on bot-qualified leads?
kb_faqs.answer: TAT expectations vary by client, but industry standard is 24–48 hours for human agents to contact hot leads and 3–5 days for warm leads. If TAT is breached, leads risk going cold and conversion rates drop. Training requirement for human agents: they must be briefed on bot-generated lead classifications and be provided context from the bot conversation (key points discussed, customer objections, stated intent) to enable warm handoff. Without proper handoff documentation, agents may redo qualification work or ask redundant questions, frustrating prospects.

kb_faqs.question: What information should be passed to human agents during bot-to-human handoff?
kb_faqs.answer: Critical information to include in handoff: (1) Customer's stated interest level and objections raised, (2) Key details customer mentioned (budget, timeline, specific needs), (3) Products or services customer expressed interest in, (4) Any commitments made by customer (e.g., 'will post review'), (5) Recommended next step as determined by bot, (6) Full call recording or transcript for agent reference. Lack of this context forces agents to re-qualify and extends average handling time (AHT), increasing cost and reducing conversion likelihood. Automate handoff context passing where possible.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What is the recommended retry cadence for unanswered calls?
kb_faqs.answer: Best practice: (1) First call attempt during peak calling hours (9 AM–12 PM, 3 PM–6 PM), (2) If no answer, retry after 2–4 hours, (3) Third attempt next day during different time window, (4) After 3–4 failed attempts, move to WhatsApp + SMS fallback, (5) Wait 24 hours before next calling attempt. This balances persistence with frequency fatigue. However, retry logic should respect DND (Do Not Disturb) hours: avoid calling 9 PM–8 AM. Configurable retry windows have been requested but full implementation is pending.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What does NBA (Next Best Action) generation mean and when does it fail?
kb_faqs.answer: NBA is the system's automatic determination of the next step for a lead after a bot interaction (e.g., call, send WhatsApp message, hand off to agent, retry call). NBA generation failure (where the system does not automatically queue the next action) typically occurs when: (1) Lead status is not properly updated after a call, (2) Campaign workflow configuration has gaps, (3) Duplicate or conflicting lead records exist. When NBA fails, leads stall and miss follow-up opportunities. Create a Jira ticket with the specific lead ID if NBA generation is consistently failing for debugging.

kb_faqs.question: What system prompt patterns work well vs. fail in practice?
kb_faqs.answer: Effective patterns: (1) Short, natural opening ('Hi, this is [bot name] from [company]. How are you today?'), (2) Clear, single-focused questions instead of multiple questions in one turn, (3) Acknowledgment of customer response before moving to next topic, (4) Early exit language for disinterested customers ('Thanks so much for your time. Have a great day!'), (5) Specific call-to-action at the end of flow. Patterns that fail: (1) Long, scripted introductions with company background, (2) Multiple questions stacked in one message, (3) Verbose explanations of product features, (4) Continuing pitch after customer says 'not interested', (5) Asking for confirmation repeatedly on same action. Conversation design review recommended before any new campaign launch.

kb_faqs.question: Should bot configuration use lead scoring or entity-based classification?
kb_faqs.answer: Recommendation: Use entity-based classification over lead scoring for better accuracy, especially in pilots. Entity extraction identifies specific customer intent signals (e.g., 'interested in X product', 'budget Y amount') without relying on overall lead scoring algorithms. Lead scoring requires extensive tuning and prompt engineering and is prone to misclassification (e.g., marking 15-second confirmations as 'warm'). For pilots, entity-based extraction + manual review of edge cases is preferred. Lead scoring can be implemented post-pilot once accuracy benchmarks are established.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot handle customers who have already agreed to post a review?
kb_faqs.answer: Current issue: Bot continues following up seeking confirmation even after customer has agreed. Recommended approach: Bot should record the agreement and close the conversation, flagging the lead as 'hot – agreed to post review' for human follow-up. Goal definition should be 'obtain agreement to post review' not 'confirm review posted.' Human agents then follow up to ensure completion and answer any post-review questions. This reduces bot conversation cost by ~30–40% and respects customer time.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot respond when a customer expresses dissatisfaction?
kb_faqs.answer: Current behavior: Bot attempts to resolve dissatisfaction through extended conversation, increasing call duration and cost without improving conversion. Recommended response: When customer explicitly states dissatisfaction ('not happy with product', 'poor quality', etc.), bot should immediately respond: 'I understand. Let me raise this with my manager and they will get back to you shortly. Thank you for your feedback.' Then disconnect and route to supervisor/human agent for proper support. This prevents futile sales pitches, reduces cost by ~₹2k per lead, and improves customer experience by addressing concerns appropriately.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot handle coupon code and post-purchase benefit inquiries?
kb_faqs.answer: Current issue: Customers repeatedly follow up asking 'when will I get my coupon?' or 'what free items are included?', extending conversation. Bot confirmation that 'coupon will be shared' does not stop follow-ups. Recommended approach: (1) Bot confirms coupon eligibility during call, (2) System automatically triggers coupon delivery (SMS/WhatsApp) immediately after call ends, (3) Bot provides timeline for coupon delivery. If automatic delivery is not ready, bot should say: 'You'll receive your coupon code via text within 2 hours' and hand off to human agent to ensure delivery. This reduces follow-up queries by ~60%.

kb_faqs.question: What does a pre-go-live alignment meeting with clients typically cover?
kb_faqs.answer: Key topics to align before go-live: (1) Baseline conversion metrics from client's manual/human agent process, (2) Expected uplift from bot assistance (e.g., 'we expect 2–3x conversion with bot nudge'), (3) Cost per acquisition (CPA) comparison (bot CPA vs. human CPA) including all costs, (4) Lead qualification definitions (what makes a lead 'hot' vs. 'warm' vs. 'cold'), (5) Human agent SLA and handoff process, (6) Success metrics and review cadence (daily/weekly), (7) Escalation path for critical issues. Misalignment here causes disputes post-go-live; ensure this is documented and signed off.

kb_faqs.question: What percentage of leads typically result in successful call connections?
kb_faqs.answer: This varies significantly by campaign quality and lead source. In one pilot with 2,400 leads, ~467 connected on call at least once (19.5%), while 1,700+ (71%) had no response. For high-quality leads (opted-in customers), connection rates can be 40–50%. For cold outreach or low-quality lists, connection rates may be 10–20%. Low connection rates are often due to: (1) poor lead data quality, (2) network/DND issues, (3) calling at suboptimal times. Validate lead data and test on sample before large-scale campaigns.

kb_faqs.question: What are DND (Do Not Disturb) hours and how do they impact campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: In India, TRAI (Telecom Regulatory Authority of India) enforces DND hours: 9 PM–8 AM. Calls cannot be made during these hours (outbound calling is legally restricted). Campaign scheduling must respect these windows. Additionally, individual phone numbers may be registered on DND lists, preventing all telemarketing calls. Workaround for DND-listed numbers: use WhatsApp instead of voice calls, or exclude them from campaign. Best practice: Conduct DND check on lead lists before campaign start and handle separately.

kb_faqs.question: What is the spend distribution across lead interest categories?
kb_faqs.answer: In one analyzed campaign, spend per lead varied dramatically by interest level: Hot leads: ₹2,200, Warm leads: ₹2,500, Cold leads: ₹672, Not-interested/dissatisfied leads: ₹3,400. Not-interested leads consumed the highest cost (1.5x hot leads) because bot continued engagement despite disinterest. Optimization: Early exit for not-interested leads and improved dissatisfaction detection can reduce overall spend by 20–30% while improving conversion rate on remaining qualified leads.

kb_faqs.question: What specific improvements can be made to the interest classification prompt?
kb_faqs.answer: Issues with current prompts: (1) Vague interest signals (customer says 'sounds good' → marked warm, but may not follow through), (2) No distinction between active interest ('I want to buy') vs. passive interest ('tell me more'), (3) Not capturing objections separately from disinterest. Recommended prompt improvements: (1) Ask direct confirmation questions ('Are you interested in proceeding?'), (2) Probe on timeline and budget ('When do you plan to purchase?'), (3) Explicitly identify blockers ('What concerns do you have?'), (4) Use entity extraction for product-specific interest signals. Conversation design team should review and iterate on these prompts before next pilot launch.

kb_faqs.question: What is the impact of extended conversations on review posting conversion?
kb_faqs.answer: In one pilot, review posting flow required customers to complete actions (find review link, write review, submit) live during the call. Many customers struggled with this, extending calls beyond 5 minutes unnecessarily. Findings: Average review posting can be completed in 3 minutes and <10 WhatsApp messages. Leads with calls >5 minutes showed no additional conversion improvement. Recommendation: Keep bot conversation to <3 minutes for review posting, then hand off completion verification to human agents. This reduces AHT by ~40% and maintains conversion without efficiency loss.

kb_faqs.question: How should campaigns handle customer fulfillment queries (coupons, free items)?
kb_faqs.answer: Finding: High volume of customer follow-ups post-review asking about promised benefits. Bot currently confirms benefits but provides no delivery mechanism. Three-step solution: (1) Bot confirms benefit eligibility during call, (2) System automatically sends fulfillment (coupon code, free gift info) immediately post-call, (3) Human agent follows up only if customer has additional questions 24 hours later. If automated delivery unavailable, use human agents for post-review support and remove from bot flow. This reduces cost and improves customer satisfaction simultaneously.

kb_faqs.question: What connectivity rate and call success metrics should we expect from a typical pilot deployment?
kb_faqs.answer: Clients typically see 80% of calls with less than 3 seconds average latency. However, latency issues remain a critical pain point, with approximately 14% of calls showing multiple user 'hello' utterances (695 out of 4930 connected calls), indicating bot silence or delayed responses. Connection stability varies by dialer provider, with some experiencing unexpected backup number usage that impacts consistency. Early detection of connectivity issues requires monitoring both average latency metrics and anomalies like repeated user prompts within single calls.

kb_faqs.question: What is the recommended prompt size and structure for optimal bot performance?
kb_faqs.answer: Prompt size should be capped at 25,000–30,000 characters; anything above 50,000 characters (e.g., 88,000–95,000 character prompts) leads to degraded performance and unreliable outcomes. Instead of bloated prompts, use the RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) tool to store and dynamically fetch knowledge base content. Best practice: include a fixed opening line template verbatim in the prompt ('Hi [name], this is [bot name] from [company]...') for first-call consistency, with instructions to switch to dynamic openings on follow-up attempts based on call history.

kb_faqs.question: What are the most common bot failures observed in pilot calls and how are they resolved?
kb_faqs.answer: Common failures include: (1) Pronunciation issues ('Are' and 'Do' misrendered; 'May' pronounced as 'Mai'), fixed by updating TTS configuration; (2) Latency and bot silence (4+ second pauses), caused by WebSocket connection drops with TTS provider, fixed by reconnection logic; (3) Bot ignoring customer questions despite correct transcription, fixed by prompt refinement to include instructions for handling mid-conversation queries; (4) Language switching mid-call (English to Hindi), resolved via prompt instruction to maintain default language; (5) End-statement looping where bot repeats closing line, fixed by removing or disabling the human handoff tool. Latency improvements require backend connection optimization with TTS providers.

kb_faqs.question: How should bots handle appointment booking when customers use casual language like 'abhi book karva do' (book it now)?
kb_faqs.answer: Add explicit instructions in the prompt to recognize and handle casual appointment language. When a customer says 'abhi book karva do' or similar, the bot should confirm the intent rather than re-asking for date/time. The bot must separate date and time capture into two distinct questions, and use automatic date recognition to calculate actual calendar dates from relative terms (tomorrow, next week, Monday). When a past date/time is mentioned, the bot should pivot to asking if the customer attended or will attend the session. Testing shows ~85% accuracy with this approach when instructions are clear and separate from the main prompt logic.

kb_faqs.question: What is the pre-campaign checklist for launching a WhatsApp campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: Key checklist items: (1) Secure virtual phone numbers (procure from dialer provider like Exotel; whitelist with client's systems); (2) Register WhatsApp Business Account (WABA) and template types (Utility vs. Marketing); (3) Ensure payment/wallet balance in WABA to avoid message delivery failures; (4) Upload and clean lead CSV with required fields and proper formatting; (5) Complete UAT with 5–10 test calls, verifying script adherence, pronunciation, latency, and appointment scheduling; (6) Get stakeholder alignment on conversion benchmarks and ROI expectations *before* campaign launch; (7) Configure campaign settings including initial_message (mandatory), lead ingestion API variables, and NBA (Next Best Action) retry rules; (8) Test template delivery across different message types to validate rates (typically 35–40% with marketing templates vs. higher with utility templates).

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What makes a lead CSV 'good' vs. 'bad' in practice?
What are the typical WhatsApp message delivery rates and failure modes?
kb_faqs.answer: Marketing template messages typically achieve 35–40% delivery rates; Utility template messages see higher rates. Common failure modes: (1) Payment issues in WABA account (blocks all message delivery); (2) Template rejection by Meta (content violates policy); (3) Number not whitelisted by Meta (for utility templates); (4) Batch message sending in rapid succession causes delivery delays or failures. When 85% of messages fail due to marketing template rejection, switch to Utility template or adjust messaging. Ensure WABA payment is current before launching campaigns; delayed payment detection is a leading cause of campaign stalls.

kb_faqs.question: Should Convin offer Truecaller whitelisting as a standard service?
kb_faqs.answer: Truecaller whitelisting is NOT a standard included service; it was tested as a pilot-specific experiment with one client and costs ₹15,000/month. Do not offer whitelisting to clients unless explicitly scoped in the contract and pricing. If clients have Truecaller premium subscriptions, they can request whitelisting directly from Truecaller; otherwise, the cost is client-borne. Avoid circulating whitelisting requirements broadly; it sets incorrect expectations and creates unnecessary delays.

kb_faqs.question: What happens when a bot hands off a lead to a human agent and what are TAT expectations?
kb_faqs.answer: When the bot identifies a 'hot lead' (e.g., customer agrees to use a coupon code or shows purchase intent), it attempts to transfer the call to a human agent using the human handoff tool. However, this tool can cause end-statement looping (bot repeating its closing line), requiring removal or disabling as a workaround. Best practice: define 'hot lead' criteria clearly with the client upfront (e.g., 'leads who agreed to purchase and confirmed coupon usage'). Human agents should be briefed with conversation summaries (bot call duration, key objections raised, customer tone) to enable warm handoff. TAT for agent pickup should be <2 minutes to avoid customer disconnection.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: How should NBA (Next Best Action) retry logic be configured for optimal campaign performance?
kb_faqs.answer: NBA retry logic should include: (1) DND (Do Not Disturb) hours configuration to avoid calling outside business hours; (2) Retry window of 24–48 hours for first retry, 72+ hours for second retry; (3) Maximum 2–3 retry attempts per lead to avoid harassment; (4) Different opening lines for retry calls based on previous attempt outcome (e.g., 'This is a follow-up from yesterday's call...'); (5) NBA execution monitoring to verify all queued leads are processed (not stuck in progress bar). Testing shows NBA execution can lag or stall if backend tools are misconfigured; monitor internal domain tools execution history and raise tickets if progress doesn't update within 30 minutes.

kb_faqs.question: What ROI metrics and business case data should be presented to prospects during pilots?
kb_faqs.answer: Frame the business case as: 'You provide X leads; Convin converts Y% of them into qualified/hot leads.' Align on conversion percentage benchmarks *before* campaign launch, ideally with decision-makers (not just operational contacts). Example metrics: if 1,000 leads are provided, clarify expected conversion to 'hot leads' (e.g., 5–15% depending on product and lead quality). Measure success by hot lead volume and handoff quality, not just call completion. Avoid using sole metrics like 'coupon code redemption' unless both parties explicitly agree this is the success indicator; it narrows the value proposition. Typical CPA varies by industry; premium services (health, education) see 5–10% hot lead rates; B2C e-commerce sees 10–20%.

kb_faqs.question: How should 'hot leads' be defined and qualified?
kb_faqs.answer: A 'hot lead' definition must be mutually agreed upon *before* campaign kickoff. Common definitions: (1) Customer agreed to book/schedule a consultation or appointment; (2) Customer expressed purchase intent and agreed to use a coupon code; (3) Customer answered key qualifying questions affirmatively (e.g., 'Do you want to proceed?'); (4) Customer provided follow-up contact preference or time window. The bot should be configured to explicitly ask for confirmation ('Can I schedule you for [date/time]?') rather than assuming agreement. Data point: if definition is vague, stakeholder misalignment occurs later (e.g., one party counts all call completions as hot leads, the other only counts actual bookings).

kb_faqs.question: What call quality metrics should be monitored and reported to clients regularly?
kb_faqs.answer: Key metrics: (1) Connected calls (total calls answered, typically 40–60% pickup rate); (2) Average call duration (target 2–5 min for consultations, 1–2 min for surveys); (3) Latency (% of calls <3 sec avg, target >80%); (4) Dead air instances (% of calls with 5+ second silence when bot should speak); (5) Script adherence score (% of calls where bot followed the defined opening); (6) Qualification rate (% of connected calls that became hot leads); (7) Repeat customer 'hello' count (indicator of bot silence). Provide a monthly Dead Air Report using configurable silence thresholds (e.g., >5 seconds) to prove latency improvements. Clients often cite recording samples of poor calls; quantify the issue with aggregate metrics to provide context (e.g., 'This happened in <2% of calls').

kb_faqs.question: How many test calls should be run during UAT before go-live?
kb_faqs.answer: Minimum 10–15 UAT calls recommended, covering: (1) Happy path (ideal scenario); (2) Objection handling (customer hesitation or refusal); (3) Clarification requests (customer asks bot to repeat or explain); (4) Appointment booking with various date/time formats; (5) Gender-neutral response verification (no 'Sir/Ma'am' confusion); (6) Late-night/off-hours scenarios (to test DND rules); (7) Network latency edge cases. UAT should include both internal team testing and 2–3 client stakeholder calls. Issues discovered in UAT (pronunciation, latency, script deviation) must be fixed and re-tested before campaign launch. A single UAT call may not reveal intermittent issues; multiple calls build confidence.

kb_faqs.question: What should be discussed in the kickoff call with a new pilot client?
kb_faqs.answer: Kickoff call agenda: (1) Define bot use case and success criteria clearly; (2) Agree on conversion definition and target metrics; (3) Provide timeline (UAT duration, go-live date, campaign duration); (4) Clarify lead sourcing (client will provide CSV, timing, freshness); (5) Explain bot capabilities and limitations (e.g., appointment scheduling requires calendar integration; product knowledge from KB only); (6) Assign Convin SPOC (Account Manager or CSM) and client SPOC (decision-maker + operations contact); (7) Discuss data privacy and recording consent; (8) Set up integration details (WhatsApp WABA, CRM webhook if applicable); (9) Agree on support model (response time SLA, escalation path). Document all agreements in a handover form to avoid later misalignment.

kb_faqs.question: What integration challenges are commonly faced during client onboarding?
kb_faqs.answer: Common integration delays: (1) LSQ (lead source queue) integration for automated lead fetch—testing shows 1-month+ delays and potential instability; recommend manual CSV upload as faster alternative; (2) WABA (WhatsApp Business Account) phone number registration requiring client whitelisting, causing 1–2 day delays if client is slow to respond; (3) CRM webhook configuration rarely completed by clients (only 3 out of 10+ tenants had this live)—clarify whether this is in scope and provide step-by-step docs; (4) Calendar/scheduling tool integration (required for appointment slots) not yet built, currently causing bot to propose past time slots; (5) Payment/wallet top-up in WABA forgotten, causing sudden message delivery stoppage mid-campaign. Recommendation: prioritize faster integration paths (manual upload, no external tools) for pilot speed; defer complex integrations to later phases.

kb_faqs.question: What features have clients requested based on real pilot pain points?
kb_faqs.answer: Top feature requests: (1) Calendar integration tool to fetch available time slots and prevent bot from suggesting past appointments; (2) Web search tool to get current time accurately (especially across timezones); (3) Ability to handle casual appointment language ('abhi karva do', 'kal lana hai') without re-asking for date/time; (4) Gender-neutral pronoun handling ('You' instead of 'Sir/Ma'am') as a configurable default; (5) Dynamic opening line that adapts based on call attempt history (e.g., 'This is a follow-up from yesterday'); (6) Dead air reporting with configurable thresholds to help clients understand latency issues; (7) Filtered analytics dashboard (by date range, lead source, outcome) that downloads correctly; (8) RAG tool training/documentation (currently underdocumented, causing setup delays). Workarounds currently in use: (1) Remove human handoff tool to stop end-statement looping; (2) Cap prompt at 25k characters and use RAG for KB instead; (3) Manually configure appointment time slots in knowledge base rather than integrating calendar.

kb_faqs.question: What is the root cause of bot 'hanging up' mid-call and how is it diagnosed?
kb_faqs.answer: Bot hangs up (call terminates unexpectedly) when: (1) Human handoff tool is triggered but no agent is available or transfer fails; (2) Bot runs out of context or token limit (especially with large prompts); (3) TTS (Text-to-Speech) connection drops and bot cannot recover gracefully. Diagnosis: check call recording for exact moment of disconnection, review backend logs for WebSocket/TTS connection events, and verify human handoff tool configuration. Quick fix: disable human handoff tool if this is the cause. Permanent fix: implement retry logic for TTS reconnection and set a max context limit in the LLM configuration. One client reported 2 out of 2 test calls hung up; after redesigning the prompt and removing the handoff tool, subsequent calls completed successfully.

kb_faqs.question: How are WhatsApp template failures diagnosed and resolved?
kb_faqs.answer: Template failure diagnosis: (1) Check WABA payment status first—if overdue, all messages fail with 'payment error'; (2) Verify template compliance with Meta's messaging policy (no promotional language, no links in utility templates, proper variable formatting); (3) Check template type: Marketing templates have lower delivery (~35–40%); Utility templates higher (~60–80%); (4) If 85%+ of messages fail due to template, switch template type and re-test; (5) Monitor delivery status in Meta's WABA dashboard alongside Convin analytics. Real example: Guvi's campaign had 8,221 attempted messages, 5,000 failed (10% payment, 85% marketing template rejection, 5% other); switching to Utility template and clearing payment resolved most failures. Always ensure WABA payment is current before launching campaigns.

kb_faqs.question: What should be done when the bot repeats its closing statement or gets stuck in a loop?
kb_faqs.answer: End-statement looping (bot repeating closing line like 'Thanks for your time, goodbye') typically occurs when the human handoff tool is triggered but fails silently. Immediate workaround: disable or remove the 'Human_Handoff' tool from the agent configuration; this stops the loop. Root cause fix: implement proper error handling in the handoff tool so that if transfer fails, the bot gracefully ends the call instead of looping. Before disabling, verify the tool is not critical to the campaign (if handoffs are required, fix the tool instead). Test 3–5 calls after the fix to confirm the loop is gone. Document this as a known issue to prevent other teams from encountering the same blocker.

kb_faqs.question: How should 'hello' in mid-conversation be handled to prevent bot reset?
kb_faqs.answer: When a customer says 'hello' or similar interruption mid-call, the bot currently resets to the initial conversation state instead of continuing the conversation. This is a prompt/LLM configuration issue. Fix: (1) Add explicit instruction in prompt: 'If the customer says hello, greeting-type words, or asks to repeat, do NOT restart the conversation. Acknowledge and continue from the current state.'; (2) Use system prompts to track conversation state separately from user input; (3) Test with edge cases like customer putting bot on speaker, background noise triggering false 'hello' detection. Metrics: Ezo had 695 out of 4,930 calls with multiple 'hello' utterances, indicating the bot was getting stuck or resetting frequently. After prompt fix, this should decrease.

kb_faqs.question: How should a bot handle customers asking 'tell me more' or requesting clarification?
kb_faqs.answer: When a customer asks 'Can you tell me more about [product]?', the bot should NOT go silent or ignore the request. Fix: (1) Add an instruction in the system prompt: 'When the customer asks for more information, provide a 30–45 second summary from the knowledge base, then return to the qualification flow.'; (2) Use the RAG tool to fetch relevant product details rather than hard-coding them; (3) After providing info, ask a follow-up question to re-engage ('Does this sound interesting to you?'). Real example: Customer said 'Can you tell me more?', bot went silent for 2+ seconds, then continued with questions, losing the customer's attention. After prompt revision with explicit instructions, the bot now handles this smoothly.

kb_faqs.question: What is the best approach for handling gender-neutral pronouns and titles?
kb_faqs.answer: Avoid using 'Sir' or 'Ma'am' entirely. Instead: (1) Use only the customer's first name ('Hi Bhargav...', 'Thanks Bhargav...'); (2) Use second-person pronouns ('you', 'your') instead of titles; (3) Add explicit instruction in prompt: 'Never use Sir, Ma'am, or gender-specific titles. Use only the first name and you/your.'; (4) Test with a diverse set of names to ensure no unintended gender assumptions slip through the system. One client reported bot called a male customer 'Ma'am' throughout the call; after adding gender-neutral instructions, subsequent tests showed 100% accuracy.

kb_faqs.question: Should the bot opening line be static or dynamic, and when?
kb_faqs.answer: First call opening line should be STATIC (fixed, verbatim). Follow-up calls should be DYNAMIC (adapted based on call history). Implementation: (1) Create a static opening template for the first call ('Hi [name], this is [bot name] from [company]. I'm calling about [product] you inquired about. Is this a good time?'); (2) Use a conversation flag or attempt counter to detect if this is a first call or retry; (3) For retries, generate a dynamic opening that references the prior attempt ('Hi [name], this is a follow-up from my call yesterday...'). Benefit: ensures consistent brand messaging on first contact while making retries feel personal and reducing customer friction. Client feedback: when the bot deviated from the agreed opening on the first call, it reduced customer trust; static openings should never be paraphrased by the bot.

kb_faqs.question: What is the sequence of steps to migrate a client from UAT to production?
kb_faqs.answer: Migration sequence: (1) Complete UAT with 10–15 test calls, sign-off from client stakeholders; (2) Fix any critical issues identified (latency, pronunciation, script deviation) and re-test; (3) If switching dialers (e.g., from internal testing to Exotel), procure new virtual numbers and whitelist with client; (4) Ensure WABA payment is loaded and templates are approved by Meta; (5) Schedule a final 'go-live readiness' call with client SPOC; (6) Execute first batch with 50–100 leads as a smoke test, monitor for errors in real-time; (7) Once smoke test passes, expand to full lead volume in batches; (8) Monitor daily for first week: call quality, message delivery, hot lead generation; (9) Assign CSM for ongoing support and weekly business reviews. Typical migration takes 1–2 weeks from UAT approval to full deployment.

kb_faqs.question: What critical issues should halt a campaign launch?
kb_faqs.answer: Do NOT launch if: (1) Average latency >5 seconds in UAT calls (indicates TTS or network issues); (2) Bot fails to follow the agreed opening statement in >10% of test calls; (3) Appointment booking fails in any test scenario (bot proposes past time, ignores date preference, etc.); (4) End-statement looping observed (indicates handoff tool bug); (5) WABA payment is not confirmed or templates are not approved; (6) Client SPOC has not signed off on the bot behavior; (7) Lead CSV has not been validated and deduplicated. These issues will surface as complaints post-launch and delay ROI. Better to delay go-live by 2–3 days and fix now than lose a client relationship.

kb_faqs.question: What are common customer objections raised during bot calls and how should bots respond?
kb_faqs.answer: Common objections and recommended bot responses: (1) 'I'm busy, call me later' → Bot: 'I understand. When would be a good time for you? I can call you then.' (Captures callback window); (2) 'What's this call about?' → Bot: 'You had shown interest in [product]. I'm calling to answer any questions and help you schedule a consultation.' (Establishes context); (3) 'Is this a sales call?' → Bot: 'Yes, I'm calling to see if [product] is a good fit for your needs and schedule a time to discuss.' (Honest acknowledgment); (4) 'Send me information via email instead' → Bot: 'I can do that, but let me quickly confirm a few details so I send you the right information.' (Attempts qualification before transferring); (5) 'I'm not interested' → Bot: 'I understand. Can I ask what specifically doesn't interest you? It might help me show you something relevant.' (Attempts to probe objection). Bot should never argue or use high-pressure language; if customer repeats 'not interested', respectfully end the call.

kb_faqs.question: How should bots handle the objection 'I already have a solution/provider'?
kb_faqs.answer: When customer says 'I already use [competitor product]', bot should: (1) Acknowledge ('That's great! It sounds like you're already set up'); (2) Differentiate ('Our [product] offers [specific unique benefit], which [competitor] doesn't have'); (3) Reframe pain point ('Many customers who have [competitor] are also interested in [benefit] we provide'); (4) Qualify ('If I could show you how to [specific outcome], would you be open to a quick conversation?'). If customer still objects, respect it and offer: 'No problem. If your situation changes, feel free to reach out.' The goal is not to force a conversation but to identify if there's a genuine second use case. Example: customer uses competitor dermatology booking tool but Convin's bot offers skincare product recommendations; bot could position as complementary, not competitive.

kb_faqs.question: When should a bot hand off a lead to a human agent?
kb_faqs.answer: Handoff criteria should be pre-defined with client and configured in the bot prompt. Common triggers: (1) Customer expresses strong purchase intent ('I want to buy'); (2) Customer agrees to book an appointment or schedule a call; (3) Customer asks a complex question the bot cannot answer; (4) Customer requests to speak to a human; (5) Customer shows high engagement (multiple back-and-forth exchanges). Define a 'hot lead' threshold before handoff (e.g., customer must confirm date/time AND express intent). Do NOT hand off on every objection or complex question—bot should attempt to resolve first. Example: customer asks 'What's the refund policy?', bot should retrieve policy from KB and answer, not hand off. Handoff should feel natural ('Let me connect you with my colleague who can finalize your booking') and happen within 3 seconds to avoid customer hang-up.

kb_faqs.question: What is the typical conversion rate from 'bot-qualified lead' to 'closed deal'?
kb_faqs.answer: Industry variation: Premium services (healthcare, education) see 10–25% conversion from hot leads to closed deal; B2C e-commerce sees 5–15%; B2B services see 20–40%. Variables affecting conversion: (1) Lead quality (fresh vs. old database); (2) Human agent handoff quality (agent trained on bot context or not); (3) Follow-up TAT (agent contacts within 24 hours vs. later); (4) Product-market fit for the lead segment. Clients should expect: if bot qualifies 100 leads, 5–15 become hot leads; of those hot leads, 1–5 convert to closed deal (10–30% conversion). Track both metrics separately to identify bottlenecks (if hot lead rate is low, bot needs refinement; if hot lead → close rate is low, sales team needs training). Set realistic expectations upfront to avoid disappointment.

kb_faqs.question: What should we do if 'Not Connected' calls are showing as blanks in reports instead of the correct status?
kb_faqs.answer: This is a reporting display issue where call connectivity status is not being properly reflected. A ticket (AC-1308) should be created to ensure 'Not Connected' status appears correctly in reports rather than showing blank fields. This impacts data accuracy for campaign analysis and client reporting.

kb_faqs.question: What connectivity rates should we expect when clients report only 2% of calls reaching the wrong person?
kb_faqs.answer: Clients typically report 2% of calls reaching the wrong person on average during deployments. However, this metric should be tracked consistently, as initial estimates may sometimes differ from actual results. It's important to verify these numbers before making campaign decisions.

kb_faqs.question: What happens when a bot doesn't disconnect properly and repeats its final statement, causing loops?
kb_faqs.answer: This is a critical hangup issue where the bot fails to end the call after delivering the final statement, instead repeating it and creating conversation loops. The hangup delay setting (currently 0s) should be adjusted to 1s to resolve this. Campaigns should be paused until this issue is fixed to prevent wasted spend and poor user experience.

kb_faqs.question: Why is the bot skipping its introduction and jumping directly to profiling in every call?
kb_faqs.answer: This occurs when an incorrect or test system prompt is deployed instead of the standard bot persona prompt (e.g., LHR 8 Pillar). The solution is to revert to the correct prompt version immediately. Always test prompt changes in a separate campaign/KB environment to avoid impacting live deployments.

kb_faqs.question: What's the best practice for managing multiple campaigns with different configurations?
kb_faqs.answer: Build separate campaigns with separate knowledge bases for different configurations or experiments. Do not mix campaigns or KBs, as this can cause configuration conflicts, prompt issues, and inaccurate performance metrics.

kb_faqs.question: How should we time campaign launches to optimize conversion rates?
kb_faqs.answer: Start campaigns on Mondays rather than weekends when possible, as this can improve conversion by 1% or more. Ensure human follow-up resources are available on Saturday but not Sunday, so align launch timing accordingly. This prevents leads from going cold and ensures proper handoff logistics.

kb_faqs.question: What's the priority order for human agent follow-up: hot or warm leads?
kb_faqs.answer: Hot leads can be handled effectively by bot alone, but warm leads definitely need human agent follow-up. Prioritize human resources for warm lead attempts to maximize conversion, as bots are less effective at handling objection-based conversations on partially-qualified leads.

kb_faqs.question: What causes a WhatsApp WABA to get flagged or templates to get paused?
kb_faqs.answer: When a WABA number receives spam complaints from users, Meta may flag it with a warning and reduce message delivery. This typically happens when sending too many utility messages to leads without response (3+ messages). Solution: reduce messages without response to 2, or shift to marketing templates for pilot campaigns. Monitor quality rating and health status in WhatsApp Business settings.

kb_faqs.question: What's the difference between utility and marketing WhatsApp templates, and when should each be used?
kb_faqs.answer: Utility templates are for transactional messages and have higher delivery rates; marketing templates are for promotional content. For pilots, use utility templates to avoid WABA flagging. Limit utility messages to 2 per lead without response to maintain sender reputation and prevent account restrictions.

kb_faqs.question: What CPC improvements should we expect from optimizing bot prompts and qualification logic?
kb_faqs.answer: One deployment saw a 50% reduction in CPC from Rs 202 (bot only) to Rs 113 (bot only) through prompt optimization and improved lead qualification. Previous campaign was Rs 7,079 spend with 35 conversions; optimized campaign was Rs 3,616.75 with 32 conversions. When humans added conversion follow-up, CPC dropped further to Rs 80, matching or beating human-only approaches.

kb_faqs.question: How can we demonstrate that bot+human hybrid model achieves better ROI than human-only?
kb_faqs.answer: Run multiple consecutive campaigns with the same optimization settings to show consistent results. Expected metrics: Bot alone converts 15-20% of leads; humans convert additional 6-7% of remaining warm/hot leads. Combined CPC of Rs 80-90 is achievable, matching or beating human-only CPC of Rs 90+. Consistency across 2+ campaigns proves the model works, not just one outlier.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What lead quality issues should we watch for before campaign launch?
kb_faqs.answer: Verify that leads are actual contacts who opted in (consent-based) rather than cold database lists. DND-filtered and consent-verified leads have better connectivity rates. Poor lead quality (non-consenting, invalid numbers, wrong decision-makers) directly impacts bot connectivity and conversion rates.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot persona be configured to improve qualification accuracy and conversion?
kb_faqs.answer: Use an empathy-focused, qualification-first approach: be friendly to prospects who are happy with current solutions, ask them to contact support if unhappy, and avoid being pushy. The bot should focus on identifying genuinely interested prospects ('hot' leads) rather than trying to convert everyone. This reduces wasted conversations and improves downstream human agent efficiency.

kb_faqs.question: What should the bot do when a prospect says they're unhappy or have issues?
kb_faqs.answer: Direct unhappy prospects to the business's own support/boat support system rather than continuing the sales pitch. The bot should recognize dissatisfaction signals and politely hand off, positioning support as the better resource. This prevents bad experiences and preserves the business relationship.

kb_faqs.question: What causes calls to auto-disconnect mid-conversation and how do we prevent it?
kb_faqs.answer: Silence handling mechanism can malfunction, causing calls to drop after extended silence. This needs to be monitored at the backend level. Campaigns should pause if call durations suddenly spike (e.g., 18+ minutes) as this indicates the bot is not properly detecting conversation end. Create a Jira ticket immediately and notify CSM for spend analysis.

kb_faqs.question: How do we fix mispronunciation of Hindi words in bot calls?
kb_faqs.answer: Two approaches: (1) Use word replacement in Settings > Integrations > TTS to replace words with their Devanagari equivalents (e.g., 'do' → 'डू'), or (2) Configure the LLM prompt to generate Hindi responses in Devanagari script instead of Roman script. Be careful with word replacements to avoid breaking partial matches (e.g., replacing 'do' breaks 'does'). Test thoroughly before deployment.

kb_faqs.question: Why is the bot generating Hindi responses in Roman script instead of Devanagari, causing mispronunciations?
kb_faqs.answer: The LLM prompt should instruct the model to generate Hindi-language responses in Devanagari script, not Roman script. However, modifying the 8 Pillar prompt globally may break WhatsApp text messaging. The issue should be handled at the VoiceBot level with proper encoding. Create a ticket to investigate why the existing Voicebot-level solution isn't working for all cases.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What happens when a customer sends multiple messages within 10 seconds and what should we do?
kb_faqs.answer: If a new customer message arrives before the bot sends its response to the previous message, the bot should cancel the pending response and generate a new NBA response incorporating both messages. This should work for up to 5 rapid back-to-back messages. Avoid infinite cancellation loops as the NLM may struggle with too many consolidated inputs.

kb_faqs.question: How do we handle custom fields from CRM integrations when variable mapping isn't working?
kb_faqs.answer: Workaround: Pass custom fields in the payload without formal variable mapping, then define them in the system prompt and explain them to the LLM (e.g., 'mx_Business_Name is the name of the business'). The LLM will use context to pick up these values. Accuracy is good for 1-2 variables but may degrade with many custom fields. A proper variable mapping fix is in development.

kb_faqs.question: What causes variable mapping to fail between integration fields and bot prompts?
kb_faqs.answer: Currently, variable mapping between custom CRM fields and bot system prompts is broken due to a product architecture issue. Custom fields arrive in the payload but can't be formally mapped. The product team is working on a fix. Until then, use the workaround of defining fields in the prompt for the LLM to interpret, or implement a custom integration solution.

kb_faqs.question: What's the pre-campaign checklist before launching a Sense deployment?
kb_faqs.answer: 1) Confirm bot persona and system prompt are correct (test in UAT environment) 2) Verify lead CSV quality (consent-based, DND-filtered, valid numbers) 3) Ensure wallet is topped up with sufficient budget 4) Test all call flows end-to-end 5) Confirm hangup delay settings (1s recommended) 6) Set silence handling timeouts appropriately 7) Test WhatsApp template delivery if multi-channel 8) Align with client on success metrics, expected CPC, and human handoff process before go-live.

kb_faqs.question: What should we test thoroughly in UAT before campaign launch to avoid production issues?
kb_faqs.answer: Test the bot persona opening statement to ensure it plays correctly and doesn't skip to profiling. Test hangup behavior—bot should end cleanly after final statement without repeating. Test silence detection with various pause lengths. Validate all system prompts are deployed correctly (not test/dev versions). Run at least 2-3 complete call flows yourself before launch.

New employees and human agents taking bot handoffs should be trained on: (1) which lead segments bot focuses on (hot leads) vs which need human touch (warm leads) 2) how to interpret bot conversation context in handoff notes 3) expected CPC and conversion rates so they understand the value-add they're providing 4) common objections already handled by bot vs new ones to expect 5) how to use the bot+human hybrid model for better conversion than either alone.
kb_faqs.question: What training should human agents receive before taking handoffs from the bot?
Why is it important to inform sales/delivery teams a week before customer closures?
kb_faqs.answer: Early notice allows onboarding teams to assign a CSM, schedule proper knowledge transfer from sales/project team, and plan in-person kickoff meetings. This improves customer onboarding experience and adoption. Last-minute closures lead to under-prepared CSMs and poor first-impression kickoffs.

kb_faqs.question: What's the value of conducting in-person customer training vs virtual sessions?
kb_faqs.answer: In-person dashboard training and hypercare sessions are significantly more effective for adoption than virtual sessions. Ideally, CSM and implementation team should spend a week on-site with new customers during hypercare period. This is especially important for consumption-based businesses where client engagement directly drives usage and ROI.

kb_faqs.question: What account-level visibility should sales and CSM teams have for proactive management?
kb_faqs.answer: Both teams should have real-time access to: wallet balance, last recharge date/amount, active plan as per MSA agreement. This enables proactive account management, spend monitoring, and upsell opportunities. Access should be available on the dashboard's Billing section.

kb_faqs.question: How can we improve bot lead qualification to focus on hot prospects first?
kb_faqs.answer: Configure the bot to ask qualifying questions early and identify leads that are actively dissatisfied or interested in solutions. Route only genuinely interested prospects ('hot' leads) to human agents. This increases human efficiency and improves overall conversion metrics. Warm leads (potentially interested but not urgent) should be prioritized after hot leads are exhausted.

kb_faqs.question: What's a realistic bot-only conversion rate expectation for Sense deployments?
kb_faqs.answer: Bot-only conversion rates typically range from 12-20% of contacted leads (depending on lead quality). For one deployment: 37 conversions from 255 hot+warm leads = 14.5%. When these are actual product reviews/actions, this represents strong bot performance. Human follow-up on remaining 218 leads yielded 19 additional conversions (8.7%), showing complementary strengths.

kb_faqs.question: What connectivity rates should we expect from bots vs human agents?
kb_faqs.answer: Bot connectivity is typically lower than human agents because bots call all numbers whereas humans may skip known invalid numbers or bad contact info. This is normal and expected. Clients should understand that bot low connectivity rate doesn't indicate failure—it's identifying bad lead quality that humans would skip. Focus on conversion rate among connected calls, not total connectivity.

kb_faqs.question: How should we position CPC comparisons between bot and human approaches to clients?
kb_faqs.answer: Present final hybrid CPC (bot + human follow-up) against client's human-only baseline, not bot-only CPC. Example: 'Bot+Human: Rs 80 CPC vs Your human-only: Rs 90 CPC' shows cost advantage. Also emphasize that bot handles initial qualification, freeing human agents for higher-value conversations. This positioning shows business value beyond just cost reduction.

kb_faqs.question: What's the client pushback when bot connectivity seems lower than expected?
kb_faqs.answer: Clients may question why bot can't reach people human agents reach. Root cause is usually poor lead quality or incorrect assumption about what 'connectivity' means. Explain that bots try all numbers (showing true data quality) while humans skip obvious bad numbers. If client has consent-based, DND-filtered leads, connectivity should improve significantly.

kb_faqs.question: What are 'hot' vs 'warm' vs 'cold' leads in the context of bot+human hybrid model?
kb_faqs.answer: Hot leads: actively dissatisfied, explicitly interested, or ready to take action (bot can convert 20%+). Warm leads: potentially interested but need nurturing (human agents convert 6-8%). Cold leads: not interested or unqualified (bot quickly identifies and dismisses). Segment leads into these buckets for targeting—bot handles hot/cold, humans handle warm.

kb_faqs.question: What's the workaround for integrating multiple dialers when only one custom telephony integration is included?
kb_faqs.answer: Most plans include 1 custom telephony integration. If client evaluates multiple dialers, communicate that additional integrations beyond the first are billable on a per-integration basis. Recommend consolidating on one dialer or evaluating integrations sequentially rather than simultaneously to manage costs.

kb_faqs.question: How do we ensure critical product information reaches all stakeholders in scattered communication?
kb_faqs.answer: Use dedicated Slack/WhatsApp channels for specific clients or use ChatGPT summarization of chat threads for consistent information sharing. Assign one person to communicate product/integration decisions to key stakeholders (sales, onboarding, product) to prevent information silos. Documentation and regular sync are critical.

kb_faqs.question: What does '8 Pillar' prompt mean and when should it be used?
kb_faqs.answer: '8 Pillar' is a standard bot persona prompt that covers core conversational principles for sales outreach (introduction, qualification, objection handling, etc.). It should be the default prompt for most Sense deployments unless a custom persona is specifically needed. Always revert to this as baseline if custom prompts cause issues.

kb_faqs.question: What's causing total spend to continuously change on a paused campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: This indicates a backend billing or spend-tracking issue where the system is continuing to accrue charges on campaigns that have been paused. It should snapshot spend at pause time and not change. This is a critical data accuracy issue—notify product team immediately and run spend analysis to identify any overcharges.

kb_faqs.question: What causes call audio quality issues and how are they identified?
kb_faqs.answer: Issues like 'Main' being mispronounced (when bot says 'main hoon') indicate TTS pronunciation bugs with Hindi language mixed with English phonetics. These are identified through call recordings and transcripts. Root cause is usually LLM generating Hindi in Roman script instead of Devanagari. Test thoroughly with Hindi-language prompts before deployment.

kb_faqs.question: What should we do if a client's human agents have already started calling our bot-generated leads?
kb_faqs.answer: This creates duplicate dialing and wastes lead spend. Clarify roles immediately: either client's humans call leads, or Convin bot calls them, not both. If client wants both to work together, implement bot→human handoff workflow instead of parallel calling. Coordinate timing and ensure CSM stays closely involved during campaign transitions.

kb_faqs.question: How should TTS word replacements handle homophones and subwords?
kb_faqs.answer: Use whole-word matching, not substring matching, to avoid unintended replacements. Example: replacing 'do' → 'डू' should not affect 'does', 'done', etc. Maintain case sensitivity in replacements (e.g., 'US' for United States, 'us' for the pronoun). Test word replacements against common words in your script to catch conflicts before deployment.

kb_faqs.question: What key metrics should be in the business case before signing an annual deal?
kb_faqs.answer: 1) Baseline metrics (client's human-only CPC, conversion %, volume) 2) Expected bot CPC and conversion % from pilot 3) Timeline and volume to reach ROI breakeven 4) Hybrid model cost comparison (bot + human follow-up vs human-only) 5) Adoption targets (% of leads handled by bot in Month 1, 3, 6) 6) Success KPIs and how they'll be measured

kb_faqs.question: What's the typical timeline from pilot completion to contract signature?
kb_faqs.answer: After successful pilot metrics are achieved and final numbers are confirmed, a client decision call should happen within 1-2 days. From decision to contract signature typically takes 3-7 days pending legal/finance review. Momentum is critical—delay hurts closing likelihood. Have the signature-ready contract prepared before the final metrics call.

kb_faqs.question: How can we improve bot engagement on leads that are initially skeptical or cold?
kb_faqs.answer: Use empathy-first approach: validate their current situation, ask permission-based questions, and be clear about bot limitations. If they're not interested, hand off to support/customer service rather than pushing harder. This builds goodwill and may convert them later. Focus bot energy on warm/hot leads and use humans for leads showing mild interest (warm).

kb_faqs.question: What should we do if a deployment shows unexpected behavior just before a critical client decision call?
kb_faqs.answer: Pause immediately and fix before the call. Even one bad call (e.g., TTS issue, skipped introduction) can tank client confidence. Run a UAT test of all critical flows, confirm prompt is correct, and do a live test call from a customer number if possible. Brief the client on the issue and fix, then reschedule the call—transparency builds trust more than rushing a bad demo.

kb_faqs.question: What integration capability should we confirm before client kickoff?
kb_faqs.answer: Confirm: 1) Lead ingestion method (CSV upload, API, CRM sync) 2) Required custom fields and how they map to bot variables 3) Call logging destination (phone, CRM, Sense dashboard) 4) Callback/recording delivery (email, webhook, SFDC) 5) Any custom integrations needed (dialers, payment systems) 6) Data security/PII handling requirements. Document all in a tech spec before kickoff.

kb_faqs.question: What's the best way to collect insights from multiple pilots to improve the product?
kb_faqs.answer: Create a systematic process: 1) Document every issue from each pilot (bug, UX problem, configuration challenge) 2) Categorize by severity and frequency 3) Aggregate across all pilots to identify patterns 4) Prioritize fixes based on impact to multiple clients 5) Create Jira tickets with real examples from pilots 6) Share learnings with product team for roadmap prioritization.

kb_faqs.question: What's the minimum viable CPC we should target for Sense to be competitive with human agents?
kb_faqs.answer: Target CPC should be 10-15% lower than client's human-only baseline to justify bot investment and setup cost. Example: if client's human-only CPC is Rs 90-100, aim for bot+human hybrid CPC of Rs 80-85. Going below this may indicate over-optimization and potential quality loss. Benchmark against client's actual performance, not industry averages.

kb_faqs.question: What are typical connectivity rates when clients switch between different telephony providers?
kb_faqs.answer: Connectivity rates fluctuate significantly when switching telephony integrations. Clients should standardize on one provider (e.g., Exotel vs Vobiz) to allow proper number whitelisting and avoid backup number usage. Switching providers mid-pilot delays whitelisting timelines by 7+ days and creates uncertainty around call completion rates. Best practice: commit to one telephony provider for the entire pilot duration and coordinate whitelisting in advance.

kb_faqs.question: What causes STT (speech-to-text) failures in regional languages like Tamil?
kb_faqs.answer: STT transcription fails in regional languages when the Sarvam STT integration encounters language-specific edge cases. This is a known technical limitation that requires ticket creation and product team triage. Workaround: test STT quality in UAT with 5-10 real calls in the target language before go-live. Root cause is typically language model limitations rather than configuration errors.

kb_faqs.question: Why does the bot fail to respond for 10+ seconds after customer interruption?
kb_faqs.answer: Bot response delays after customer interruption occur when the message queue gets cleared or the bot's state machine doesn't properly recover from interruptions. This is not a normal behavior when the bot is interrupted—even after customer responds, the bot should respond within 2-3 seconds. Root cause requires technical review of queue handling and state management. Create ticket for product team to investigate call flow logs in detail.

kb_faqs.question: How should opening statements in bot messages be formatted to avoid incomplete phrases?
kb_faqs.answer: Opening statements should always be complete sentences. Avoid incomplete phrases like 'Have 2 mins?' and instead generate 'Do you have 2 minutes?' System prompts were updated to enforce this rule. This issue was observed across multiple clients (e.g., Miles Education) and has been fixed in the central system prompt. Test 20+ calls post-fix to confirm language completeness before declaring resolved.

kb_faqs.question: What system prompt changes improve language-switching accuracy in multilingual campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: System prompt modifications targeting language-switching accuracy should address: (1) forcing complete opening statements instead of fragments, and (2) ensuring proper language selection based on customer response. Test changes on 20 calls minimum, measuring both language-switch accuracy and opening-message completeness. Example: Miles Education received prompt updates specifically for education-domain language switching.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What constitutes a high-quality lead CSV for a pilot campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: High-quality lead CSVs must include: (1) valid phone numbers with correct country codes, (2) customer names or identifiers for personalization, (3) lead source tracking (fresh vs. old leads), (4) timestamp of lead creation, and (5) any available customer context (education level, previous interactions). Avoid: duplicate phone numbers, missing required fields, inconsistent data formats. Best practice: validate CSV in UAT with 100-200 leads before running full campaign.

kb_faqs.question: What is a typical qualification rate from bot-attempted leads to sales-ready leads?
kb_faqs.answer: Across client pilots, typical qualification funnel is: 100 attempted leads → 50 connected → 25 meaningful/hot/warm leads → 5 passed to sales team → 1-2 final conversions. This varies by industry: education verticals see ~21% qualify from 100 touches, while others see 5-10%. Qualification % depends heavily on bot script quality, customer fit, and definition of 'qualified'. Always get baseline human-agent qualification rates from client's current process for comparison.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: How should NBA (Next Best Action) logic handle DND (Do Not Disturb) hours and cooldown periods?
kb_faqs.answer: NBA should respect configured DND hours and post-attempt cooldown periods. If a call attempt fails at 6:30 PM with DND start at 8 PM and 14-hour cooldown, NBA should ideally generate WhatsApp before 8 PM, not wait until next morning. Current behavior: NBA generates after cooldown expires (8:30 AM next day), missing the pre-DND window. This requires product review to determine if WhatsApp NBA should bypass cooldown or if cooldown logic needs adjustment for multi-channel strategies.

kb_faqs.question: When should the bot send WhatsApp messages vs. attempting phone calls in a multi-channel campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: WhatsApp should be prioritized for leads that didn't pick up phone calls, especially if those calls failed during DND hours. In the Leap Scholar pilot, ~10.5% of demo bookings came from WhatsApp-only conversations, while 10.5% came from multi-channel engagement. WhatsApp shows higher engagement when phone attempts have failed. Recommendation: auto-send WhatsApp before entering post-attempt cooldown, and use it as the primary retry channel during DND hours.

kb_faqs.question: What ROI metrics should be included in a pilot conclusion deck?
kb_faqs.answer: ROI decks must include: (1) total leads attempted, (2) connectivity rate (%), (3) meaningful lead rate (%), (4) bookings/conversions generated, (5) cost per lead (Rs.), (6) cost per booking, (7) revenue impact (if available), (8) comparison to human agent baseline where available. Define all metrics clearly—e.g., 'Xyz 15-second pulses' instead of just 'pulses'. Include confidence levels if data is incomplete. Avoid overstating results without full campaign completion.

kb_faqs.question: How should you handle benchmark data when a client has never run rechurn campaigns on old leads?
kb_faqs.answer: If client has never run agent outreach on old leads, establish a business case based on cost-to-acquire (Y) vs. revenue generated (X). Aim for 2-3x ROI (X = 2-3× Y) depending on product margin. Example: client sent 40k leads in December, human agents converted ~4k (10% conversion). Without agent baseline on old leads, use the 10% human conversion as upper bound and aim to match or exceed it with bot. Present pilot as exploratory, not as replacement, until benchmarks are established.

kb_faqs.question: What is the expected TAT (turnaround time) for handing off qualified leads from bot to human agents?
kb_faqs.answer: Lead handoff TAT should be <2 hours for hot leads and <24 hours for warm leads. When human agents receive qualified leads, they should attempt contact within same business day. Delays in handoff (>4 hours) reduce conversion probability by ~15-20% due to lead freshness decay. CRM integration (e.g., Vyapar) should auto-push qualified leads with lead-interest metadata to minimize manual handoff steps. Monitor handoff completion rate weekly.

kb_faqs.question: What is the most effective bot response when a customer mentions they are driving or in an emergency?
kb_faqs.answer: Bot should immediately thank the customer and disconnect the call without prolonging conversation. Previously, bots would continue engaging for several seconds before disconnecting, creating safety risks. Recommendation: add explicit system prompt instruction to recognize safety-critical keywords ('driving', 'emergency', 'accident') and trigger immediate disconnect. This should be added to the 8-Pillar system prompt framework to ensure consistent safety handling across all campaigns.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot handle customer objections about discount codes or repeated requests?
kb_faqs.answer: Bot should acknowledge the objection, provide a one-time response, and then gracefully close the call rather than looping through the same response. Example: if customer repeatedly asks for discount code, bot should say 'I've provided the discount code [XYZ]. I'm going to close this call now—please reach out to support if you have further questions.' Previously, bots would loop on discount code explanations, frustrating customers. Add escalation/closure logic to system prompt when facing repeated objections.

kb_faqs.question: What are the critical blockers that delay campaign go-live?
kb_faqs.answer: Common blockers: (1) WABA card integration—client infosec concerns prevent using client's card or Convin's card (delays 1-2 weeks), (2) missing bot script or training data from client (delays 3-5 days), (3) telephony number whitelisting (delays 5-7 days), (4) unresolved technical issues in UAT (delays 3-7 days). Mitigation: create pre-campaign checklist covering all integration, script, and compliance items 2 weeks before planned launch. Escalate blockers to account manager immediately.

kb_faqs.question: What pre-campaign checklist items should be completed before launching a Convin Sense pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: Pre-launch checklist: (1) Lead CSV validated (100-200 rows in UAT), (2) Bot script finalized and reviewed by stakeholder, (3) WABA integration approved and configured, (4) Telephony provider selected and numbers whitelisted, (5) Wallet top-up confirmed (minimum 3-5 days of spend), (6) DND hours configured per country/time zone, (7) Human handoff process documented, (8) KT session completed with agent team, (9) Sample call recordings reviewed, (10) Escalation workflow defined. Complete 7-10 days before go-live.

kb_faqs.question: What should the wallet top-up strategy be before a campaign launch?
kb_faqs.answer: Top up wallet with 3-5 days of expected spend buffer to avoid mid-campaign interruptions. Example: if expected daily spend is Rs. 5,000, top up with Rs. 25,000 minimum. Monitor wallet daily during first week and top up before balance drops below 2 days of spend. Auto-pause features should trigger at 10% balance remaining. Brief client on top-up process and set up billing alerts to CSM so no campaign pauses due to wallet depletion.

kb_faqs.question: How many UAT cycles should be completed before go-live?
kb_faqs.answer: Minimum 2-3 UAT cycles recommended: (1) Functional testing (lead flow, bot responses, handoff), (2) Integration testing (WABA, telephony, CRM), (3) Load testing (100+ leads, concurrent calls). Each cycle should include 50-100 test leads. First cycle takes 3-5 days, second takes 2-3 days, third takes 1-2 days if issues are minor. Total UAT timeline: 7-10 days. Do not skip UAT even for time-sensitive pilots—it reduces go-live bugs by 60-70%.

kb_faqs.question: What is the typical demo-to-conversion rate in a sales cycle?
kb_faqs.answer: Varies significantly by vertical: education verticals see 2-3% final conversion from initial leads (100 leads → ~2-3 sales), while field sales heavy verticals see 5-15% depending on sales team quality. For demo-to-enrollment specifically: typical rate is 10-30% (10 demos → 1-3 enrollments). This requires client's historical human-agent conversion data to benchmark. Never assume bot conversion will match human rates initially—expect 50-70% of human rates in month 1, improving to 80-90% by month 3.

kb_faqs.question: Should the system prompt be modified to handle safety-critical scenarios like driving or emergencies?
kb_faqs.answer: Yes. Add explicit instruction to system prompt to immediately thank and disconnect when customer mentions safety-critical situations. This prevents bot from continuing conversation, reducing liability and improving customer safety perception. Implementation: add to 8-Pillar system prompt as a dedicated safety handler. This is a feature request that should be standardized across all clients, not a one-off workaround.

kb_faqs.question: What should be done if a client requests Lead Intake form integration mid-pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: Avoid Lead Intake integration if significant bot flow modifications are already planned. Reason: integration effort is 5-7 days, and if bot quality issues remain, integration effort is wasted. Instead: (1) offer as post-pilot enhancement, (2) complete core bot quality improvements first, (3) then add integrations in phase 2. Document client's expectation gap early—if they expect 95%+ accuracy, be clear that's not realistic for month-1 pilots. Better to set expectations low and over-deliver.

kb_faqs.question: Why is the 'Lead Interest' field showing as NA/Blank for not-connected calls in CRM integrations?
kb_faqs.answer: Lead Interest field is not populated when bot fails to connect with customer. This breaks CRM workflows that rely on interest-level filtering. Root cause: lead-interest classification logic only triggers post-connection; if call never connects, no interest signal is generated. Fix: populate Lead Interest as 'Not Attempted' or 'Not Connected' to maintain data integrity in CRM push. This impacts clients with direct CRM integrations (e.g., Vyapar) who consume lead data directly.
