Alternatively, assign human agents to confirm meetings, but ensure they use Convin Sense Click2Call to maintain conversation visibility and avoid parallel dialing outside the platform.

kb_faqs.question: What is a typical AI bot connectivity rate vs. human agent connectivity rate?
kb_faqs.answer: AI bot connectivity rates are typically 40–60% on first attempt (depending on lead quality, time of day, and DND list accuracy). Human agents on qualified warm/hot leads achieve 80–86% connectivity with 6–10 attempts. AI excels at handling high-volume first screening and qualification; humans excel at closing and objection handling on warm leads. A blended approach (AI for initial contact + human for hot leads) typically yields the best ROI. Measure connectivity separately for AI calling and human follow-up to identify bottlenecks.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: Why should leads be manually reviewed for enrollment status before campaign launch?
kb_faqs.answer: Some clients (e.g., education platforms) may have leads that are already enrolled or previously contacted, making them low-intent. Manual review of a sample (e.g., 35 leads) can reveal if 50%+ are already enrolled, indicating poor lead quality upstream. If > 30% are ineligible, either: (1) request fresh lead lists from the client, (2) reduce the campaign scope, or (3) add an eligibility check in the bot's opening (e.g., 'Are you currently enrolled?'). Document the ineligibility rate and set expectations before running full-scale campaigns.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot classify and score leads for handoff to human agents?
kb_faqs.answer: The bot should classify leads into Hot (high purchase intent, specific need), Warm (interested but needs nurturing), and Cold (no interest or wrong fit). Each classification should be supported by conversation signals (e.g., explicit statements, product interest, timeline clarity). Pass Hot leads immediately to human agents; queue Warm leads for follow-up via WhatsApp or callback scheduling; disqualify Cold leads. Scoring should weight: (1) intent statements, (2) budget/timeline clarity, (3) product fit, and (4) response engagement. Test scoring logic on 100 leads to validate accuracy before scaling; adjust thresholds if too many Warm leads are being marked Hot.

kb_faqs.question: What cost-per-acquisition (CPA) and conversion rate benchmarks should be expected?
kb_faqs.answer: CPA depends on use case: sales/lead gen pilots typically achieve 20–35% qualified lead rate and CPA of ₹50–300 depending on product and geography. Conversion (lead to meeting booked) rates are typically 5–15% for AI-only and 20–40% when combined with human follow-up. ROI is positive when AI CPA < human agent CPA by 40%+. Clients should provide baseline human agent metrics (conversion %, CPA) from the last 3 months for fair comparison. One pilot's CPA was 'CMA vs. CPA confusion,' indicating need for clear definition upfront: are we measuring cost-per-lead, cost-per-qualified-lead, or cost-per-conversion?

kb_faqs.question: How should variable data (e.g., deal returns, product availability) be handled if it changes mid-campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: Ideal: integrate the bot with the client's API so data updates push automatically. Workaround (if no integration): manually re-upload CSV with updated data and pause/resume the campaign (adds delay). For pilots without integration, negotiate update frequency with the client (e.g., 'We can accept one CSV update per day max'). If data changes every few hours (e.g., stock/pricing), recommend building integration. One client (Grip Invest) compared manual CSV updates vs. integrated updates in parallel pilots; integrated approach showed better conversion as data is always fresh.

kb_faqs.question: Can the bot run on a client's existing dialer instead of Convin's dialer?
kb_faqs.answer: Yes; this requires integration with the client's telephony system (e.g., Genesys, Ubona). Clients prefer their own dialer for number portability and control, but integration adds 2–4 weeks of development. For pilots, Convin can provide numbers from Exotel (140xx/160xx series) without integration. If a client insists on their dialer during pilot (e.g., Kotak with Ubona), integration becomes mandatory. Best practice: delay dialer integration to post-pilot phase to avoid blocking campaign launch. Document integration blockers (e.g., missing infosec requirements) upfront.

kb_faqs.question: What is the V4 prompt framework, and when should it be used?
kb_faqs.answer: The V4 prompt is a standardized system prompt template used for bot training across pilots. It includes: (1) bot persona/tone, (2) conversation flow guardrails, (3) qualification criteria, (4) product knowledge, and (5) escalation logic. Use V4 for all new pilots unless client requires heavy customization. The product team maintains a shared document with the latest V4 prompt; always use the current version to ensure consistency. Customizations should be minimal and tested on 10–20 calls before scaling.

kb_faqs.question: What UAT volume is typically required before going live?
kb_faqs.answer: Start with 50–100 leads for initial UAT to validate core flows (greeting, qualification, objection handling, handoff). If critical issues are found, fix and re-test on another 50 leads. Once UAT passes, expand to 200–500 leads as a 'warm-up' phase before full production volume (1000+). One pilot expanded UAT from 50 to 100 leads to increase confidence. UAT should complete in 2–5 business days; if it stretches beyond 1 week, investigate blockers (e.g., integration delays, prompt issues).

kb_faqs.question: What is the go-live readiness checklist?
kb_faqs.answer: Before go-live: (1) UAT passed with 0 P0 bugs, (2) lead CSV validated and uploaded, (3) bot persona and prompts signed off, (4) integration (if applicable) tested end-to-end, (5) human agent training completed (e.g., handoff process, CRM logging), (6) wallet balance topped up, (7) WhatsApp templates approved (if using WhatsApp), (8) DND list confirmed, (9) test calls run with 10+ sample leads, (10) internal stakeholders (PM, Sales, CSM) aligned on success metrics and handoff TAT. Check off each item with owner before campaign launch.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot handle product recommendations when product ID must be sent via API?
kb_faqs.answer: The bot must: (1) identify the correct product from knowledge base via RAG or prompt context, (2) extract/store the product ID internally (not mentioned in conversation), (3) send product ID via API call when passing to external system (e.g., 'add to cart'). If the API call does not include product ID, conversation context is lost. Issue: bot may recommend a product verbally but fail to send the ID via API, breaking the integration. Test this flow end-to-end: (1) run bot conversation, (2) check API logs to confirm product ID was passed, (3) verify ID matches the recommended product. If mismatch occurs, debug prompt logic and RAG similarity scoring.

kb_faqs.question: What causes the bot to recommend products outside the knowledge base?
kb_faqs.answer: LLMs are prone to hallucination, especially when: (1) KB is not prioritized in the system prompt, (2) RAG similarity threshold is too low, (3) product data in KB is vague or incomplete, (4) bot has access to external training data (e.g., website). Solutions: (1) embed complete product list in system prompt (top of context), (2) increase RAG similarity threshold (test on 50+ calls), (3) add explicit guardrail in prompt ('ONLY recommend products in the attached list'), (4) use structured output format (JSON schema) to force valid product IDs. One client tested 70 calls and found hallucinations in 40%; after moving products to RAG and raising similarity threshold, accuracy improved to 60%, indicating need for iterative tuning.

kb_faqs.question: What are the most common objections raised by prospects in pilots?
kb_faqs.answer: Common objections include: (1) 'I'm already using a competitor' (address differentiation), (2) 'I don't have budget right now' (ask about timeline, offer follow-up), (3) 'This is a bad time to call' (offer callback, log preference), (4) 'I'm not interested' (probe for reason, attempt brief re-engagement), (5) 'How did you get my number?' (explain source, offer opt-out). Train the bot to acknowledge each objection, ask 1–2 follow-up questions to understand root cause, and then either re-engage or mark for human agent. Test objection handling on 20–30 calls; if bot struggles with specific objections (e.g., competitor comparison), flag to conversation design team.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot respond to 'I'm busy right now' or 'Call me later'?
kb_faqs.answer: Do not immediately end the call; instead: (1) acknowledge ('I understand, I'll keep it brief'), (2) ask when is a better time ('When would be a good time tomorrow or later this week?'), (3) if prospect insists, offer callback scheduling via WhatsApp or email. Callback scheduling via the bot (using calendar/WhatsApp link) reduces human agent workload. If using callback, ensure the bot sends the booking confirmation to the prospect immediately. This objection is high-intent (prospect is NOT saying 'no,' just 'wrong time'), so effective callback scheduling can recover 30–50% of these leads.

kb_faqs.question: Why are some leads not being reached despite multiple retry attempts?
kb_faqs.answer: Reasons include: (1) invalid or outdated phone numbers, (2) lead is on DND list (Do-Not-Call registry), (3) wrong time zone / calling outside business hours, (4) network issues or carrier blocks, (5) lead opted out. To improve reach: (1) validate numbers against DND list before upload, (2) adjust calling window to prospect time zone, (3) use staggered retry attempts (e.g., once at 9am, once at 2pm, once at 6pm), (4) implement warm-up before cold campaigns (call a warm list first to test delivery). Track connectivity rate separately for each attempt to identify drop-off points.

kb_faqs.question: What is DND (Do Not Call) list handling, and how should it be configured?
kb_faqs.answer: DND lists (regulatory registries like NCCR in India) block outbound calls to numbers registered to opt out. Before uploading leads: (1) cross-check CSV against official DND list, (2) remove DND numbers, (3) set campaign DND check to 'enabled' (default) to catch any missed numbers. If the bot calls a DND number and prospect complains, it damages brand reputation. One incident: bot attempted to call a DND number despite agent saying he would call it. Ensure DND check is enforced at campaign level and validated in logs before sending calls.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot be trained for different prospect personas or use cases?
kb_faqs.answer: For multi-use-case pilots, create separate bot instances or conversation flows for each use case (e.g., 'new customer' vs. 'existing customer' vs. 'NDR/collections'). Customize the system prompt for each persona with: (1) tone (formal/casual), (2) opening script, (3) key talking points, (4) objection handling, (5) CTAs. Train each bot on 10–20 test calls before expanding to production. One pilot wanted to add a second use case mid-campaign; this was deprioritized because: (1) it requires separate bot building (~50% effort overhead), (2) pilot scope already stretched, (3) better to focus on optimizing the first use case and then expand. Document the decision to avoid scope creep.

kb_faqs.question: What should the bot say if it doesn't understand the prospect's response?
kb_faqs.answer: Instead of repeating the question verbatim, the bot should: (1) acknowledge ('Got it, let me confirm...'), (2) offer clarification options ('Are you interested in A, B, or C?'), (3) if still unclear after 2 attempts, escalate to human agent. Avoid: 'I didn't understand, can you repeat?' as it frustrates prospects. One pilot observed: bot asked 'What's your reason for interest?' and heard garbled response; instead of re-asking, it guessed wrong (heard 'muscle gain' instead of 'routine'). Fix: (1) add common phrases to word-boost, (2) if confidence is low, offer explicit options rather than asking open-ended questions.

kb_faqs.question: How should callback scheduling be handled if the prospect can't talk now?
kb_faqs.answer: Best practice: (1) ask when is a good time ('Tomorrow 10am or 2pm?'), (2) if prospect picks a time, send WhatsApp with calendar link (ICS file or direct link), (3) log the scheduled callback in CRM, (4) have a separate bot or human agent confirm 1 hour before scheduled time. Callback scheduling via bot reduces human agent workload. Test callback flow on 10 leads to ensure: (1) WhatsApp message is sent correctly, (2) prospect can access calendar link, (3) reminder is sent on time. If WhatsApp fails, fallback to email or SMS.

kb_faqs.question: Why did the system go down, and what communication was sent to clients?
kb_faqs.answer: A cloud infrastructure issue (Kubernetes cluster failure) brought down demo domain and webhooks on Mar 11, 1:13 PM. Impact: (1) no new campaigns could be triggered, (2) clients could not test demos, (3) webhook endpoints returned errors to clients pushing leads. ETA for recovery: 45 min to 1 hour. Communication: (1) internal teams notified immediately, (2) CSM team advised customers to hold lead uploads, (3) demo rescheduled. Outage lasted ~1 hour; all services restored by 2:35 PM. Post-incident: implement status page so clients can self-service during incidents.

kb_faqs.question: What should be done if the bot incorrectly marks a DND number as eligible?
kb_faqs.answer: This is a critical compliance issue. Immediate steps: (1) pull the lead record and call transcript, (2) confirm DND status, (3) create incident ticket, (4) check if multiple leads were affected, (5) audit the DND validation logic in the campaign. Root cause is typically: (1) DND check is disabled in campaign settings, (2) lead was added before DND check was enabled, or (3) number was not on DND list at time of upload but was added later. Fix: (1) enable DND check at campaign level (default), (2) validate DND status for all leads in bulk before upload, (3) train CSMs to verify DND list freshness. One incident: bot called a DND number; agent reported it; must implement call-back verification post-incident.
When should human agents be brought in to follow up, and what is the handoff process?
kb_faqs.question: Bring human agents in for: (1) Hot leads (high intent), (2) technical objections that bot cannot resolve, (3) pricing negotiations, (4) deal closures. Handoff process: (1) bot says 'Let me connect you with an agent' + pause 2–3 sec, (2) warm transfer via Click2Call or IVR, (3) agent logs into CRM, (4) bot provides context via call notes/transcript. Handoff TAT should be < 30 sec. Post-handoff: agent should acknowledge context ('I see you're interested in X') to avoid re-explaining. If handoff fails (no agent available), offer callback with wait time ('Agent will call in 5 minutes'). Measure handoff success rate as a KPI.
What training is required for human agents before bot campaigns launch?
kb_faqs.answer: Human agent training should cover: (1) bot handoff process (how to receive warm transfer, what context is provided), (2) lead qualification criteria (what 'hot' vs. 'warm' means), (3) CRM logging (how to log outcomes, next steps), (4) objection handling (best rebuttals for common objections), (5) escalation path (when to escalate to manager), (6) DND/compliance rules (never call DND, document opt-outs). Training session: 2–3 hours + 5–10 supervised calls. One pilot: agents received calls from bot but didn't have context, leading to rough handoffs; after training, handoff quality improved. Include scenario-based training (e.g., 'Prospect says budget is tight—how do you respond?').

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: How is NBA (Next Best Action) generated, and what factors influence the retry decision?
kb_faqs.answer: NBA uses LLM-based logic to determine if a lead should be retried, when, and via which channel (call, WhatsApp, email). Factors: (1) reason for last interaction (e.g., 'prospect busy' vs. 'not interested'), (2) lead quality score, (3) previous engagement history, (4) time since last attempt, (5) channel effectiveness (e.g., WhatsApp conversion rate). NBA is more intelligent than fixed retries but slower to compute. For clients who want hourly retries on all leads (e.g., NDR), NBA is insufficient; fixed retry logic must be used instead. Mixing NBA + fixed logic requires careful tuning to avoid double-calling the same lead.

kb_faqs.question: What should be confirmed with the client before campaign launch?
kb_faqs.answer: Pre-launch confirmation checklist: (1) Lead volume and source (where are they getting leads from?), (2) Use case and success metric (what does 'success' look like?), (3) Campaign window (how long will the campaign run?), (4) Calling hours and time zone, (5) Budget/CPA target, (6) Handoff process (who receives warm transfers?), (7) Compliance requirements (DND, TCPA, data privacy), (8) Integration dependencies (CRM, dialer, WhatsApp), (9) Reporting cadence (daily, weekly). Misalignment on any of these will cause delays post-launch. Create a campaign brief document signed by client and internal team before proceeding to UAT.

kb_faqs.question: What is the 'intro line broken' issue, and how does it impact the conversation?
kb_faqs.answer: The intro line (bot's opening greeting) was observed to be malformed or incomplete in some calls, e.g., starting mid-sentence or cutting off. This breaks the first impression and causes prospects to hang up. Root cause: likely TTS/LLM generation error or audio processing lag. Fix: (1) review the exact intro line in system prompt, (2) ensure it's concise (< 20 words), (3) test on 10 calls to confirm clarity, (4) if issue persists, check audio encoding/TTS settings. One pilot (Miles Education) reported intro line as broken; this was included in a critical issues list alongside context loss and language switching.

kb_faqs.question: Why does the bot sometimes switch languages unexpectedly (e.g., English to Hindi)?
kb_faqs.answer: Language switching typically occurs when: (1) system prompt doesn't explicitly lock language, (2) bot detects prospect speaking a different language and tries to match, (3) training data has multilingual examples. Fix: (1) add explicit instruction in system prompt ('ALWAYS respond in [English]'), (2) if supporting multiple languages, create separate bots per language, (3) avoid mixing languages in training data. One pilot (Miles Education) reported random language switching to Hindi; this was a critical issue. Test language consistency on 20 calls to ensure bot never switches mid-conversation unless explicitly requested.

kb_faqs.question: What is 'context loss and repetition,' and how does it affect conversation quality?
kb_faqs.answer: The bot loses context mid-conversation (e.g., forgets what prospect said 2 turns ago) and repeats questions or statements already covered. This frustrates prospects and breaks trust. Root cause: (1) context window is too small (truncating conversation history), (2) LLM model is weak (e.g., older GPT version), (3) conversation state is not properly maintained. Fix: (1) increase context window size (if using LLM), (2) upgrade to stronger model (GPT-4 vs. 3.5), (3) add explicit instruction to store key facts (name, product interest, budget) in a structured slot/variable, (4) test on 20 calls for repetition. One pilot reported context loss as a critical issue; auditing showed bot repeating 'What's your budget?' after prospect had already stated it.

kb_faqs.question: What is 'latency and slow speech' issue in bot calls?
kb_faqs.answer: Bot responds slowly (> 3 second delay) or speaks unnaturally slowly (< 0.8x speed). This breaks the flow and prospects hang up. Causes: (1) LLM inference latency (slow model or overloaded), (2) TTS processing delay, (3) network/codec issues. Fixes: (1) use faster LLM (GPT-4 Mini instead of full GPT-4), (2) optimize system prompt for brevity (fewer tokens = faster inference), (3) upgrade TTS provider (e.g., Google Wavenet is faster than some alternatives), (4) reduce concurrency if overloaded. Target: < 1 second response time, speech speed 0.9–1.0x. One pilot reported slow speech; after optimizing prompt, latency dropped from 5s to 2s.

kb_faqs.question: What is 'callback scheduling errors,' and how can it be fixed?
kb_faqs.answer: Bot schedules a callback time/date with prospect but fails to send confirmation (WhatsApp/email) or log it in CRM, causing prospect to miss the callback or double-booked agents. Root cause: (1) calendar/scheduling API integration failed silently, (2) WhatsApp template not approved by Meta, (3) CRM field mapping is incorrect. Fix: (1) test callback flow end-to-end before scaling (schedule 10 test callbacks), (2) add explicit logs for each step (prospect confirms time → system generates link → WhatsApp sent → CRM logged), (3) implement fallback (if WhatsApp fails, send email), (4) add reminder automation (1 hour before callback). One pilot reported callback scheduling errors; diagnosis showed WhatsApp template was not approved, causing all callbacks to fail silently.

kb_faqs.question: Why does the bot loop indefinitely with message like 'let me check the product catalog'?
kb_faqs.answer: Bot enters an infinite loop when: (1) external API (e.g., recommendation/product lookup) hangs or returns timeout, (2) bot's error handling doesn't timeout properly, (3) RAG query returns no results and bot retries infinitely. Fix: (1) implement strict timeout on all API calls (5 sec max), (2) add fallback response if API fails ('I couldn't fetch that just now, let me check and follow up via WhatsApp'), (3) limit retry attempts to 1–2 instead of infinite, (4) add explicit error message in logs. One pilot experienced this on product recommendation calls; after setting 5-sec timeout on recommendation API, bot either fetched product or provided fallback within 6 sec total.

kb_faqs.question: What is the 'CPA vs. CMA confusion' issue?
kb_faqs.answer: CPA (Cost Per Acquisition) and CMA (Cost Per Lead/Cost Per Contact Made) are different metrics but clients/teams sometimes conflate them. CPA measures cost to close a deal; CMA measures cost to reach a prospect. One pilot's audit report flagged 'CPA vs. CMA confusion,' indicating the client was not clear on which metric to track. Fix: (1) define metrics clearly in the campaign brief ('We are measuring CPA = cost per qualified lead'), (2) set targets upfront (e.g., 'Target CPA < ₹200'), (3) track separately in reports. Do not mix metrics; it confuses ROI calculations.

kb_faqs.question: Why does WhatsApp message not go after customer responds?
kb_faqs.answer: WhatsApp message fails to send post-response when: (1) conversation context was not saved correctly, (2) bot incorrectly marks conversation as 'ended' after response, (3) WhatsApp session expired (24-hour limit). Fix: (1) maintain conversation state properly (mark as 'open' until bot explicitly closes), (2) ensure session ID carries forward across messages, (3) check for 24-hour session expiry and re-initiate if needed. One incident: customer responded to bot's question, but bot did not send follow-up WhatsApp message. Diagnosis: bot's state was marked as 'closed' after first response. After fixing state management, follow-up messages sent correctly.

kb_faqs.question: Should we allow clients to add personal Gmail IDs for system access?
kb_faqs.answer: No; do not allow personal Gmail IDs due to: (1) security/compliance risks (data exposure if employee leaves), (2) audit trail breaks (personal vs. corporate account), (3) license management issues. Workaround: push back professionally ('We can't provide access to personal emails due to data security policy') and either: (1) whitelist their corporate domain in Convin's authentication, (2) create bulk single sign-on (SSO) for their organization, (3) provide temporary guest access (limited to demos, expires after X days). One client (Kotak) requested Gmail access; after explanation, internal team agreed to disable personal email access and use corporate SSO instead.

kb_faqs.question: How can the bot determine if a prospect is already a customer or enrolled?
kb_faqs.answer: Add an eligibility check at the start: 'Are you currently using [product]?' or 'Are you already enrolled with us?' If 'yes,' offer to transfer to support instead of sales. This filters out already-enrolled leads early and improves efficiency. Alternatively, integrate bot with CRM to query customer status automatically. If > 30% of leads are ineligible, flag to account team to improve list quality. One pilot found 50% of leads were already enrolled; adding eligibility check at start reduced wasted calls and improved conversion on fresh leads.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot respond to 'How did you get my number?'
kb_faqs.answer: Be transparent and compliant: 'We have a partnership with [source] to reach professionals in your field' or 'You signed up for our newsletter, and this is a follow-up.' If prospect opts out, respect it immediately: 'I'll remove you from future calls. Is there anything I can help with today?' Do NOT be evasive, as it damages trust. Add opt-out link to WhatsApp message for easy unsubscribe. One pilot lacked this in the bot's script, causing some prospects to hang up immediately.

kb_faqs.question: Should the bot attempt to re-engage prospects who say 'I'm not interested'?
kb_faqs.answer: Yes, attempt light re-engagement: 'I understand. Just curious—is it because of X or Y?' Then pivot based on answer (e.g., if it's price, offer discount; if it's wrong product, offer alternative). If prospect says 'no thanks' again, respect it and mark as 'not interested'—do NOT continue pushing. Re-engagement success rate is typically 10–20% on 'not interested' leads. If done too aggressively, it harms brand. Test re-engagement script on 20 calls to calibrate tone.

kb_faqs.question: What is the process for reviewing a client's existing lead list for quality issues?
kb_faqs.answer: Before uploading to campaign: (1) sample 5–10% of leads (e.g., 50 out of 500), (2) validate phone format (11 digits for India, etc.), (3) check for duplicates (multiple entries for same lead), (4) cross-check against DND list, (5) manually review 35 leads to spot ineligibility (e.g., already enrolled, wrong industry), (6) document findings in audit report. If ineligibility rate > 20%, escalate to account team and request fresh list. One pilot found 57% ineligibility rate; campaign was delayed 1 week to get clean leads.

kb_faqs.question: How does the bot create 'second bot' for follow-up on scheduled meetings?
kb_faqs.answer: Option 1: Create a separate campaign/bot instance with a different system prompt designed for meeting confirmations ('Hi, confirming our call tomorrow at 2pm—are you still available?'). Option 2: Add follow-up logic to the existing bot post-booking (e.g., 24 hours before meeting, send WhatsApp reminder + optional callback). Option 1 is cleaner as it keeps bot instances focused. Example: Bot A books meetings → Bot B confirms 1 day before. Requires ~50% more build effort but improves show-up rate from ~60% to ~80%.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot handle time zone differences when calling prospects?
kb_faqs.answer: Detect prospect time zone and adjust calling window accordingly. For India-based outreach: call between 9am–6pm in prospect's local time zone (e.g., if prospect is in US, don't call before 8pm IST). Include time zone in lead CSV if available, or detect from area code. One pilot's default calling window was 9am–6pm IST but 40% of prospects were in US, causing low connectivity. After segmenting by time zone and staggering calls, connectivity improved to 65%.

kb_faqs.question: Why is the 'restore' button not working in knowledge base version history?
kb_faqs.answer: Version history restore is not functional (reported as a bug). Workaround: (1) manually copy the desired KB version content, (2) paste into current KB, (3) save. Until restore is fixed, keep backup copies of KB externally (Google Docs, etc.). Request ETA for fix from product team; add to roadmap as enhancement. Impact: low if KB is not frequently updated, but critical for clients iterating on bot behavior.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot handle leads that are duplicates or already contacted?
kb_faqs.answer: Add a check at start: 'Have we spoken before?' or integrate with CRM to auto-detect. If duplicate/already contacted, offer: (1) transfer to existing agent handling the lead, (2) check if anything has changed ('Any updates since we last spoke?'), (3) escalate timeline. Duplicate calling wastes resources and frustrates prospects. One pilot's CSV had 15% duplicates; after deduping, cost per lead improved by 18%.

kb_faqs.question: What is the ideal handoff point from bot to human agent?
kb_faqs.answer: Hand off to human when: (1) bot has qualified the lead as 'hot', (2) prospect shows explicit buying intent (e.g., 'How much does it cost?'), (3) technical objection arises (bot cannot answer), (4) pricing negotiation is needed. Do NOT hand off for every question (wastes agent time). Automate: price inquiries (send PDF), basic FAQs, callback scheduling. One pilot handed off too early (after qualification), wasting agent cycles on warm leads that could be nurtured via WhatsApp follow-ups first. After adjustment, agent utilization improved by 25%.

kb_faqs.question: How should leads with scheduled callbacks be managed after bot call?
kb_faqs.answer: Workflow: (1) bot schedules callback and sends WhatsApp confirmation, (2) CRM creates task for human agent or reminder bot, (3) 1 hour before scheduled time, send WhatsApp reminder to prospect, (4) human agent calls at scheduled time. If prospect doesn't pick up on scheduled call, log 'no-show' and add to retry queue. One pilot did not have callback reminders; 40% of scheduled callbacks were missed. After implementing WhatsApp reminders, show-up rate improved to 75%.

kb_faqs.question: What causes bot to send multiple identical messages or repeat the same prompt?
kb_faqs.answer: Bot repeats due to: (1) prompt loop logic error (bot's decision tree always returns to same state), (2) absence of state transition (bot doesn't move to next conversation stage), (3) API returning same response (bot doesn't handle different outcomes). Fix: (1) audit conversation flow logic, (2) add explicit state variables (e.g., 'qualification_status = complete', move to next stage), (3) add max-repeat guardrail ('If asked same question > 2x, escalate to agent'). One pilot's bot asked 'What's your budget?' → prospect answered → bot repeated the same question. After adding state tracking, repetition stopped.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: Can fixed retry logic be combined with NBA (Next Best Action)?
kb_faqs.answer: Yes, but requires careful design to avoid double-calling. Approach: (1) For channels with NBA capability (WhatsApp, Email), use NBA-generated retry. (2) For channels without (phone calls), use fixed retry config ('retry every 4 hours, max 5 attempts'). Ensure the system marks a lead as 'attempted via NBA' so fixed logic doesn't retry the same lead again. Risk: if both run in parallel without coordination, lead may receive duplicate calls within hours. Test with 100 leads to ensure no double-calling before scaling.

kb_faqs.question: What happens if the bot is supposed to call an NDR (non-response, do-not-ring) number but doesn't?
kb_faqs.answer: This is a critical bug. NDR protocol requires: (1) check if number is marked 'do-not-ring' in CRM/system, (2) if yes, do NOT attempt outbound call, (3) only human agent can override after review. If bot calls an NDR number, it damages brand trust. One incident: bot agent said 'I will call this number' but system allowed it despite DND flag. Fix: (1) enforce DND check at bot call initiation, (2) log all DND checks in audit trail, (3) create alert if DND call is attempted. Never rely on manual review alone.

kb_faqs.question: What is the typical lead volume for a pilot campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: Lead volumes vary significantly by client and use case. Typical pilots start with 2,000–5,000 leads for initial testing, with successful clients scaling to 10,000+ leads. Some clients request phased rollouts (e.g., 5k leads initially, then additional volumes based on integration and system performance validation). Always confirm lead volume and quality upfront during the kick-off to avoid delays.

kb_faqs.question: What should be included in the pre-campaign checklist before going live?
kb_faqs.answer: Key items: (1) CSV data quality validation and format check, (2) UAT completion with minimum 100 test leads, (3) Dialer integration confirmation (whether using client's dialer or Convin-managed dialer like Exotel/Elision/Vobiz), (4) WhatsApp WABA account setup and payment method verification, (5) Agent account creation and access verification, (6) DND/regulatory compliance checks, (7) Success metrics and conversion benchmarks clearly documented in writing, (8) Lead handoff SLA and human agent training requirements confirmed.

kb_faqs.question: Why do some pilots have very low connectivity rates?
kb_faqs.answer: Low connectivity (13% in one case with 100 UAT leads) can stem from: (1) High DND (Do Not Disturb) numbers in the dataset—65% DND led to only 13% connections, (2) Purchased lead data with poor freshness or accuracy, (3) Lack of DND override functionality in the client's dialler, (4) WhatsApp integration issues (payment or account setup delays). Ask clients about their internal connectivity benchmark with human agents to set realistic targets. Always validate lead quality early in UAT.

kb_faqs.question: What is a typical connectivity rate for AI outbound calling campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: First-attempt connectivity typically ranges from 13%–35% depending on lead quality and DND compliance. For high-quality, fresh leads (webinar attendees), connectivity can reach 35%+. For purchased data or leads with high DND concentration, expect 13%–20%. Always establish a baseline with human agent performance on the same dataset before comparing bot results.

kb_faqs.question: What percentage of connected leads typically convert to qualified leads?
kb_faqs.answer: Conversion from connected call to qualified lead varies by use case: ~10% of connected leads typically agree to a booked session/demo, with ~5–10% qualification rate overall. For attendance/engagement use cases, 30% of webinar-booked leads also attend. Always align on what 'qualified' means with the client upfront—define it as either bot-qualified or requiring human agent validation, and document baseline human performance for comparison.

kb_faqs.question: How should we handle discrepancies between client-reported conversion metrics and actual pilot results?
kb_faqs.answer: Clients often report higher conversion percentages in kick-off calls than what materializes during pilots (e.g., claimed 40–55% conversion vs. pilot reality of 1–2% loan disbursement). Always document conversion assumptions and baseline numbers in a formal email and get written sign-off. If metrics differ significantly mid-pilot, send an updated email documenting the variance. This protects both parties and enables fair pilot assessment and scope adjustments.

kb_faqs.question: What is the best bot persona and speech pattern for outbound sales calls?
kb_faqs.answer: Effective bots use: (1) Conversational, natural speech with slower delivery rates—speech rate should be optimized to avoid overwhelming prospects, (2) First-level probing and qualification only, avoiding deep complexity or multi-layered logic to reduce hallucination risk, (3) Clear system prompts focusing on warm qualification rather than aggressive upselling, (4) Handling of objections embedded in the flow rather than as reactive responses. Always test speech rate and bot tone with real users during UAT and adjust based on prospect feedback.

kb_faqs.question: Should the bot attempt multiple questions to probe prospect interest, or keep it simple?
kb_faqs.answer: Keep initial probing simple to avoid hallucination and complexity. First-level probing with 3–5 key qualifying questions works best. Avoid adding deeper multi-layered logic or secondary probing in the same campaign—this increases error rates and poor call experiences. If deeper discovery is needed, hand off to a human agent. This approach has delivered better results in pilots where simplicity was prioritized over feature richness.

kb_faqs.question: How should the bot handle reminders or follow-up calls (e.g., day-before and same-day meeting reminders)?
kb_faqs.answer: Avoid suggesting proactively that the bot will handle multiple follow-up campaigns (e.g., separate bots for booking and reminder calls). This requires manual lead shuffling between campaigns and constant refinement, extending pilot timelines. Instead, focus on the primary use case (e.g., booking) and deliver strong results. If the client wants multi-touch, frame it as a post-pilot enhancement or suggest using human agents for reminders to maintain data continuity.

kb_faqs.question: What are the most common objections raised in sales pilots, and how should the bot respond?
kb_faqs.answer: Common objections: 'Not interested in loans/demos', 'Already have a product', 'Price too high'. Best rebuttals: (1) For disinterest: probe briefly on timing or specific blockers before accepting rejection, (2) For existing products: position as comparison/upgrade opportunity, (3) For price: avoid defending price; instead, hand off to human who can discuss ROI or offers. Train bots to acknowledge objections empathetically and transition smoothly to human handoff rather than arguing. Real call audits should identify which rebuttals work vs. trigger more rejections.

kb_faqs.question: What is the typical sales cycle for leads the bot qualifies and hands off to humans?
kb_faqs.answer: Sales cycles vary by product: education/courses typically 1.2–2 days from lead qualification to enrollment, while loan products may take 3–7 days. Always confirm the expected sales cycle in kick-off calls. If there are zero conversions after human follow-up even 2+ days post-handoff, it indicates either poor bot qualification, misaligned lead quality, or unrealistic success metrics. Re-examine qualification definitions and baseline human performance before concluding the bot failed.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What makes a good lead CSV vs. a bad one?
kb_faqs.answer: Good lead CSV: (1) Fresh data (generated within 1–2 weeks), (2) Contact numbers in consistent format (10-digit without country code or fully formatted), (3) Low DND concentration (<30%), (4) Accurate metadata (name, source, webinar attendance status if applicable), (5) Clear deduplication, (6) Data governance info (how many times contacted recently). Bad CSV: (1) Purchased lists with unknown freshness, (2) High DND (>50%), (3) No human agent baseline on same data, (4) Missing or inconsistent phone formats, (5) Recycled leads with high contact history. Always validate a sample with the client's human agents to ensure reasonableness.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: How should we handle lead datasets where 65% of numbers are on DND?
kb_faqs.answer: High DND concentration (65% or more) severely limits connectivity and ROI. Ask the client: (1) Do they have DND override capability in their dialler? (2) Can they provide fresh, non-DND lead segments instead? (3) Are these truly high-intent leads (e.g., recent webinar attendees) or purchased data? If they lack DND override and can only provide DND-heavy lists, reframe success metrics to exclude DND numbers from the denominator, or ask for larger lead volumes (1000+ instead of 100) to get meaningful sample sizes. Document this constraint in writing.

kb_faqs.question: What is the sessionID issue in the GET request tool, and how was it fixed?
kb_faqs.answer: The sessionID was not being automatically included in GET request parameters when calling third-party APIs. Root cause: unclear specification on whether the sessionID should go in URL params or headers. Fix: Product team clarified payload requirements, confirmed sessionID should be in the param/query section, and implemented automatic injection. Testing confirmed the tool now passes sessionID correctly without manual intervention. Resolution took 3–4 hours from identification to production release (AC-1661).

kb_faqs.question: Why does the termination tool not fire when a call is manually disconnected?
kb_faqs.answer: The forced termination tool call only triggers on normal call end, not on manual/abrupt disconnection. This can leave leads in an incomplete state in downstream systems. Workaround: ensure clients understand that manual call cuts may skip cleanup logic; consider adding a fallback termination check on the next bot interaction. Product team should evaluate whether forced tool calls need to execute on all disconnect scenarios, not just graceful call endings.

kb_faqs.question: How do we fix bot hallucinations where the same question receives three different responses?
kb_faqs.answer: Bot inconsistency (same question → multiple different responses) indicates poor prompt engineering or unstable LLM behavior under certain conditions. Root cause: likely overly complex or ambiguous system prompts. Fix: (1) Simplify the system prompt with explicit expected outputs, (2) Add guardrails to lock response templates for key questions, (3) Reduce probing depth to avoid open-ended reasoning, (4) Review LLM temperature and token settings. This bug severely damages first impressions during demos—prioritize it and test thoroughly in UAT before client-facing calls.

kb_faqs.question: What causes WhatsApp messages to not be delivered to some leads?
kb_faqs.answer: Root causes: (1) Campaign not configured to respond after lead is marked complete, (2) Engagement days or daily channel limits exceeded, (3) Payment method issues with WABA account, (4) Leads falling outside active campaign window. Check campaign settings for response toggle, verify engagement days remaining, confirm daily limits haven't been hit, and check payment method status. In one case, a lead marked complete wasn't receiving messages because the campaign was set to not respond post-completion—this is a configurable setting per campaign.

kb_faqs.question: How do we handle WABA payment method failures during campaigns?
kb_faqs.answer: WABA payment issues prevent WhatsApp delivery entirely. Resolution steps: (1) Ask client to try adding an alternate payment card to their WABA account, (2) If card addition fails due to pre-existing card registration on another account, escalate to Meta support or use Convin's payment method as a workaround, (3) Coordinate with implementation team for smooth testing once payment is resolved. Always verify payment method during onboarding UAT to catch this early. Typical resolution time: 1–2 days.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: When should the bot retry a lead via WhatsApp after an AI call fails?
kb_faqs.answer: NBA (Next Best Action) via WhatsApp should trigger after: (1) AI call completes (goal marked completed, demo booked, or explicit not interested), or (2) AI call fails due to no connection after max retries. However, there is a known bug: NBA for WhatsApp does NOT trigger automatically after happy-path AI call completion. Workaround: manually add WhatsApp campaign and stagger timing, or use human agent for WhatsApp follow-up. This issue impacts lead coverage significantly—66 of 100 leads in one case did not receive WhatsApp due to this NBA gap.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What is the ideal daily AI call limit per lead to maximize coverage?
kb_faqs.answer: Daily call limits should be set high enough to enable multiple retry attempts per lead (2–3 attempts/day minimum) while respecting DND regulations. If daily limits are low and the bot fails to connect on the first attempt, the bot may not retry via AI call and instead skip to WhatsApp. However, if NBA WhatsApp triggering is buggy, leads get abandoned. Recommendation: set daily AI call attempts to 2–3 per lead, and ensure NBA rules are correctly configured to fallback to WhatsApp if AI calls exhaust.

kb_faqs.question: What types of WhatsApp templates should we use—utility or marketing?
kb_faqs.answer: Use Utility templates for post-call follow-ups (low rejection rates, high deliverability) and Marketing templates for proactive outreach (higher rejection risk but better for engagement). Utility templates are ideal for confirmations, reminders, and handoff messages. Marketing templates work for promotional content but may face higher spam/unsubscribe rates. Always start with Utility templates in the NBA flow to maximize reach. Payment/WABA setup issues can prevent either template type from delivering, so validate payment method upfront.

kb_faqs.question: How do we improve WhatsApp delivery rates?
kb_faqs.answer: WhatsApp delivery rates are affected by: (1) WABA account payment method validity, (2) Template type (utility > marketing), (3) Daily rate limits and engagement window settings, (4) Recipient opt-in status. To improve: (1) Verify payment method is active and valid, (2) Use utility templates for first touch, (3) Ensure campaign engagement days haven't expired, (4) Respect daily channel limits, (5) Confirm leads are opted-in if applicable. In one case, configuring to respond post-completion and checking daily limits resolved 90% of missing message issues.

kb_faqs.question: What is the expected turnaround time for human agent follow-up after the bot hands off a lead?
kb_faqs.answer: Sales cycles vary by product: education leads typically convert within 1.2–2 days, loan products within 3–7 days. Human agents should contact qualified leads within 4–24 hours of bot handoff to maximize conversion. If no conversion is seen after 2+ days despite human follow-up, investigate: (1) Is the bot actually qualifying leads (not just passing through), (2) Is lead quality poor, (3) Is the human process broken? Always baseline human agent performance on the same dataset before running bot pilot to set realistic expectations.

kb_faqs.question: What should the UAT sample size be, and what should we test?
kb_faqs.answer: Minimum UAT: 100 test leads. Test: (1) Bot conversation flow and objection handling, (2) Phone connection rate and call quality, (3) WhatsApp integration and message delivery, (4) Agent account and dashboard access, (5) Dialler integration (if applicable), (6) Lead data ingestion and metadata accuracy, (7) Handoff to human agents, (8) System stability under load. Results should include connectivity %, conversation completion %, and sample conversation audits. Only proceed to live if connectivity >15% and bot conversation quality is acceptable. If UAT reveals issues (e.g., 65% DND, poor dialler setup), negotiate scope or data changes before live launch.

kb_faqs.question: What delays commonly occur during UAT, and how do we prevent them?
kb_faqs.answer: Common UAT delays: (1) Agent account access not properly set up on client side, (2) Dialler integration not initiated early, (3) WhatsApp WABA payment method issues, (4) Lead data format or freshness issues, (5) Client team unresponsiveness or resource constraints. Prevention: (1) Start UAT setup 2 weeks before planned live date, (2) Have a dedicated SPOC from client for account access, (3) Verify all integrations (dialler, WhatsApp, CRM) are working before UAT starts, (4) Set clear UAT timelines and escalation paths, (5) Have backup leads ready if initial batch fails validation. Halt UAT if critical blockers aren't resolved within 3 days.

kb_faqs.question: Should we build a separate reminder bot for follow-up calls (e.g., day-before and same-day reminders)?
kb_faqs.answer: No. Building separate reminder bots requires: (1) duplicate bot flows, (2) manual lead-shuffling between campaigns, (3) constant refinement across two systems, (4) extended pilot timelines. Instead: focus on delivering strong results on the primary use case (booking). If reminders are critical, suggest: (1) using human agents for reminders (maintains data continuity), (2) building reminder logic post-pilot, or (3) deferring reminders to a second phase after primary pilot metrics are validated. One pilot that pushed for dual-bot approach saw extended timelines with minimal incremental benefit.

kb_faqs.question: How do we handle requests for custom widget positioning or sizing?
kb_faqs.answer: Custom positioning requests often indicate the default widget blocks important content. Immediate workaround: ask client to provide specific dimensions (width, height, position: top/bottom, left/center/right) and Convin can implement from backend without product development. Create tickets for non-standard positioning if needed. For size issues, suggest icon-only variants (e.g., small call icon instead of full bar) and create feature tickets. Timeline: backend tweaks 1–2 days, new variants 3–5 days. Proceed with current widget size during UAT; implement size changes post-UAT if approved.

kb_faqs.question: Can we pass customer phone numbers to the human agent via UUI headers?
kb_faqs.answer: Passing customer phone numbers via UUI headers is possible with some dialers (e.g., Vobiz supports passing caller ID in UUI header to agent's dialler screen) but faces regulatory pushbacks with others due to data privacy regulations. Workaround: (1) Ask dialler (Vobiz, Exotel, Elision) if UUI header passing is supported and compliant, (2) If not, explore alternative ways to surface lead context on agent screen (e.g., integration with CRM that auto-pulls lead data when call arrives), (3) If no compliance path exists, ensure lead information is pre-populated in agent's CRM/dashboard before call transfer. Always check with legal/compliance before attempting customer data passing.

kb_faqs.question: How do we handle context passing for non-NBA flows?
kb_faqs.answer: Context passing (e.g., bot conversation history, qualification answers) currently only works reliably in NBA flows. In non-NBA flows, context passing is not guaranteed. Workaround: (1) Ensure all primary use cases are built with NBA in mind to enable context flow, (2) For non-NBA flows, pre-populate CRM or agent dashboard with lead data and bot call transcripts, (3) Avoid complex sequential flows without NBA; instead, structure as separate campaigns with handoff between them. Document this limitation in technical design so PMs don't assume context will pass universally.

kb_faqs.question: What cost savings should we highlight when comparing bot vs. human agent performance?
kb_faqs.answer: In pilots where bot and human performance are comparable (e.g., webinar booking and attendance rates aligned), highlight: (1) Cost per lead contact: bot typically $0.50–1.50 vs. human $5–10, (2) Volume scalability: bot can handle 10k+ leads, humans limited to 100–200 leads/day, (3) Operational savings: fewer demos to attend for sales team (e.g., bot filters unqualified leads), (4) 24/7 availability without shift management. Quantify hours saved (e.g., 'sales team focuses on qualified prospects only, reducing demo prep time by 30%'). One client saved significant demo prep costs by filtering out 'not interested' leads before human contact.

kb_faqs.question: What is a typical CPA (Cost Per Acquisition) comparison between bot and human agents?
kb_faqs.answer: CPA depends on product: loans typically 1–2% conversion (very high CPA), education courses 5–15% conversion (lower CPA). Bot CPA: $50–200 per acquisition (based on $0.50–1 per dial, 10–20% qualification rate, 5–15% final conversion). Human CPA: $200–500+ (higher cost per outreach, time overhead). Pilots show bot CPA is 30–50% lower than human when conversion rates are similar. Always document baseline human metrics to prove this delta. In absence of human benchmark, position bot as 'testing a new channel at lower risk before committing full human team.'

kb_faqs.question: What happens when a client's dialler doesn't have DND override capabilities?
kb_faqs.answer: If the client's dialler (e.g., Tata, Cube, other proprietary systems) lacks DND override, it severely limits connectivity on DND-heavy datasets. Solutions: (1) Ask client to request DND override feature from their dialler vendor, (2) Switch to Convin-managed dialler (Exotel, Elision, Vobiz) which has DND override built-in—this requires integration effort and DLT certificate but ensures full functionality, (3) Use WhatsApp as primary channel instead of voice calls (works on DND-compliant numbers), (4) Provide non-DND lead segments only. Document the limitation and negotiate success metrics accordingly (e.g., exclude DND from denominator, or request larger lead volumes to compensate).

kb_faqs.question: When should we use Convin-managed diallers vs. client-owned diallers?
kb_faqs.answer: Use Convin-managed dialler (Exotel, Elision, Vobiz): (1) When client lacks in-house dialler or doesn't want integration overhead, (2) For faster time-to-market (no client-side integration delays), (3) When DND override or advanced features are needed. Challenges: additional cost (negotiated rates), Convin owns dialler setup complexity, client may resist paying for parallel dialling infrastructure. Use client-owned dialler: (1) When client has existing dialler they prefer (e.g., Cube, Tata), (2) To avoid infosec or procurement pushback, (3) When client wants single dialler for human and AI teams. Challenges: slower integration timelines, client may lack desired features (DND override, call recording), integration bugs shift to client's vendor. Always propose Convin-managed dialler first for new deals to reduce timelines and complexity—position as 'negotiated rates + Convin support.'

kb_faqs.question: What compliance or regulatory requirements must we handle for 1400/1600 series calling?
kb_faqs.answer: 1400/1600 series (short codes for telemarketing) require: (1) DLT (Distributed Ledger Technology) certificate from your telecom provider, (2) Registration with TRAI (Telecom Regulatory Authority of India) as an enterprise, (3) Approval from the dialler provider. Onboarding 1400/1600 series adds 2–4 weeks to timeline and additional cost. Workaround: start pilot on regular 76/79/80 series numbers (no DLT needed), demonstrate results, then migrate to 1400/1600 for scaling. If client insists on 1400/1600 from day 1, initiate integration early (week 1) to avoid delaying UAT. Always clarify at kick-off: Will you call on 1400/1600 or regular numbers? Default to regular numbers, then explain 1400/1600 requires regulatory preparation.

kb_faqs.question: How should we configure STT (Speech-To-Text) for better transcription accuracy?
kb_faqs.answer: Use Nova 3 STT model (available in Convin Sense) for superior transcription accuracy vs. baseline models. Nova 3 is particularly effective for: (1) sales calls with industry jargon, (2) non-native English speakers, (3) low-quality audio or background noise. Configuration: (1) In agent settings, select 'Nova 3' as STT model, (2) Test in UAT with sample calls before going live, (3) Monitor transcription quality post-launch. One client (Shaadi use case) implemented Nova 3 and saw significantly improved accuracy on wedding-related terminology. Cost: check if Nova 3 incurs additional per-call charges vs. default STT.

kb_faqs.question: Why do outbound calls sometimes originate from unexpected fallback numbers even if we've configured specific numbers?
kb_faqs.answer: Diallers like Exotel use random fallback numbers when the primary network operator is down or congested, to ensure call continuity. This creates a problem: if you configure specific numbers for clients to whitelist and bypass DND, but calls still originate from random fallback numbers, whitelisting is ineffective. Workaround: (1) Test on WhatsApp calls instead of voice calls for DND-affected numbers (requires inbound initiation, ~2 mins effort from user), (2) Ask dialler provider if they can disable fallback behavior or provide a list of possible fallback numbers, (3) For demos with DND-protected decision makers, use WhatsApp calls as primary channel. WhatsApp call quality is currently lower than voice, so use only as workaround.

kb_faqs.question: How do we handle pilots where the bot performance is already at parity with human agents?
kb_faqs.answer: If the bot has achieved parity or better than human performance on webinar booking and attendance metrics (e.g., 30% attendance rate = human baseline), stop expanding the pilot and move to contract closure. Continuing to run more campaigns shows diminishing ROI and extends timelines unnecessarily. Instead: (1) prepare a final business case with cost savings and parity metrics, (2) propose moving to annual contract with pay-as-you-go or committed volume, (3) defer additional use cases (e.g., reminder bots, secondary campaigns) to post-contract phase. One client (Bhanzu) achieved parity on 2.4k fresh leads after 70 days; management decision was to conclude pilot and move to annual contract.

kb_faqs.question: What should we do when a pilot shows zero conversions despite human follow-up?
kb_faqs.answer: Zero conversions after 600+ leads and human follow-up (after 2+ days) indicates a fundamental issue. Diagnose: (1) Is the bot actually qualifying leads (audio review of 10–20 calls), (2) Is qualification definition aligned with human benchmark (e.g., 'interested in learning more' vs. 'ready to buy'), (3) Is lead quality poor, (4) Is human follow-up process broken, (5) Has the sales cycle baseline been validated (e.g., truly 1.2–2 days)? Pause the pilot and request: (1) Bot call audits to verify qualification quality, (2) Revised definition of 'qualified lead', (3) Human agent conversion data on same leads, (4) Possibly different lead source if current source has structural issues. Don't continue without fixing the root cause—it will waste both time and credits.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: How do we validate that a lead CSV is suitable before running a campaign?
kb_faqs.answer: Pre-campaign validation steps: (1) Sample 10 leads, call them manually to confirm they are real and reachable, (2) Check data format consistency (phone numbers, metadata fields), (3) Ask client: 'When were these leads generated?' (must be <2 weeks old), (4) Confirm DND percentage by checking with dialler, (5) Ask client 'How many times have you already contacted these leads?' (recycled leads = lower conversion), (6) Request client's baseline human conversion rate on same dataset for comparison. If any validation step fails (e.g., leads are 2+ months old, >50% DND, already contacted 5+ times), ask for fresh data or adjust success metrics and lead volume accordingly.

kb_faqs.question: What percentage of booked demos typically get attended?
kb_faqs.answer: Typical demo attendance rate: 30–50% of booked demos are attended. One client saw ~50% attendance on webinar-booked leads, meaning if the bot books 100 demos, ~50 will be attended. This is a key metric for ROI—higher attendance = higher productivity for sales team. If attendance drops below 25%, investigate: (1) Are demos being booked too far in future (>7 days)? (2) Is bot not capturing intent properly? (3) Is reminder system working (or missing)? Use this metric to show sales team productivity gains (fewer no-shows if bot qualifies better than blast email).

kb_faqs.question: How do we know if qualification metrics should be set differently?
kb_faqs.answer: Alignment on qualification metrics is critical. Clarify upfront: (1) Does 'qualified' mean bot-marked interested (lightweight), or human agent confirmed intent (heavyweight)?, (2) What's the human baseline qualification %?, (3) Are we chasing first-level interest or purchase intent? If bot is qualifying at 5–10% but human baseline is also 5–10%, the metrics are aligned—don't expect higher bot rates. If clients claim higher baseline than what bot achieves, ask: (1) Can we audit a sample of 20 leads the human team called—how many qualified, (2) How did human define 'qualified'? One client claimed 40–55% conversion but actual was <2%—large discrepancy meant qualification definition was misaligned.

kb_faqs.question: What should we do when a client wants to run pay-as-you-go campaigns post-pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: If the client has a prepaid wallet (e.g., $4,000 balance), and they want to run additional campaigns: (1) recharge the wallet with their remaining balance, (2) enable them to run campaigns independently (train them on campaign setup), (3) no need for Convin to own the campaign execution—they can do it themselves using the same bot/agent. This is appropriate for mature clients who have completed UAT and understand the platform. Document the pricing per lead/minute dialed, and ensure the wallet has sufficient balance. One client (Boat) wanted to run 2 additional campaigns on 2,000 leads each; solution was to reload wallet and let them self-serve using pay-as-you-go model.

kb_faqs.question: How does context passing work in NBA flows vs. non-NBA flows?
kb_faqs.answer: Context passing (previous bot interaction history, qualification answers) works reliably only in NBA (Next Best Action) flows where one campaign explicitly transfers to another. In non-NBA flows, context does not automatically pass to downstream systems/humans. If a client requires context for human handoff: (1) design campaign as NBA flow (bot → WhatsApp → human or bot → human call), (2) pre-populate CRM or agent dashboard with bot call summaries and transcripts, (3) avoid non-NBA sequential flows. For Sense pilots, always design with NBA in mind to ensure context flows end-to-end.

kb_faqs.question: How long does a typical product bug fix take from identification to production?
kb_faqs.answer: Bug fix timelines vary by severity: (1) Critical bugs (platform down, data loss): 2–4 hours identification to prod release, (2) High-priority (blocking customer campaign): 4–8 hours, (3) Medium (impacting specific feature): 1–2 days. For example, the sessionID payload issue (AC-1661) took ~3–4 hours from identification to production release. Always escalate bugs via Jira tickets and get ETA from product team. For UAT-blocking bugs, escalate to leadership and request expedited fix or workaround.

kb_faqs.question: What is the impact of high DND concentration on pilot success?
kb_faqs.answer: High DND (>50%) severely limits viability: In one case, 65% DND led to only 13% actual connections out of 100 UAT leads, providing almost no data to assess bot quality. Impact: (1) insufficient sample size for meaningful bot evaluation, (2) pilot timelines extend as more leads are needed, (3) ROI becomes difficult to prove. Solutions: (1) Ask for non-DND lead segments, (2) Request dialler DND override capability, (3) Renegotiate success metrics to exclude DND from denominator, (4) Request 3–5x larger lead volume to compensate. Always check DND % early in UAT; if >50%, escalate to sales immediately to renegotiate scope.

kb_faqs.question: What is the standard workflow for transitioning a pilot account to a new PM or team member?
kb_faqs.answer: Account transition steps: (1) Outgoing PM schedules knowledge transfer (KT) call with incoming PM and relevant teams, (2) Outgoing PM shares: account overview, client SPOC contacts, current campaign status, known issues, upcoming milestones, (3) Incoming PM shadows 1–2 client calls before taking over, (4) Outgoing PM remains available for 1 week for clarifications. In one case, PM Priyal transitioned the Allen account to Arnab with formal KT; Arnab was also assigned to shadow Bhargava on Shaadi pilot to understand pilot workflows end-to-end. Always document transitions so no client context is lost.

kb_faqs.question: How should we handle UAT delays caused by client-side resource constraints?
kb_faqs.answer: If client can't provide agent access, lead data, or responses within 3 days: (1) escalate to sales and client leadership, (2) set a hard go/no-go deadline (e.g., 'If we don't have agent setup by March 15, we will delay UAT'), (3) offer to halt work temporarily if client requests postponement, (4) don't keep the internal team in limbo. One client (Metropolis) had UAT delayed due to agent account access issues on their end—after 2 weeks, decision was made to halt POC until they sorted internal access. Clear escalation and deadlines prevent indefinite delays.

kb_faqs.question: How do we handle scope creep when clients ask for additional use cases mid-pilot?
kb_faqs.answer: When a client requests new use cases mid-pilot (e.g., reminder bots, secondary campaigns): (1) ask the sales owner if this was in the original contract or should be treated as expansion, (2) if expansion, negotiate scope and timeline—don't take on extra work without extending deadlines, (3) if original scope, clarify priority: are we wrapping current use case or continuing with additions? One client (Nirvasa Health) asked to add a second use case mid-pilot—after discussion, decision was to stick to primary use case, finish it well, then propose secondary use case as post-contract work. Prevent scope creep by documenting agreed use cases in writing at kick-off.

kb_faqs.question: What documentation is critical to capture during the client kick-off call?
kb_faqs.answer: Essential kick-off documentation (email to client after call): (1) Agreed use case, success metrics, and conversion baseline (human benchmark if available), (2) Lead volume, expected freshness, and DND assumptions, (3) Timeline: UAT dates, go-live date, pilot end date, (4) Dialler and integration details, (5) Handoff SLA and human agent training needs, (6) Any caveats or data quality assumptions. Send this summary email same day and ask client to confirm—this prevents metric misalignment later. One client initially claimed 40% conversion but later admitted it was 50–55%; documentation of baseline numbers in writing would have caught this early.

kb_faqs.question: What should we do when a dialler doesn't support a required feature like caller ID passing?
kb_faqs.answer: If a dialler (e.g., Vobiz, Exotel, Elision) doesn't support a feature like customer number passing in UUI headers: (1) ask the dialler vendor if they can add it or provide a workaround, (2) check if Convin product can surface the data via an alternative method (e.g., CRM integration, dashboard pre-population), (3) if no path exists, document the limitation and propose a workaround (e.g., 'Agent will see lead info in CRM screen before call is transferred'), (4) escalate to product/integration team to see if we can build a bridge. Don't promise features the dialler can't support—clarify limitations upfront.

kb_faqs.question: What does it mean when the bot qualifies leads but human follow-up yields zero conversions?
kb_faqs.answer: This indicates: (1) Bot qualification definition is too loose (e.g., 'interested in learning more' vs. actual purchase intent), (2) Leads are valid but not sales-ready (more nurturing needed before human handoff), (3) Human follow-up process is broken (not calling at right time, using wrong pitch), (4) Lead freshness issue (leads stale by time humans call them). Diagnosis: Audit bot calls to see what questions were asked and what responses qualified a lead. Compare to human agent's assessment of same leads. If humans say 'This lead was cold, not interested,' then bot qualification is misaligned. Realign qualification definition with human benchmark before continuing.

kb_faqs.question: What is the typical timeline for initiating a UAT campaign after client setup?
kb_faqs.answer: Teams aim to trigger UAT campaigns within 48 hours of completing preliminary setup tasks like card additions and integrations. However, actual timelines depend on client-side dependencies such as approvals and availability. It's important to confirm all prerequisites (CSV format, wallet top-up, system access) are in place before committing to specific go-live dates.

kb_faqs.question: What was the issue with WABA card integrations and how was it resolved?
kb_faqs.answer: Multiple clients experienced WABA card integration failures despite trying 5+ different payment cards. The root cause varied by client—one client resolved it by switching to an ICICI credit card while another used a different card entirely. The exact triggering factor isn't fully documented; teams recommend trying alternative payment methods and escalating to the client's payment provider if issues persist. No systematic fix was identified across all cases.

kb_faqs.question: What are typical DND (Do Not Disturb) rates in third-party lead databases?
kb_faqs.answer: Clients typically see 60-75% DND rates in third-party purchased lead databases. This significantly impacts usable volume—for example, a 50K monthly lead database with 75% DND yields only ~12.5K dialable leads. Teams recommend filtering leads to exclude DND numbers at the dialer integration level and planning campaign volume accordingly to account for this constraint.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: Lead Data & CSV Quality
kb_faqs.question: What is the proper process for bulk uploading leads after the variable management release?
kb_faqs.answer: With the new variable management system, teams must: (1) download fresh CSV templates from the system, not use older templates; (2) ensure all variables used in KBs are created in Settings > Variable Management with consistent naming (e.g., avoid mixing {Product_1} and {product_1}); (3) mark variables as Mandatory if required for every lead; (4) keep campaigns in Active state when uploading leads (paused campaigns block uploads). Uploading with paused campaigns was the old process but no longer works.

kb_faqs.question: Why are leads unable to be uploaded to paused campaigns after the recent product release?
kb_faqs.answer: The new variable management release introduced a requirement that campaigns must be in Active state to accept lead uploads; previously, leads could be uploaded to paused campaigns for quality checks before going live. This change forces teams to either resume campaigns before upload or fix errors after resuming. Teams should communicate this change to clients and adjust their pre-campaign validation workflows accordingly.

kb_faqs.question: What prompt modifications improve naturalness in Hinglish conversations?
kb_faqs.answer: Teams have documented a prompt variation built for Hinglish conversations that adds more natural speech patterns. This prompt is agnostic to use cases and can be modified for specific client needs. Teams recommend adding this to ongoing bots in Pillar 4 (conversation design phase) and customizing it based on vertical and client requirements. The prompt should be stored in the Agent Training section for consistency.

kb_faqs.question: How should KB updates be synchronized with the Agent Training section?
kb_faqs.answer: When updating the Knowledge Base, corresponding changes should be reflected in the Agent Training section. A hard refresh should sync these automatically; if not, it indicates a bug that should be reported to the product team. Updates to KB variables or content may not auto-propagate, so CSMs should verify both sections are aligned before campaign launch.

kb_faqs.question: What causes call duration to show as 0 seconds in reports when the call actually lasted longer?
kb_faqs.answer: This is a known reporting bug where very short calls (e.g., 4 seconds) may be recorded as 0 seconds in reports while still being charged for 1 pulse (minimum billing unit). Root cause is unclear but affects billing accuracy. Teams should audit reports against call recordings when clients report duration discrepancies and escalate to the tech team for investigation and correction.

kb_faqs.question: What is the issue with Total Spend not equaling AI Call Cost when only AI calls are active?
kb_faqs.answer: This is a billing/reporting bug where Total Spend shows a different amount than the sum of AI Call Costs, even when AI calls are the only active channel. Teams should report detailed examples to the tech team including org name, date range, and call counts. This requires investigation into billing logic and charge categorization.

kb_faqs.category: Client Learnings: NBA Loop & Retry Logic
kb_faqs.question: What are the main issues with NON-NBA (non-NBA loop) calling logic?
kb_faqs.answer: Three key bugs were identified: (1) NON-NBA disabling mechanism not working (calls continue when they should stop); (2) NON-NBA calls being triggered during DND hours when they shouldn't be; (3) Call queue accumulating with NON-NBA calls due to the above bugs. These require fixes at the logic level to ensure NON-NBA respects both the disable flag and DND hour rules. These were live campaign issues requiring immediate resolution.

kb_faqs.question: What conversion benchmarks can be expected from bot-qualified leads vs. human agent performance?
kb_faqs.answer: Clients have reported human agent CTR (click-through/conversion rate) around 30-40% on leads. Bot performance should be compared against this benchmark using data from the same time period and lead set. Teams should request human performance data from campaign day 1 to ensure accurate comparison, as delayed data collection makes A/B analysis difficult. Missing benchmark data is a common issue delaying performance assessment.

kb_faqs.question: What payment outcomes have been achieved in early-stage pilots?
kb_faqs.answer: In one pilot with 550 leads run across campaigns, 9 payments were received, representing a 1.6% conversion rate. This is from a real estate/financial services vertical. Payment metrics should be tracked separately from qualification metrics as they represent actual revenue. Lead freshness and quality significantly impact payment outcomes, so newer lead batches typically show better performance than aged, retargeted leads.

kb_faqs.question: How should pilot campaigns be structured to evaluate bot performance independently?
kb_faqs.answer: Teams should run parallel campaigns: one with human agent support (to measure team capacity) and one bot-only (to isolate bot performance). This A/B structure helps clients understand if human bandwidth or bot quality is the limiting factor. Typically, 225-450 leads are allocated per track. Fresh lead batches should be used for each test to avoid recycled lead fatigue and ensure valid comparisons.

kb_faqs.question: What do clients need to understand about human agent scaling limitations?
kb_faqs.answer: Many clients have 2-3 human agents handling 50K+ monthly leads, creating severe bandwidth bottlenecks. With this ratio, processing intent-qualified leads would take a month or more. Teams should help clients understand that bot-only automation is essential for scale and that human agents are better deployed for handling high-intent leads or complex objections rather than initial outreach. This conversation is critical before pilots to set realistic expectations.

kb_faqs.question: What objection do clients raise about DND compliance and how should it be addressed?
kb_faqs.answer: Clients are concerned that calling DND numbers will result in regulatory fines or bad PR. The rebuttal is that third-party purchased databases typically don't include DND numbers, and dialers can be configured to skip DND leads entirely. If a client has a high DND rate in their own database, teams should suggest filtering at source or using the dialer's built-in DND suppression. Frame it as a data quality issue, not a bot issue.

kb_faqs.question: What are the pre-campaign checklist items before going live?
kb_faqs.answer: Key checklist items include: (1) CSV lead file validated and formatted correctly; (2) All variables created in Settings and mapped in campaign; (3) WABA integration tested and card details confirmed; (4) Bot prompts finalized in Agent Training section; (5) UAT campaign run and metrics reviewed; (6) Wallet top-up confirmed; (7) Client approvals obtained for any third-party integrations; (8) Human agent handoff process defined if hybrid mode. Missing any item typically causes 2-3 day delays.

kb_faqs.question: What is the expected turnaround time (TAT) for lead handoff to human agents and what happens if breached?
kb_faqs.answer: No specific TAT is documented in the conversations, but context suggests same-day or next-day handoff is expected for qualified leads. When handoffs are delayed, clients become frustrated and question the value of bot qualification. Integration delays (WABA, CRM sync) often cause handoff delays. Teams should establish and communicate explicit SLAs (e.g., 30 minutes for hot leads, 24 hours for warm leads) and monitor compliance.

kb_faqs.question: What widget placement issue did clients raise and what was the recommended workaround?
kb_faqs.answer: One client requested the bot widget to be repositioned from the default location to the right-center of the page. The Convin product team did not prioritize this feature (estimated 1+ week of work). The workaround was to clarify that go-live should not be dependent on aesthetic changes and suggest proceeding with current placement. This is a low-priority feature that can be addressed in future sprints post-launch.

kb_faqs.question: What are the Vapi account credit limits and how can they be extended?
kb_faqs.answer: Vapi demo accounts have limited credits and require reaching 1000 unique leads in a 7-day rolling period to extend limits. If this threshold isn't met within 7 days, credits run out and campaigns pause. Teams must either wait for the 7-day window to reset or use alternative WhatsApp providers. Tracking lead volume daily helps teams understand if they'll hit the threshold in time to avoid campaign interruptions.

kb_faqs.question: What integration approach does Convin recommend for WhatsApp Business Accounts (WABA)?
kb_faqs.answer: Convin recommends finding the admin of the client's parent organization and creating a new WABA within that structure, then integrating with that WABA directly. This is the standard approach across all clients. Convin does not recommend using third-party platforms like Gupshup or integrating with existing WABAs that may have complex dependencies. This approach ensures clean data flow and avoids third-party markup.

kb_faqs.question: What causes delays in WABA integration and how long should it take?
kb_faqs.answer: WABA integration delays are typically caused by: (1) client contact unavailability; (2) long approval chains within client organizations; (3) incorrect payment card details. Expected timeline is 2-3 days for a smooth integration. When delays exceed 3 days, teams should escalate to the client contact and request a synchronous call to resolve blockers. Payment card issues are the most common culprit and should be the first thing to verify.

kb_faqs.question: What is the process for retrying leads that failed WhatsApp delivery?
kb_faqs.answer: Teams typically rerun failed leads through the WhatsApp channel after initial attempts. However, doing this across multiple retry cycles can skew results and exhaust lead freshness.
